8. Two doves flew out, flew to the bell tower. They mournfully cooed as if ringing a bell.  
9. When the children heard this, they immediately ran to the cemetery: Our mother, dear mother, to whom have you entrusted us?  
10. To whom, my children, to no one but the Lord God Himself. [...]  
11. And you others, who are rude, listen to one another. And may the youngest boy be provided for by the Lord God Himself!

label: BALADY
translation: 1. On the shore lay a little white shirt, surely you didn’t go swimming since it was Sunday? 2. I didn’t go swimming, I was fording horses and my little horse threw me into the water.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. On the little hill, there on the dam, live two blacksmiths, on the little hill, there on the dam, live two blacksmiths.  
2. One of them has a beautiful daughter, named Filomena.  
3. Filomena, a beautiful maiden, is not easily deceived.  
4. There was a Tatar king, he was a consummate deceiver.  
5. He deceived seventy maidens, but he could not deceive Filomena.  
6. He had an old woman with him, who gave him advice.  
7. Old woman, old woman, give me advice on how I might deceive Filomena.  
8. Have a laced bodice and skirts made in the Moravian style.  
9. Knock on the little window: Open for me, Filomena!  
10. Who is knocking on my gate, disturbing my silver and gold?  
11. I am a maiden from Moravia, carrying spices to Prague.  
12. Filomena, prepare the dinner so that the maiden may eat.  
13. Filomena, go and make the bed, the maiden will sleep with you.  
14. I will not sleep with that maiden, she has a man's appearance.  
15. I have never seen a maiden with a beard.  
16. I am a maiden from Moravia, and there maidens have beards.  
17. When it was about midnight, the maiden turned to the other maiden.  
18. Early the next morning, a note was written on the door:  
19. Filomena, a beautiful maiden, was deceived during the night.  
20. All the maidens in their wreaths, Filomena in her headscarf.  
21. All the maidens go for water, Filomena goes into labor.  
22. All the maidens chop wood, Filomena celebrates the christening.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. The child was orphaned, the child was orphaned at a year and a half, at a year and a half.  
2. When it began to understand, it asked about its mother.  
3. Oh father, dear father, where did you put mother?  
4. Your mother sleeps soundly not far from the door.  
5. When the child heard this, it ran to the cemetery.  
6. It dug with a pin, scratched with its little finger.  
7. When it had dug through, it wept sadly:  
8. Oh mother, dear mother, say a word.  
9. Oh child, go home, you have another mother there.  
10. She is not like the one you had.  
11. When she cuts bread, she throws a knife at me.  
12. When you cut it, you spread butter.  
13. When she combs my little head, blood flows like a stream.  
14. When you combed it, you caressed it.  
15. When she washes my little feet, she hits me into the basin.  
16. When you washed me, you always kissed me.  
17. When she washes my shirt, she almost curses me.  
18. When you washed it, you used to sing.  
19. Go, child, go home, I will come for you.  
20. The child came home, laid down its head:  
21. Oh father, dear father, prepare the little coffin.  
22. Mother is coming to me, she will take me.  
23. The first day it was sick, the first day it was sick, the second day it died, the third day it had a funeral.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. My lady, you have a beautiful daughter, but if she is not at home, that is bad, but if she is not at home, there.  
2. If you had her at home, handsome boys would come to you.  
3. Our daughter is nowhere at home, she is weeding wheat with darnel.  
4. When she weeded two measures of wheat, she saw three squadrons of hussars.  
5. I warmly welcome you, gentlemen hussars, have you seen my delight?  
6. We have seen him, but not alive, his body lies in the middle of the sea.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. Madam, where is your daughter? I came to visit her. Three years have passed, we have not seen each other, I came to comfort her.  
2. Our daughter lies in the cemetery, there is her little bed. Surely you did not think, my dear Pepíček, that she would be your wife?  
3. Show me the way to the cemetery, I want to look at her; if there is still a blue-eyed girl there, I will call to her.  
4. I took the first step to the cemetery, I saw a new grave there, three red roses gave me the sign that there lies my heart.  
5. Rise, my dear, from the deep grave, speak a word to me. If I could speak, I could walk too; my little heart is withered.  
6. Sleep sweetly here, my dearest, I am now leaving from here, my hair is gray and all fallen; I will never see you again.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. The shepherd is grazing sheep on the green meadow, hablum pablum hábilitum dytum tytum pábilitum, on the green meadow.  
2. A butcher came to him, accompanied by a spotted dog.  
3. Shepherd, shepherd, sell four sheep!  
4. The shepherd sold the sheep, lost the money with him.  
5. The shepherd is driven home, the master will take care of it.  
6. Shepherd, shepherd, where are the four sheep?  
7. Four wolves came, each stole a sheep from the shepherd.  
8. You had a stick, why didn’t you fight them? You had a dog, why didn’t you set it on them?  
9. I forgot about the dog, I didn’t remember the stick.  
10. Up by Kobylisy, there the shepherd is already hanging.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. I served in the yard, in the yard, and that in the town of Tábor, Tábor, and that in the town of Tábor.  
2. I courted a maiden, blue-eyed Mařenka.  
3. There was a military draft announced by the Emperor, by the lord.  
4. I fought for seven years, leaving my beloved at home.  
5. I am riding home on a horse, my saber jingling.  
6. Mother came out on the doorstep. Give lodging to the soldier!  
7. I did not dismount from the horse; I waited for Mařenka.  
8. However, Mařenka did not come out; the younger sister came out.  
9. Where is my Mařenka, that she does not welcome me home?  
10. Your Mařenka sleeps in the grave; no one will wake her anymore.  
11. I immediately turned the horse around and returned to the cemetery.  
12. Who is walking around the grave? I am, Mařenka, coming to you.  
13. I did not find you at home; I went to the cemetery to you.  
14. Return, my dear, return back; you have the world full of girls.  
15. Choose one among them, your one true wife.  
16. I do not want another maiden if I do not have my Mařenka.  
17. The bell rang in the tower; the beloved belongs to the dear.  
18. The horse broke into a run, galloping into the village.  
19. People run to the cemetery; the soldier embraces the grave there.  
[The singer learned the song in Galicia and also knows how to sing it in a distorted Polish.]

label: BALADY
translation:
1. My mother took care of me, just like my little bed is. My bed made ready, prepared for my body, prepared for my body.  
2. In the Libeň cemetery, there will be my bed, my bed close to the door, where it is already soft to lie.  
3. When they laid me in the grave, the musicians played sadly. If they played for a hundred hours, I would not wake up anymore.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. Water flows, it flows from the stream to the river, said the little dragoon to the blue-eyed girl.  
2. Oh mother, my mother, give me advice, I have promised myself to the dragoon, I am going with him now.  
3. Oh daughter, my daughter, leave the dragoon, the dragoons will leave tomorrow, you will remain alone.  
4. Oh mother, my mother, that advice is hard, I already have my clothes packed, I will go with the dragoons.  
5. When a year had passed, the daughter came to her mother, carrying a little dragoon in a striped blanket.  
6. Oh mother, my mother, help me provide for him, the little dragoon will go for water for you.  
7. Oh daughter, my daughter, your advice is hard, you did not want to listen, so support him yourself.  
8. Oh father, my father, help me provide for him, the little dragoon will call you grandpa.  
9. Oh daughter, my daughter, your advice is hard, you did not want to listen, so support him yourself.  
10. I am as abandoned as that pear tree in the field, when its leaves fall off, how sadly it stands.  

Note: dragoon = a member of mounted infantry; štráfový (from German) = striped.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. In the black forest in a clearing, in the black forest in a clearing, my dear is cutting grass there, my dear is cutting grass there.  
2. She saw a young man there, a green huntsman.  
3. Who told you to cut the grass? Now you must perish here!  
4. Rather than perish here, I would call my brother.  
5. She called only once, her brother heard under the gamekeeper’s enclosure.  
6. It’s bad, it’s bad, it’s bad for us, dear sister is calling in the grove.  
7. When they ran to the clearing, they caught sight of the huntsman.  
8. What have you been doing, huntsman, that you have a gun stained with blood?  
9. I shot a dove in the bailiff’s little window.  
10. She hooted day and night, she wouldn’t let me, a sad one, sleep.  
11. That was not a dove, that was our sister,  
12. who used to go to the forest to cut the grass.  
13. Four lords judge who killed the girl in the grove.  
14. They carry the sister on a stretcher and the huntsman in chains.  
15. They buried the sister in the grave and hanged the huntsman.  

šafář = the manager responsible for the farm staff on an agricultural estate

label: BALADY
translation:
1. It was a beautiful summer day and around the roses bloomed, I often remember the youth of my age.  
2. Above me, clouds like white swans majestically spread their white wings.  
3. Fly away, clouds, fly to those mountains, greet my beloved and our homeland.  
4. I like girls, I like boys even more, and my beloved, I like him the most.  
5. What is it, my beloved, what have you done, that you let yourself be taken to the army so young?  
6. I did it for my Boženka, because she did not come for me when they played volleyball.  
7. I did it for my parents, because they had a hardened heart towards me.  
8. The corporal says to them: Fall in line, aim your rifles, the enemy is here.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. Early in the morning the maiden got up, tied the knot of her dress. I will go, mother, to the lake, there I will wash my little dress.  
2. Don’t go, don’t go, to the lake, stay at home, my daughter, I had a bad dream last night, don’t go, little daughter, to the water outside.  
3. My daughter has no rest, the lake drives her, the lake only compels her, everything at home, everything she does with reluctance.  
4. She dipped the first scarf, the footbridge broke under her, and the depths stirred after the young girl.  
5. The waves surged from below, spread out in wide circles, and on the poplar by the rocks the green little man clapped.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. Good evening, my Andulka, what are you doing, my darling? I have come to visit you, to wish you happiness and health, to wish you happiness and health.  
2. What happiness do you wish me for my whole life? If you leave me, may the Lord God repay you.  
3. I have to leave you, our parents forbid us, because you are a poor girl, you have no thousands.  
4. You knew well that I have no thousands, you didn’t have to come to me and sadden my heart.  
5. You knew well that I am a poor girl, you didn’t have to come to me under the window into the yard.  
6. For three years and several months you have been coming to me, you knew well that I have no thousands.  
7. I forgive you the months, but not the years, my little heart aches deeply when I remember everything.  
8. It’s all in vain, my Mařenka, we must say goodbye, we must forget about our old love.  
9. And when it was nine o’clock, they brought them to the altar, they rang the funeral bell for him, the dearest of all.  
10. When it was midnight, at the midnight hour, his former lover came and stood by the bed.  
11. Don’t be afraid, my Jeníček, that I come to you at night, I came to look at your rich wife.  
12. I will visit you by day and also by night until I close your false black eyes.  
13. Wherever he went, he cried, he never had peace, he always saw his Andulka standing by the bed.  
14. He took a revolver on his desk and hurried with it to the river, there he aimed at his chest and shot through his heart.  
15. Three months after the wedding there was grief in the house, they are carrying the young husband who had shot himself.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. When the moon was shining beautifully, Duke Břetislav rose, the fire he felt for Judita, which had long been kindled in him, had long been kindled for Judita.  
2. Saying: Even if she were secluded before me beyond the Rhine and even if the heavens covered her, Judita must be mine!  
3. Thus he spoke and, spurring his horse with his squires, rode to Brod, where the emperor had hidden his daughter in a monastery choir, had hidden her before him.  
4. Near the monastery, in the green bushes, the prince hid his squires and hurried over hills, valleys, and ditches to his beloved, directed towards his Judita.  
5. And in the morning, when the maidens were singing, Břetislav stood in the monastery, and when they were about to go from the choir, he stepped forward, took Judita, took his beloved onto a horse.  
6. Then the maidens began to scream, the guards rushed out, and two chased after him armed, shouting: Don’t let him out, close the gates, don’t let him out!  
7. And immediately they pulled the gate shut with a mighty chain, and the castle guards wanted to strike him with their raised sword, raised above his head.  
8. Then breaking through the castle guard, he rushed to the gate with Jitka, breaking the chains and making his way toward Prague with Jitka, hurrying to Prague with his beloved.  
9. He said to her: And now, dear beloved, you are mine forever! And Jitka, happy with joy, said: Forever yours, now I am forever yours!  
10. And before the northern lights appeared for the second time, the joyful faces of the people of Prague welcomed Judita, welcomed their lady.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. Love that is not angered is not loved, it is even more steadfast, bound by a tunnel given by God.  
2. My dear maiden, if you love me, come, let's go for a walk, and that this evening, there is no cold.  
3. We came to the grove, sat on the grass, the birds were singing, we loved each other under the bright moonlight.  
4. When we were walking home from the grove, she went around a tree, grabbed a twig, tore her apron, her wreath fell down.  
5. She came and cried, lamenting to her mother, that she had been with her sweetheart, with her lover, she broke into tears.  
6. The mother weeps and the father curses, the youngest sister, like a little dove, laughs aside.  
7. Her friends also laugh at her, saying that her black dress is shortening, that her black dress is shortening.  
8. I will adjust my dress, I will bear sorrow, the longest until death, you my dearest, I will not go to the grove.  
9. There is a path to the Danube, worn and beautiful, my dear, lovely maiden walked on it, to drown herself.  
10. When she came to the river, she asked the Danube: If you are as deep as I am tall, I would bathe.  
11. Before she finished speaking, she jumped into the water, in a little while, false lover, she was drowned.  
12. Her light hair floated on the water, her blue eyes, her blue eyes lifted to the sky.  
13. Her bare feet were wading in the sand, her white hands, her white hands grasped the shore.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. On the day of Saint Rufus, Czech blood flowed like a river across the Moravian field, until the earth was stained with blood.  
2. There the Czech regiments stood, Otakar led the army, oh glorious Czech king, this was your final battle.  
3. Thus Otakar the victorious, who bore the Czech name as far as the Baltic Sea, fell by his Morava.  
4. Morava roars darkly and all the people weep, as if the last light of the sun had gone out forever.  
5. On the day of Saint Julius, in the land of the French, John of Luxembourg fell, the crown of knights.  
6. There at Mainz the king led the first attack, enemy troops immediately came against each other.  
7. The battle lost, we will lead you out so that you do not stay here. It was not the way that the Czech king would flee from battle.  
8. Tie my horse, lead me into the thickest fighting, so that the sword of the blind king may destroy the enemies.  
9. Led into the thickest battle, he began to wield his sword, the faithful retinue fell, the last Czech king.  
10. In the midst of his company lies the Czech king, who, although blind, fought heroically.  
11. The tragic battle of the camps, near Lipany, there fell Prokop the Bald, the great Taborite.  
12. There the battle was fought, a terribly bloody fight, there the proud army of the Taborites first fell.  
13. There the faithful Hussites poured out their blood, may every sincere Czech keep this in memory.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. Nearby outside the town in a quiet grove, you will see a grave adorned with roses.  
2. In it lie two faithful lovers, who swore love to each other.  
3. Vnislav was a dear boy, full of virtue, promising, hardworking, and did not know nightlife.  
4. Blažena faithfully loved him and wished to have him as her husband.  
5. Blažena’s father was very proud; he said: Vnislav, you are poor,  
6. my daughter has an estate and money, richer suitors are even more,  
7. my daughter has an estate, many fields; tomorrow the priest will join her with another.  
8. With cries, the lovers leave; Vnislav secured two pistols.  
9. Then he dug a grave in the quiet grove, waiting for Blažena at dawn.  
10. Guests all gathered for the wedding; Blažena was nowhere to be found.  
11. No one saw her, only a maidservant walking sadly into the grove.  
12. The guests hurried immediately to the grove, for they heard two gunshots.  
13. Here lies, here lies a young body, which for love shot itself.  
14. Here, according to Blažena, lies a letter, in which she thanked her parents:  
15. You did not want to let us live together, at least wish us to rot side by side.  
16. And according to Vnislav, a few lines: Let us lie in this little grove.  
17. When you go through that forest, look back, you will see a grave adorned, sigh yourself.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. Knock, knock on the window, open up for me, my maiden, knock, knock on the window, open up for me, my maiden.  
2. I am bringing you a golden ring, it has pearls and diamonds in it.  
3. She took the ring, put it on her finger, kissed me all over.  
4. We must not desire, boy, we must part.  
5. Not for the boy, but for wealth, that is the will of father and mother.  
6. The wedding took place, the music played, the bride did not smile.  
7. She used to sit under the window, remembering her love.  
8. Two ravens flew over: What troubles you, young lady?  
9. Secret love troubles me, it does not let me sleep at night.  
10. I cannot sleep day or night, I am always tormented because of you.  
11. We flew over the black forest, today we saw my beloved.  
12. He lies there, dozing in the forest, always thinking of you.  
The song is unfinished.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. In our Czech land stands Husinec, where Jan Hus was born, a Czech patriot. As a young boy, he would sit on his mother's lap and promise that he would be a son to the homeland.  
2. In his adult years, he studied in Prague, never forgetting what he had once promised. He embraced the truth with all his heart and proclaimed the holy truth as a son of the homeland.  
3. There on the banks of the Rhine he was built, on the border he was burned at the stake. The stake is already burning, smoke is rising from it, and now the son of the beloved homeland is dying.  
4. The stake is already burning, no one was there to take a spoonful of water and pour it on him, the stake is already burning, smoke is rising from it, and now the son of the beloved homeland is dying.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. In the field stands a cross, come, my dear, closer, before I go to that army, you will swear an oath to me.  
2. My dear rose, hurried to the cross, my Pepíček, my darling, she said to me.  
3. She went to the cross, raised three fingers, swore to Pepíček that she would be faithful to him.  
4. When he went to the army, he commanded her not to marry anyone, to wait for him.  
5. Three days after a year, Milka got married, completely forgot Pepíček, her darling.  
6. He came to Orláb, asked about Milka, friends said that she had married.  
7. He saddled his horse, took his carbine, aimed for Milka under the window, called her out.  
8. Milka, my Milka, you were false, at the cross you swore an oath, and you were unfaithful.  
9. With a loaded revolver in his hand, he sharply aimed at Milka’s chest, shot through her heart.  
10. Jesus, Mary, what have I done, that I have broken the oath to my Pepíček?  
11. Under Vysočany stands a chapel, there my Andulička swore falsely.  
12. My dearest, I cannot love you, you were unfaithful to me, I must not remember you.  
13. You took love from me, gave it to another, deprived me, the unhappy young man, of life.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. You mountains, black mountains, you know my pain, you know the cruel battles that torment my heart for false love.  
2. You know my boy who loved me, you know the deceitful one, you know the false one who deceived me.  
3. Who will kiss me now, who will comfort me more, I wither like a rose, no one helps me, nothing pleases me anymore.  
4. You promised me that I would be your wife, but times have changed, but times have changed, and another will be yours.  
5. I will no longer be yours, I do not want to be yours, you already have another girl, that deceived girl, you can take her.  
6. When you lay me in the coffin, in the cold coffin, put on my body, then put on my body withered lilies.  
10. A withered lily flower on cream-colored clothes, and all this as a sign, and all this as a sign of my sincere love.

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. A little tree blooms in the field, a little tree and under the little tree a little grave, a little grave, in which lies a young soldier.  
2. Birds fly to him and sit on the branches, singing to their beloved.  
3. The beloved at home cried and waited for her beloved and waited for her beloved.  
4. When she could not wait any longer, she ran out into the dark night and called to her beloved.  
5. Return to me, my dear, come back, sincere heart, wherever you are, my dear.  
6. The little horse trots along the road, the beloved returns to the bride, the beloved returns to the bride.  
7. Knock, knock on the window: Are you sleeping, my dear maiden? Open your little window!  
8. Oh, I am not sleeping, not sleeping, my dear, for I have been waiting for you all this time, for I have been waiting for you all this time.  
9. The beloved opens the door, embraces her beloved, embraces her beloved.  
10. Where did you put the gold ring, which had a precious stone in it, which had a precious stone in it?  
11. I hid the ring in the ground, when I was fighting, my dear, when I was fighting, my dear.  
12. Where did you put the handkerchief, my dear, which my hands embroidered, decorated with little flowers?  
13. The handkerchief tore apart, when I was fighting in Russia, the wind blew it away in the field.  
14. If you are a sincere girl, sit with me on the horse, we will go home together.  
15. Where is your home, my dear? There is my home built, far away in Russian land.  
16. They rode out into the dark night, the horse’s hooves rang, until they arrived at the cemetery.  
17. What is that, my dear, an enclosure? That is my garden, my green garden.  
18. What are those crosses, my dear? Those are my green trees, those are my green trees.  
19. What grave is opening? That is my home, my dear, that is my home, my dear.  
20. If you are sincere to me, come inside with me, my dear, come inside with me, my dear.  
21. Why wouldn’t I be sincere, since I waited for you, since I stayed faithful to you?  
22. And when they entered the grave, the stones fell down, covering the two lovers.  
23. The organ played in the church, the bells rang in the tower, thus they celebrated their wedding together.

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. What does it mean middle of the new? We have not seen a clear sky, the stars shine beautifully, it must be something, it must be something.  
2. A beautiful angel floats in the clouds, bringing joy to all creation. Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace to people. Hopsa, boys, together with girls, let us hurry to the manger, salvation has come to us, hopsa, hejsa, tralala, hopsa, hejsa, tralala. Merry Christmas festivities, sing, children, carols, about what truly happened, that a child was born to people. Christ the Lord was born, let us rejoice, a flower bloomed from the rose for us, let us rejoice, from a pure life, from a royal lineage for us, for us was born.

label: KOLEDY
translation: God bless this house, we sing to the Lord God, to the little child, Christ Jesus, born today in Bethlehem.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Good evening to you from God, I am a little girl, I would like some apples. Don’t be angry with me, give me many for free, I’m not driving a cart, whatever you give me, I will take. Our Prague, vol. 1 (1924–1925), no. 2, p. 32.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Little hole, little hole, a carol, give me a mutt or two. If you don't give me a mutt, I'll pierce your fur, little hole, little hole, a carol. Children sing this at Christmas.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Children go caroling around the village. They stop at grandma's, at auntie's, at the godmother's, and receive something everywhere.

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. When I went in the morning to carol there, the cook chased me down with a pitchfork.  
2. She fell down the stairs, broke her face, broke her face, I was glad about that.  
3. Then some dog grabbed her by the skirt, it was a sad dance, until I tell you about it.  
4. She immediately came upstairs, the capon escaped from the fire, from the fireplace, that was sorrow.  
5. The goose got burned, the roast ran out, the cakes got burnt, that was sorrow.  
6. I stood under the stairs, watched everything, she deserves all that because I got nothing.  

The children were singing about Christmas.

label: KOLEDY
translation: 1. Gloria, let glory be given in heaven! Peace to people all over the earth, among all Christians, and among us. 2. A precious lord has come to us, king of earth and heaven, from a humble stable, lying on hay in the town near Bethlehem. 3. His mother rocks him, Joseph sings to him, the ox and the donkey breathe on him and warm Jesus, the Son of God. Our Prague, vol. 4 (1927–1928), no. 4, p. 64.

label: KOLEDY
translation: 1. Hush now, give, little Jesus, son of Mary, sweet little boy, son of Mary. 2. We hurry to Bethlehem, we will sing to Baby Jesus. 3. The little birds will rejoice for you, the shepherds will sing. The carolers sang at Christmas.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Feast, feast, companions, give painted eggs. If you don’t give red, at least give white, the hen will lay you another. Jump, maiden, into the water, hand strawberries to the gentlemen! Why should I jump in there? I would get all soaked. Where would I dry? At the lady’s place. The lady is not home, she took the goat to town, she took a piece of dough with her. She was giving it to the birds, to the peasants in the field.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Hody, hody, primrose, don’t let me go away empty-handed. Give me at least five eggs, cut me a mazance cake. If you give me nothing, you will soon see it—you will find great damage on your farm. The cows will have no calves, the sheep will have no lambs, the hens will stop laying, if you let me go empty-handed. Our Prague, vol. 2 (1925–1926), no. 7, p. 112.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Hody, hody, pomlázku, I am coming to you for love, so please give me a painted egg quickly. Shup, shup, shup, I gobble up the egg. Our Prague, vol. 4 (1927–1928), no. 7, p. 112.

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. Boys, come to us, I will tell you something: Dress yourselves in a little coat, we will all go to Baby Jesus, we will sing, we will carol.  
2. An angel is floating in beautiful clouds, singing with a pleasant voice, revealing joy to the people: Jesus was born to us, God and Lord.  
3. He lies in Bethlehem, in a stable on hay, he has no pillow nor ragged swaddling clothes, born into this poor world in poverty.  
4. Honzík, hurry and run to the forest, bring him some dry wood, keep little Jesus warm, so he does not freeze with his dear mother!  
5. Nanynka is running, she is already at the door, carrying a bottle of cream, a piece of butter for the semolina, to make porridge, to give to Jesus.  
6. Tonda with the pipes, Vašek with the trumpets are hurrying to Bethlehem, to play for him a little, to make music there.  
7. Then they played for the little king right away: břim břim tradla fidli fidli, dujduj dadla dydli dydli; hop hop hopsasa, sang the group.

label: KOLEDY
translation: I’m going caroling, carrying a bag; whoever fills it for me will be my friend. Whoever gives me half, I will take a stick to them; whoever gives me a quarter, I will send death upon them.

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. I am a little doll, bringing you news, I have just run in from Bethlehem at this time.  
2. There is a great commotion, for the Virgin Mary has given birth to a beautiful little boy in this world.  
3. Lojza, a little boy, is just pretending that he has a new violin and wants to be a shepherd.  
4. I was standing by the door, just looking, when little Lojzíček ran up to me and grabbed me around.  
5. And so he spun me around as much as he could, and I tore my pink shoes.  
6. I ask you, young gentlemen, please give me a Christmas carol, for I am not allowed to go home.  
7. My dad would scold me for not having shoes; I won’t go home before I earn them. The carolers sang at Christmas.

label: KOLEDY
translation: 1. I have come to you to tell you that today is Christmas caroling. Whoever gives me something, I will bless that one, that one, that one. 2. Whoever gives me nothing is from the Stingy ones. I will frown at them, growl like a bear, then, then, then I will go away.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Prague 8, 1904, said Josef Heller We are coming to you for caroling, oh our lord, we are coming to you, we ask you. Believe us when we say we are poor, just do not mock our poverty.

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. Jurinka from the yard, what are you doing? Martínku from the farm, why are you calling? Near Bethlehem in that barn there is a very bright light, you will see it.  
2. What could it be, Jurinka? Maybe you imagined it, silly. Just don't delay, hurry there, you will see what is there, Martínku!  
3. Let’s take both of you, Jurinka, surely you are afraid of a bear. Let's stop at the shepherd's hut, at least two Wallachians will come with us.  
4. Good, Jurinka, you are right, surely the Messiah was born. He was born in Bethlehem, he lies there on the straw, our Lord. The carolers sang at Christmas. Transcribed by tinsmiths.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Carol, carol, little horse, carry grandma on your back.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Carol, carol, the whip, uncle will spank auntie. Alright, uncle, alright then, auntie should obey you.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Caroling, caroling, Štěpán, what are you carrying in the jug? I'm carrying carols, I slipped on the ice with them, dogs gathered around me, they ate my carols. And I, a little caroler, came here to get a treat. Give me a treat, don’t laugh at me. If you give me a piece of honey, I’ll go even happier. If you give me pastries, so my little cap doesn’t cry.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Carol, carol, auntie, give me a nice little apple, some nuts and a little pear, so I would have a full basket, also some rolls, two or three pieces. May God grant it to you in the garden, in the field.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Prague 8, 1906, said J. Poledna: Carol, carol at the window, may God grant you a good day. Pajmáma killed a hen, pantáta gave her a pinch on the nose.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Carolers at midnight: What will you give from the courtyard on Christmas Eve? The lady was generous, she did not want to get up for us: Get up, daughter, go upstairs, give them the carol, a thrifty one, a bit on the side, some tolar coin!

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. The Christmas holidays have come, let us be merry, the Christmas holidays have come, let us be merry! Carolers are walking around, giving honor and praise, whoever gives them a generous carol, they sing nicely.  
2. And when I heard them, I immediately went among them, and when I heard them, I immediately went among them. First, I go to the living room, to get the most, and a few groshes from your pocket into my corner.  
3. I came to you for caroling, give me some cakes, I haven’t eaten any yet this year and almost cry. My dear housewife, come first to the hall and give me some cake before someone sees me.  
4. Someone gives me one cake, someone maybe two, but since yeast is very bad now, they see that it’s before the holidays, they pour water into it, then people sell it off, and suffer losses from it.  
5. But someone bakes a good number of nice cakes, and I, with great anger, ran away from the oven [...] I close this song, bidding you farewell.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Bake the doughnuts, the bear hunters will come before you bake one. If there were a doughnut, it would surely come to me; my sweet tooth would once be treated like a lady. Boys from Libeň used to go "for doughnuts" to houses during the carnival and sing. Our Prague, vol. 2 (1925–1926), no. 6, p. 91.

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. Praised be the Lord Jesus Christ, brother Matěj! Forever and ever, amen, brother Ondřej. I’m running to Bethlehem, I don’t know what is happening there. Oh please, come back with me, I will tell you something.  
2. Our boys were tending the flocks near Bethlehem at night, at midnight there was a brightness as clear as day, they heard angelic singing, they ran to Bethlehem, saying the Messiah was born, they spoke the truth.  
3. In that common stable lies in the hay in the manger, he has a mother, a poor mother in wretched swaddling clothes, he is said to be so beautiful, he smiles at everyone, whoever looks at him feels joy from him.  
4. Anastasia the merchant’s [brings] almonds, biscuits, Cecilia the craftsman's daughter brings apples, nuts, and Františka brings spices and Františka brings spices, Lucka brings gingerbread, marzipan, and Lenka brings melons.  
5. Františka, the smith's daughter, carries the quilt, just hurry up, she’s trembling from the cold, Františka is pulling feathers, Verunka will sew it, just hurry quickly, or it is cold.  
6. And you, brother friends, tune the instruments, where are the little children, they will sing, Franta, you start the organ, Ondřej start the trombones, Tadeáš had a horn and Nácek the drums.  
7. Bagpipes, bagpipes I will blow, let them roar loudly, let the music by the manger please him, let us be joyful when Jesus is sleeping, his mother will rock him, he will fall asleep peacefully. Children still sang this as a Christmas carol about thirty years ago.

label: KOLEDY
translation: The whip is being braided, we rush to the door and from the door to the kitchen among good housewives. We will ask uncle for red eggs, we will ask aunt for a red decorated egg.

label: KOLEDY
translation: 1. Listen to me a little, my dear lady, and you as well, dear father, what I will tell you at this moment, what new and unheard-of thing I will tell you. 2. That there lies on the hay the heavenly child, the heavenly little child, the beautiful little Jesus, Mary cradles him, Saint Joseph sings to him. 3. Shepherds came there, they wanted to play the bagpipes, they were not tuned, not coordinated together, each played his own note and meanwhile stomped his foot.

label: KOLEDY
translation: A little twig, a red rooster, a clucking hen, a speckled egg, madam, you have to give it to me.

label: KOLEDY
translation: 1. Angels came, they came from heaven, flying to the Virgin Mary. 2. In the Bethlehem manger, Mary sits, awaiting the birth of God. 3. And Saint Joseph tends the son, his little son, our king. 4. The shepherds played the bagpipes for him there, the holy angels sang a song. The Christmas carolers sang.

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. God’s luck, God’s peace we wish you, first to the master of the house, then to his children, we come from afar, bringing you news of what has happened here in the town of Bethlehem.  
2. A Savior has been born to us, he sends me to you, give me a little carol, and I will give it to him, he does not want bread, his mother has fed him, rather you should send a small coin or a ducat.  
3. I kindly ask you, send me off, I have no time to wait, I must go to Bethlehem to rock the child, this child is a great lord, he will repay you for it, you will rejoice in heaven forever.

label: KOLEDY
translation: 1. That unfortunate feast: the women, all terrified, wanted to bake cakes and poppy seed flatcakes. 2. My wife sent me for spices to Kouřim, for yeast to Prague, because they were expensive here. 3. I had to go to Vyžlovka for almonds and raisins, to Benešov for milk, which I felt very sorry about. 4. And now for poppy seeds, for jam, I went to Divišov, to Hrušov for paper, that was a grumpy walk. 5. And to Lenka Fialová, there I had to get dried fruit, in Kocerady at Trýba’s I stopped for fish. 6. I endured this whole week, and when the celebrated day came, we baked the cakes, we got terribly scared. 7. The yeast was spoiled, the cakes were clumped, my wife jumped with anger, hitting me with a wooden spoon. 8. I wanted a piece of the flatcake from her, she immediately gave me two slaps, for my troubles I got beaten. 9. So I end this song here and warn you, men, take my example and don’t interfere with women. The singer learned the song from his grandmother.

label: KOLEDY
translation:
Libeň 1913, sung by Josef Němeček and Miroslav Selingr

1. Christmas has come, our uncle, we come to you, we ask you to believe that we are poor, then do not mock our poverty.

2. Baby Jesus was poorer, was the eternal God, deigned to be born, was the eternal God. He was born in Bethlehem, lies there poor on the stone.

label: KOLEDY
translation: An egg, eggs are laid by the hen, they are searched for and gathered by the housewife. Write words on those eggs, so that my little heart will rejoice!

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. Easter has come, our dear mister, the frosts have already stopped, spring is approaching.  
2. The field is turning green, flowers are blooming again, the day is warming up as the sun shines.  
3. Foals are being born, goslings are hatching, sheep are lambing, cows have calves.  
4. The whole world rejoices at these holy holidays, give us a whip, or the day will be short.  
5. The kind lady is bringing us a whip, she will cut us a mazance and give us an egg each.  
6. We have some flour at home, we will bake cakes, if we don't like them, we will run away from them.  
7. Add us a little schnapps, so we can go further, when the music starts, we will lead you through.  
8. I will throw you up high, cheerfully yell, when my mouth is in a festive mood, I will not complain.  
9. I am already finishing the whipping, my mouth hurts, may God grant you in the garden, in the field.  
The singer learned the carol from his grandfather, a native of Libeň.

label: KOLEDY
translation: The wolf is sitting under the oak tree, playing cards with the dove. Little dove, give me an egg, when I go to the woods, I will bring you beads! The boys said about the Easter whip.

label: KOLEDY
translation: Arise, shepherds, Kuba, Matouš, and Ondráš! Why do you call us, won’t you let us sleep, our brother? Are you sleeping so deeply that you don’t hear the call, the angel sent down from God’s throne? Rise up, give praise! Older Jakub, brother shepherd! What did we say years ago when we were tending those sheep? That a Savior would be born to us, the redeemer of the whole world. What shall I give Him, my Lord, what shall I give Him? I will give Him my little Veruška, my little ladybug, that I will give Him, my Lord, I will give Him. I am a poor bagpiper, I have only my bagpipes, and I will play for Him, my Lord, I will play for Him.

label: KOLEDY
translation:
1. The meadow is green, on that meadow there is a twig, shepherds were grazing there, eating porridge made from milk, shepherds were grazing there, eating porridge made from milk.  
2. An angel flew to them, told them something, that Christ the Lord was born, so that everyone would know.  
3. They did not delay, they set out on the road to Bethlehem to Jesus, called him Lord.  
4. We little children would gladly take caroling by the hand, if you would give us something.  
5. If you do not give us anything, you will see, we will break all the pots you have on the shelf,  
6. and the little balls too, then we will run away from the house, because you did not want to give carols to anyone.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: 1. Fiksum, fárum, hambasárum, there stands a little grove, fiksum, fárum, hambasárum, and in it a small house. 2. In that little house, a maiden was looking out of the window. 3. A young man passed by, asking the maiden for a wreath. 4. She did not give it to him, she even laughed at him. Song for a children's game.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Blow, blow, little wind, drop me one pear, drop me one or two, both will be good.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Swing, darling, swing, swing on my knees, little Johnny is rocking you, rocking, rocking, whoa. Lullaby.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: I have a spotted little dog at home, he guards my black horse. Help, help, my Mařenka, to weave a wreath. Song for a children's game: The children join hands in a circle, one goes to the middle, covers their eyes with their hand, spins around and sings. With each syllable, they point to one player. The person on whom the last syllable falls runs out of the circle, and the one in the middle chases them until they bring them back. Then the chaser is replaced by the returned player.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation:
1st row: He rides, he rides, Mr. Jan rides, Šalman rides, the whole of his knighthood rides.  
2nd row: What does Mr. Jan command, what does Šalman command, what does his entire knighthood command?  
1st row: We command your daughter, beautiful as a bright star, who has five keys from nine granaries.  
2nd row: Here you have our daughter, beautiful as a bright star, who has five keys from nine granaries.  
1st row [to the tune of the first verse]: Mr. Jan commands, Šalman commands, the whole of his knighthood commands.  

Song for children’s game: The same number of boys and girls. Boys stand in a row, one next to the other, holding hands, and opposite them, about six steps away, girls also stand in a row side by side, holding hands. Each verse of the song is eight measures long, each line consisting of two measures. During the first two lines, the row of boys or girls singing that verse moves one step closer to the opposite row with each measure; during the third and fourth lines, they move back about four steps. Then, one girl at the end of her row steps into the boys’ row between the first and second boy at the front, and the boys, together with that girl, lead the game again for the second girl in the same manner as they played for the one who was between them, and they repeat this continuously until even the last girl finally joins the boys’ row. When by this method only one row remains from both rows, they form a circle, hold hands, and sing.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The old woman rocked the devil on an oak stump: Hushabye, my little devil, I won't tell on you.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation:
The mill wheel, the mill wheel for four rixdollars, the wheel broke apart on us, causing much damage, it went snap. Give us a plane, a saw, we'll play a little longer.  
Song for the children's game "The mill wheel": Children hold hands in a circle, spin around and sing. At the word "snap," they squat down, then jump up at the end and the game starts again.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: I have a handkerchief, I have, to whom shall I give it? To no one else but my beloved, to him I will give it. Song for the children's handkerchief game: Children stand in a circle, hold hands, one has an open handkerchief in their hand, walks around and sings. At the last verse, they give the handkerchief to the person standing opposite them face to face. That person goes into the circle, and the game repeats.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Mařenka is crying for cakes. We don’t have cakes and we won’t give buns, she doesn’t want bread, she doesn’t want bread. Lullaby.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation:
1. Mařenko, Mařenko, don’t cry, the horned one would come for you.  
2. He would cuckold the girl, the girl would be miserable.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The spin dance spun around day and night across the field. Song for the children's dance game "rejdovačka": The children hold hands diagonally and hop with a sliding step four steps to the right, then four to the left. They sing while doing this. This repeats as many times as desired. Mostly girls play.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Libeň, undated. [before 1908], performer not specified The Samaritan woman is already coming, she is carrying a pretty bridesmaid. The Samaritans are great gentlemen, and you, bridesmaid, get off the wheel! The girls are singing. Song for the children's dance game "The Samaritan Woman."

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Here I am a little tinsmith, I make tinware, I went through the village, along the big street, three girls were looking through one window. One was Julita, the second a cook, and I won’t tell you who the third was, she is my lover. Children sing, imitating the tinsmith's song.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: 1. This is how young gentlemen ride, this is how old gentlemen ride, this is how goose-, goose-, goose-, goose-, hussars ride. 2. This is how half-men ride, this is how peasants ride, this is how wealthy fat cats ride. Song for a play with a small child: The child is bounced on the knee and the song is sung to them.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Two dolls were dancing, two shoemakers were dancing, one told the other that no one wants her. They danced it, they didn't know how, they came home and learned it. Children's dance: Two girls take each other by the hands in a cross and spin around.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation:
Libeň 1911, sung by Oldřich Kejha and Josef Kopřiva  
You butchers, craftsmen, who are in the habit of taking a cut from everything you buy? How much will you literally pay for that ox? How much will you pay, that you don’t bargain at all, how much will you literally pay?  

Song for a children's dance game: Two children stand facing each other, holding hands crossed.  
Measure 1–2: four side steps to the right,  
Measure 3–4: four side steps to the left,  
Measure 5–6: four side steps to the right,  
Measure 7–8: four side steps to the left.  
Stop.  
Measure 9–12: applause on the heavy beat,  
Measure 13–20: holding right hands and spinning, slapping thighs with left hands (one slap per lift).

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Little garden, little enclosure, little flower. Who taught you that? The late uncle. Song for a children's game called "drábky."

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The little hare sat alone in its hole, sat alone. Poor thing, what’s wrong that you can’t hop? Feel like hopping, feel like hopping and jump up! Song for a children's game about the little hare: Children stand in a circle, hold hands, and spin around. One sits in the middle on their haunches and uses their fingers to mimic hare ears. The circle sings. The hare jumps up, catches one child from the circle, and that child becomes the hare.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Lord Jesus in my thoughts, Virgin Mary at my feet, little angels of God all around on every side!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Little angel, my guardian! Watch over my little soul.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Sleep, my little darling, sleep and rest, close your little eyes: sleep, baby, baby little one! close your tiny little eyes. Sleep, my little angel, sleep and rest, mother is rocking her baby; sleep, baby, baby little one! sleep, my little angel, sleep and rest.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Sleep, little son, sleep, close your eyes: The Lord God will sleep with you, rocking you like little angels: sleep, little angel, sleep! Sleep, little angel, sleep, close your eyes: when you wake up in a little while, your mother will give you some porridge, sleep, darling, sleep! Sleep, darling, sleep, close your eyes: she will give you a drum and little violin, she would never give you away to the whole world, sleep, little son, sleep!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: I would sleep, sleep, but not alone: with Lord Jesus, with the little angels. I would even say a prayer for that, to sleep with the Virgin Mary.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Little baby, how sleepy you are! The little birds have long since risen, sung their prayers – and you are still sleeping. Little baby, how sleepy you are! I have milked the cows, cooked porridge for you – and you are still sleeping.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: A little mouse runs to Tábor, carrying a bag of ginger. And you, little one, stay at home; and you, tall one, go to the Tábor tower!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: A little mouse ran up the wall: so that our Vojtíšek would laugh!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Rolling bread. The child lies on a blanket, and the mother or nanny rolls them from one side to the other, saying: I am rolling, rolling the bread; if I don't succeed, I will give it a bun on the back – bun, bun, bun! At the last words, she gently pats the child three times on the backside.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Waving hands. A mother or nanny teaching a child to wave or clap their hands says: Patsy, patsy, little hands! Daddy bought little shoes, and mommy a belt for the mouse’s tail. Or: and mommy a little broom for the crying Ančička.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: I shake, I shake my little hands! Daddy bought little shoes so that my little feet can run and my little hands can shake.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Trips, trips, little trips! The Lord gave us little hands so that the little hands could work and the little feet could run.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Paci, paci handkerchiefs! We will go to the meadows, through Bohemia to Němec Majdalenka for a wreath.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Splinters, splinters, little hands, may the Lord give us little rolls, we’ll nibble on the little rolls, we’ll share them with the storks. Where are those little storks? They flew off to the gate. Where is that gate? It burned to dust. Where is that dust? The water took it. Where is that water? The ox drank it. Where is that ox? The virgin ate it. Where is that virgin? Buried in the churchyard.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Cooking porridge. The nanny takes the child by the hand and moves her finger in a circle on the child's palm, as if stirring porridge in a small pot, while saying: The little mouse is cooking porridge in a green pot; children come like crumbs, she gives each a little bite, until the pot is empty.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: What are fingers? This is dad – this is mom – this is grandpa – this is grandma – this is a grandson, a little boy.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: I’m cooking, cooking porridge in that small saucepan: this one boils – this one fries – this one bakes – this one eats – this one says: little one – give me a piece!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: To soothe a child. When a child harmlessly falls or bumps themselves and starts to cry, they sing to the child to make them laugh and stop crying, like this: Bald cat – bowl of peas: if the little boy used to sit, he wouldn’t have a bruise. Pic, pic, pic! The little boy won’t get hurt again.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Bald cat! Where have you been running? "To the kitchen." – What did you get there? "Three buns." – If that child were sitting, they wouldn't have that!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: A little mouse climbs the wall – so Karlíček can laugh!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Rock the child. Rock, rock, rock! The cat ate the groats, the tomcat ate peas on the stove; the kittens were angry that they were not given any too. Rock, rock, rock: they were all foolish!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The Little Blacksmith. Take the child's little foot into your hand and lightly tap the heel with your fist, saying: Little blacksmith, little blacksmith! Shoe my little foot: when you have shoed it, I will give you a groš for it. Little blacksmith, little blacksmith! Shoe my little foot, shoe it nicely, I will give you four Czech groschen; shoe both of them for me, I will pay you: on Saint Vitus's day I will give you a measure of rye; on Saint Lawrence's day I will give you a measure of wheat.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: How do they ride? They sit the little boy on their knee as if on a horse and, shaking their knee, they say: Here go the young gentlemen, here go the old gentlemen, here go the goose– goose– goose– goose– hussars.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Little apples. When a little girl gets an apple, her mother or nanny sings to her like this: I am small, very small, I like to eat little apples, when daddy buys them for me, and mommy peels them.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Spinning wheel. When a child cries arbitrarily, other children laugh at him, spinning their hands like a spinning wheel and saying: The spinning wheel hums, hums: tomorrow will be a big celebration, and the day after tomorrow even bigger – our Kačka’s broom suits her!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Bogeyman. A crying child is quieted by frightening them with the Bogeyman or the Horned One (Cornifer), like this: The bogeyman sits under the chimney and has a horned soul, carrying lumps with caraway in it, a head fully covered in hair: be quiet and don’t cry, my child – the lump will come and eat you!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Be quiet, little one, don’t cry, don’t cry, or the horned one would come: he would gore you with his horns, and you would be miserable.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The Beggar. A child, seeing that another child is eating something, for example bread, a roll, etc., begs from him a little piece like this: stretches out the left arm, straightens the index finger on it, and makes a pinch with the other fingers. Then the first finger of the right hand alternately jumps on the finger, on the pinch, on the palm, on the pinch, on the finger, and so on, while saying: I play, I play the organ, all the fingers are broken, only one got me so I could get something. A nun from Saint Francis came to me, placed a stone – forever and ever amen.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Who gives to me will ride a golden horse to heaven; who does not give will ride a scabby pig to hell.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Alphabet. A-B-C-D – the cat purrs, the tomcat spins, the dog counts how many threads up to ten.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Learn, schoolboy, learn gladly, you will get a golden spinning wheel; if you do not learn, you will get a broom on your bottom.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Children's handwriting. Ančička wrote: the little cat reads after her; little Mourek wrote: the tomcat reads after him.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: To grandpa and grandma. Boys, boys! Grandpa is sitting in the basket, grandma is sitting in the churn: come on, boys, churn it!

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: In that black forest, the devil is carrying the old woman: you boys, behave yourselves, pray!

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Hush hush, granny, I’m going for the lard so grandpa won’t know, he would tell on me.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Shifty shifty, old man, I'm going to get that bitch so the woman won't know, she would have told.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: There was an old woman who sold apples, sloe berries, rose hips, hawthorns, she was a small retailer.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: The old woman climbs into the elderberry bush, I will climb in after her; wherever the old woman goes, there I go, we will both be there.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Hey, nik, nik, nik, nik, nik, the chimney sweep got married, took an old woman for himself, tied her to the brewery: what to do with such a Lucifer, better a fur coat even with a sleeve.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Hey! no, no, no, no, no! the furrier got married, took an old woman for himself, the wedding was on a ship, and the old woman was happy, she had a devil for a friend; the friend was a good fox, got on the old woman, went to Silesia! as it grinds, so it grinds, boldly rides off on the old woman: and from Silesia to Moravia, may the devils take the old woman!

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: About names. Mikuláš lost his cloak, Mikuláška her skirt, they were both sad.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Michal, Michal! He poked the goat, and she bleated until the evening.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Havel, Havel! He rode into the forest on a white horse, the wolf is chasing him there.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Kučera, Kučera! What was growling over there? Maybe it was a cow or a bull: it must have been some kind of devil!

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Whoever prays in German. Our Father, who art – set the table, we are going to eat!

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: To Němec. Our Father, who art – two Germans ganged up on one Czech because he was bitten by a flea. Our Father, who art – two Germans fought over one Czech because a flea bit him.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: The German beetle was banging pots, throwing them across the clearing, and from the clearing into the puddle – the German beast in the throat! The German beetle was banging pots, hiding them under his hat; and when he had half a dozen, he cooked broth from them. The German beetle was banging pots, throwing them under the bench – because he is crazy.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: To the Frenchman. Frenchman, Frenchman! Four cats bitten, the fifth one ran away from him before it was baked.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: A strange church. In a hollow, in a valley—what is that shining there? There is a church made of cream, beaten with buttermilk; the bells are made of whey, the ropes of curd: whoever rings them there will please God. If someone does not want to believe this, ask the bell-ringer: after all, a piece of clotted milk still lies there.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Our Father – our dad sat us on the bench, gave us bread by the slice; we wanted more, he gave us old pliers; mom came with a shovel, drove us out along with dad.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: The old woman sits by the stove, licking kuthans; the dog growls at her to push her down; the rooster crows at her that it will tear her fur apart.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Saint Job says: "Eat four and the fifth is a trifle." Saint George says: "Don’t wait for one by four!" Saint Florian says: "He who doesn’t wait is a boor." Pike, fish, frog, crayfish: once you’re full, leave it be.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: I am doing, I am doing a sermon: four cats tied up, and the fifth dog climbed into the oven; he stole a toast there, ran with it through the market; a cow met him, that was his mother; a little bull met him, that was his father; a bullock met him, that was his uncle; a stallion met him, that was his grandfather; a little goat met him, that was his grandmother. Amen, amen, amen, the old woman fell from the stove and the old man from the oven: they got up after all.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Telling a fairy tale. I tell, I tell a fairy tale that the dog jumped over the pile. I tell, I tell a second one, that water flows through the gutter. I tell, I tell a third, that the children slept on the stove; and when they slept well, they received a piece of bread.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: The final death. There was a little house, in that house a little table, on the table a little bowl, in the bowl some water, in the water a little fish. "Where is the fish?" The cat ate it. "Where is the cat?" It ran into the forest. "Where is the forest?" It burned to dust. "Where is the dust?" The water took it. "Where is the water?" The oxen drank it. "Where are the oxen?" The lords ate them. "Where are the lords?" Buried in the cemetery.

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: Nine crows. To Voře, Voře Jan, flew nine crows: the first says: Good Voře; the second says: Not good Voře; the third says: Good Voře; the fourth says: Not good Voře; the fifth says: Good Voře; the sixth says: Not good Voře; the seventh says: Good Voře; the eighth says: Not good Voře; the ninth says: Good Voře Jan!

label: Dětské posměšky
translation: About the Rooster and the Hen. The rooster and the hen went together into the game preserve to gather nuts. The rooster said, "Whatever each of us finds, it will be shared equally between us." – "Alright!" replied the hen; she scratched around, scratched around, and uncovered a kernel, and honestly shared it with the rooster. Then the rooster also found a kernel, but he was greedy, wanting to swallow it himself quickly so the hen wouldn’t know, and the kernel got stuck in his throat. "Run quickly, hen, bring water, or I will die." As he said this, he rolled on the ground with his legs up in the air. The hen ran to the spring for water: "Spring, give water to my rooster; he’s lying there in the preserve, legs up – I’m afraid, afraid he will die." The spring said, "I won't give you water until you bring me a handkerchief from the seamstress." The hen ran to the seamstress: "Seamstress, give a handkerchief to the spring, the spring will give water to my rooster; he’s lying there in the preserve, legs up – I’m afraid, afraid he will die." The seamstress said, "I won’t give you a handkerchief until you bring me shoes from the shoemaker." The hen ran to the shoemaker: "Master, give shoes to the seamstress, the seamstress will give a handkerchief to the spring, the spring will give water to my rooster," etc. The shoemaker said, "I won’t give you shoes until you bring me bristles from the pig." The hen ran to the pig: "Piggy, give the shoemaker bristles, the shoemaker will give shoes to the seamstress," etc. The pig said, "I won’t give you bristles until you bring me malt from the maltster." The hen ran to the maltster: "Maltster, give the pig malt, the pig will give bristles to the shoemaker," etc. The maltster said, "I won’t give you malt until you bring me cream from the cow." The hen ran to the cow: "Cow, give the maltster cream, the maltster will give the pig malt," etc. The cow said, "I won’t give you cream until you bring me grass from the meadow." The hen ran to the meadow: "Meadow, give the cow grass, the cow will give the maltster cream," etc. The meadow said, "I won’t give you grass until you ask the sky for dew." The hen begged: "Sky, dear sky! Give the meadow dew, the meadow will give the cow grass, the cow will give the maltster cream, the maltster will give the pig malt, the pig will give the shoemaker bristles, the shoemaker will give shoes to the seamstress, the seamstress will give a handkerchief to the spring, the spring will give water to my rooster; he’s lying there in the preserve, legs up – I’m afraid, afraid he will die!" The sky took pity on the rooster and sent dew to the meadow, the meadow gave grass, the cow cream, the maltster malt, the pig bristles, the shoemaker shoes, the seamstress a handkerchief, and the spring water. The hen scooped it with her beak, and as soon as she poured it down the rooster’s throat, the kernel slid down, the rooster jumped to his feet, flapped his wings, and crowed happily: "Cock-a-doodle-doo!" – And after that, he was never greedy again and shared honestly with the hen.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Animal language. The horsefly, afraid of freezing in winter, flies off to warmer regions in autumn; and when spring comes again, it returns to Bohemia, sits on the fence, and calls to the farmer at the window: Are you alive, farmer? Are you alive? Are you alive?

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Fifteen feet. I write, I write fifteen, once again fifteen: if you don’t believe me, comrade, write it down yourself!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Thirty-two feet. 1. Hela, Hela, Helana, dad bought a ram, the ram got dizzy, he poured a mug of beer. You drink, you drink, gentlemen! and I will drink with you: believe it or not, I will write thirty-two.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: I walk, I walk around the forest, an owl hoots at me; one maiden, the other a widow, looks out from the little window: one is longing, the other is suffering – I have to write down thirty-two of them: but there aren’t more than that. Reciting these counting rhymes, they mark a stroke with chalk or pencil at each step, as indicated above, and then they count those strokes.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Counting rhymes before the game. Before the children start playing, they usually count to determine who will begin or who will take a certain role in the game. The players stand close together in a circle, and one of them recites one of the following counting rhymes, striking the rhythm of each verse louder, and with hand gestures indicates players one by one in order around the circle, starting from themselves. The player on whom the last beat falls, or as they say, the last "word," steps out. The others then count again either with the same or a different rhyme, and so on until only one remains. 1. One, two, three – we are brothers; who among us has crawled into the straw: him, him, or him, let's throw him out of the circle.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: One, two, three, four, five – the blacksmith is forging a nail, the child will cry today: and you get out of the circle!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: One, two, three, four, five – What’s that? "Roast." Where did you get it? "In the kitchen." Hupity, cupity, out of the kitchen!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: One, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine: the chimney sweep had a pretty daughter, a bear kidnapped her.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: One two – that’s me; one three – that’s us; one four – get out of the hole; one five – was picking a flower; one six – he had honor; one seven – went out in front of the house; one eight – he was wise; one nine – he was a bear; one ten – stayed hanging: and that’s you!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: One, two, three, four, five, six, seven, meadow into the hat, dumbbells, lines, whack! Or: Anda, nonsense, trouble, trouble, six, seven, meadow, into the hat, tics, hips, flok.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Eggs, hay, straw – we were building maypoles, on Saint Francis's day a nun flew in; we will sell the nun, we will buy beads.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Little angels, two blocks, nuts sticking out: if you don’t go, I’ll beat you with a golden stone.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The bagpipes played at the rascal’s, on the drum: boom boom boom! on the little violin: toodle-um! the bass rumbled: growl growl growl! go away from us, Janku!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: A black pig is rooting in the cemetery horseradish: who will harvest it? Here it is, laziness!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: There is a maple by the stream, they call it the key: whoever the word falls on must leave!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: A beech stands in the middle of the meadows, on that beech nine knots and something more: red slippers, white work shoes – out with the duke from the castle!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The crayfish crawls out of the hole, sticks out its whiskers; sickle, acorn, red stool, small hill, small forest, four eyes out!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Little axe! Chop the logs, we will go to Tábor, and from Tábor to Venice, we will buy a few calves there: one bald, another with patches, the third barely carrying its tail; we will put them in the kitchen, where there is a good housekeeper, a stingy steward – and you, Janku, go away from us.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Little blacksmith! Forge the wheels, we’ll go to Krakow; and from Krakow to Budyně, there are nice hostesses; and from Budyně to Venice, we’ll buy a few calves there: one bald, another with patches, the third barely carrying its tail. The hostess, the farmer – and you, boy, stay away from us.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Hey, hey, Bartoni! What are you carrying on the horse? Half a bushel of rye from Mr. Vít. Mr. Vít is not at home, he went to Beroun for expensive spices, for those who have toothaches; for golden wreaths for our Mařenka. Hey, out of the castle!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: When May comes, we'll go to paradise; what will we do there? Break silver and gold; to whom will we give it? To the bailiff's daughter for those golden wreaths. – The wreath is already woven: 1) You, Vávra, step out of the circle. 1) Or: The wreath lies under the threshold, smeared with curd: you step out of the circle.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: A stingray ran around the mill; it lost a wreath, a German found it. I don’t want a German, I want a young man; the young man is handsome, worth three Czechs. Hupky, hupky into the soup, and out of the soup!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The stork is flying over the field, the hen catches up to him: Where are you going, stork, where are you flying? "To the golden chamber." What will you do there? "Breaking silver and gold." "Where will you give it?" "To the miller’s daughter for the golden wreaths." – The miller’s daughter was looking out the window, dropped the wreath, a German found it. "I don’t want a German: I want a young man, a young man is handsome, worth three Czechs."

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The peacock sits under the oak tree, playing cards with the pigeon, the little dove cries because he ate her cakes.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The peacock sits under the oak, playing cards with the pigeon: don't play, don't play, pigeon! Your mother will be here soon; she will bring you a nice wreath, you will become a young man.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The peacock sits under the oak with a beautiful white dove: little dove, lay an egg! When I go to the hill, I will buy you a little cimbalom: for the cimbalom: tydli tydli! for the violin: fidli, fídli! and for the bass: rum – dzum! and for the drums: drum – bum! 24. The stork sits under the oak, playing cards with the dove: and you, little dove! lend me some money for an egg. When I go to paradise, I will buy you beads; when I come back from paradise, I will bring you a king.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: A hare ran around the fence, tearing his new shoe; the fox was sewing it for him, the squirrel was mocking him. "Why are you mocking me? 1) when you don’t have a piece of pants!" 1) Or: There’s no need to mock, but I know how to sew. Four stitches, the loop is out!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The fox is running to Tábor, carrying a sack of ginger; run, little hare, run after her, take that spice away from her. The hedgehog is hurrying after her, to tear open the sack.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The wolf is sitting in the ravine, his ears got wet; he climbed to the top to dry them.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: The ground squirrel mows the fodder, the fox rakes, the hare loads the cart, the mouse tiptoes. The mosquito is putting on shoes, saying he will get married; the fly is pricking up its ears, saying it will go to the wedding. You unfortunate monster! 1) Don’t come to my wedding: I have a sharp sword at home, I would stab you with it. 1) Or: And you earthly monster! you are not equal to me: you will not go to my wedding, you will stay at home!

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: A hedgehog climbs under the boundary, afraid of ice; godmother fox said, "I will lead you across." "No need to lead me, I can walk, I can cross myself."

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: A sparrow is sitting on the church like a young man; a crow flew to him and took his wreath. "Oh, you dear crow! You have black legs." "And you, dear sparrow! you play cards very badly; when I fly over the Vltava, I will wash my legs: you will fly into debt, you will lose your cloak."

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: A crow flew in, sat on the hill; a second flew in and gave it a tap. The crow said to the crow: Let's buy a sled, we will ride from Prague to Libáně. Also: to the barn to mom, or: in the barn on the straw.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: Sewing shoes. The child twirls the finger of one hand around the finger of the other hand, like a little mill, while singing: Sewing shoes for work; I have no bread or cheese, the cat ate it all – shh! At the sound of "shh," the child stretches out both hands, as if a shoemaker is pulling out a nail.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: On the sausage, also called "on the blood sausage" and "on the ink." The boys stand in a tight circle, one closely behind the other, facing inward, each with their hands crossed behind their backs. None of them may look around. One walks around them, holding a cloth twisted tightly like a blood sausage or sausage, and says: "Ink sausage stuffed with groats: to whom I want myself, to them I will give it."

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: I carry, I carry, I won’t say for whom I lay this down: for Mr. Papše; the Pope climbs up, pulls the woman by the leg. And you, woman! get up, give us breakfast, greasy buns, greasy peas – the shepherd grazes on the tracks.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: On Crafts. The players count off two among themselves as journeymen. The others are masters and choose one of themselves as the senior master who speaks for them. The journeymen confer behind the door or apart in some other way about a craft or occupation they want to practice, and then, coming before the masters, they say: We poor wanderers, we wander day and night: masters, give us work! The senior master asks: What craft are you of? The journeymen: Our craft is... Then they name the first letter of their agreed-upon craft, for example, if they have agreed on cooperage, they say "B". Master: Show your craft. Now the journeymen begin silently to imitate their craft using different gestures. The masters deliberate together what the craft might be, and the senior master announces it on their behalf. When choosing a craft, the journeymen mainly try to prevent the masters from guessing it immediately. If the masters do not guess correctly by the third time, or if they deliberate too long, the journeymen say: Enough of our craft: give us the lesson! Then the craft is revealed to them. If the masters give in, they take the journeymen among themselves, and two others go out as journeymen again to agree on another craft. However, if the masters guess the craft, or refuse to give the lesson because the journeymen erred in some way, for example, by indicating their craft with incorrect gestures, then the journeymen must step back and come again with another craft.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: On the sloe bushes. Two girls stand one behind the other and, taking each other’s hands crossed—i.e., right hand to right hand and left to left—sing as follows: Come on, girls, to the sloe bushes, boys will come with us; we’ll go to see if the sloes are ripe: if not ripe, they are sour, we’ll go for them only on Sunday. At the same time, they hop forward on the first and second lines of the song, then hop backward on the third and fourth lines. Then, standing still, they let go, and the first girl, singing alone “not ripe,” claps the second girl’s right palm, to which the second responds “they are sour” by also clapping the first girl’s palm. Then they again take each other’s hands crossed, and singing together the last line: “we’ll go for them only on Sunday,” they turn halfway around so that the first girl stands where the second was, and the second where the first was. Then the second girl claps the first girl’s palm, singing again: “not ripe,” and the first responds: “they are sour”; after which, singing together “we’ll go for them only on Sunday,” they turn halfway around again. They repeat this game as long as they please. If there are more girls together, they form pairs, all in one row, and play and sing simultaneously.

label: DĚTSKÉ
translation: For the cornflower. Boys and girls join hands to form a circle, and one boy, chosen by counting, steps into it. The circle begins to turn, singing: The little bird hops down the street, picking wheat for itself. The boy in the circle responds with a song: And I stand in the circle, picking cornflower. Then the circle stops, and the boy selects a girl from it, dances three times with her in the circle; afterwards, he leaves her in the circle and blends back with the others. The circle begins to turn again, singing as before: The little bird hops down the street, etc., and the girl in the circle also responds as before: And I stand in the circle, etc., after which she again chooses a boy to dance with. And so the game continues as long as desired.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Christmas Carols. a) About the Birth of Christ. These carols are performed on St. Stephen's Day, i.e., December 26; children go from house to house, singing them at the door, after which the host or hostess gives them small gifts. Their content is either secular or religious. Secular. 1. Carol, carol, Stephen! what are you carrying in the jug? I am carrying, carrying a carol, I slipped on the ice with it, dogs gathered around me, they ate my carol; 1) what sad thing should I do? I must beg for another. Give me a carol, just don't laugh at me! They gave me a carol, yet they laughed at me. 1) gentlemen, give another, this one cover with mud.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Carolers! What do you want in the yard at midnight? – Christmas Eve. The good lady was kind, she didn’t let us stand, she called her daughter. The daughter jumped up, gave us carols for a groš wide, for a golden coin round.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Bukú, bukú, bukú! Open your hand, dear lady! Come out through the gate, give us carols, do not laugh at us. Whatever you give us, we will take: marzipans are for gentlemen, and potatoes for peasants. If you give us nothing, you will find it on the pots: the miller’s hen will not peck the wheat, all your pots will fall from the shelf. This carol is usually sung with a bukál. Bukál, or also bukač, is a word for a bulbous clay vessel, e.g. Čbán, having an opening covered with a bladder like a drum and a bristly string of two strands attached through the bladder. During the caroling, the boy takes the vessel between his knees and, after moistening his fingers with saliva, rubs them up and down alternately with his right and left hand over the bristles.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I am a little caroler, I have come to you for a little coin; give me the coin, just don't laugh at me: if you laugh at me, you must give me a kreuzer; but I am not going with a cart, whatever you give me, I will take: if you give me a piece of honey, 1) I will go even better. May the Lord grant you in the garden, in the field, in the sty little calves, in the stable little foals, under the bench little goslings. 1) If you don't give me a dog, I will strip you of your coat.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
