translation: I have come to you to tell you that today is caroling: whoever gives me something today, I will gladly keep it safe; whoever gives me nothing is from Hrubianovice: I will frown at him, growl like a bear, then I will leave.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I stand behind the door, I fear God; the dog growls at me, as if my godmother or godfather is slipping me a piece of bread into my pocket: whether it is soft or hard, it will be eaten anyway.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Oh divine mother! Quickly, a roll into the pocket: whether it is hard or soft, it will be eaten anyway.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I’m going caroling, carrying a bag; whoever fills it for me will be my friend; whoever gives me half, I’ll take a stick to them; whoever gives me a quarter, I’ll send death to them; whoever gives me nothing, I’ll send even more.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Our godfather František had doughnuts baked for us; and his child lies in velvet: I will be his godfather. – Those curds with dill, those cones with caraway, soaked in wine. And you, Jíra, whistle, cheerfully squeak for us!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Caroling, caroling, grandpa! Give me a treat to eat, if you don't give me a treat, I will tear your fur.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Grandpa, grandpa! Christmas carol: give me a mutt or two; if you don't give a mutt, I'll poke holes in your fur coat.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Caroling, caroling, little bear! Give us a little mongrel: if you don’t give us a little mongrel, we’ll drill a hole in your fur.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I’m coming to you for caroling: if you don’t give, I will immediately go to the pantry with a hook for pastries with poppy seeds.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The fox was running on the ice, she lost the honey key; whoever has it, let them give it, so the fox won’t be angry. The fox was running to the grove; chase her, gentlemen; I won’t chase her, I would miss the carol.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The meadow is green, 1) on that meadow is a grove: shepherds were grazing there, eating porridge made from milk. An angel flew to them and told them this: that Christ the Lord was born, so that everyone would know. They did not delay, immediately they hurried there, to Jesus in Bethlehem, they recognized Him as the Lord. And we little boys! we would gladly take: a little carol on our hand, if you would give us some. If you do not give, then beware: we will break pots and bowls, whatever you have in the dish. And little caps with that; 2) then we will run home: because you did not want to give carols to anyone. 1) String, string, string, the meadow is green, on that meadow are shepherds, eating porridge from a cup. 2) If you have them in the oven, we will break them anyway: with iron forks, with a wooden club.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: God bless this house! We sing, we know to whom: 1) to the little child, Christ Jesus, born today in Bethlehem. He gives out treats, apples, pears, and small gifts for our singing, for caroling: may the Lord God bless you! 1) We sing to the Lord God.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: We wish you happiness, health, and holy peace, first to the master of the house, then to your children! We come from afar, bringing you news of what happened to us in the town of Bethlehem. A little Son was born there; He sends me to you so that you may give Him carols – but I will give them to Him: He does not want to take bread, His mother fed Him; rather, you should send a coin or a ducat. I kindly ask you, excuse me, I have no time to stand here, I must still go to Bethlehem to rock the child; this child is wealthy and will repay you in heaven: so that after death you will rejoice with Him.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Our dear uncle, listen to us for a moment while we sing to you and joyfully tell a story. Jesus, the little child, a beautiful boy, was born in Bethlehem, born of the Virgin Mary. And in that common stable, He was laid on hay between two oxen by His beloved mother. He suffered hunger, thirst, and cold, all for our fault: that King of heavenly glory visited us, the earthly people. Amen, we will now sing and ask for carols: do not let us stand here, nor delay long. If you do not give – let us rejoice! you will find remembrance – let us be glad! in the new little coat and warm bed you will find a hole. A hole so small that a deer could jump through it here, being shot: so you will see, if you do not give, you will see harm.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Listen to me a little, my dear lady! And you too, dear father! What I tell you now, what I tell you new and unheard of so far. I heard in Bethlehem that there lies on the hay a beautiful little child, the noble Baby Jesus: Joseph and his mother are watching over him, an ox and a donkey are keeping him warm. The shepherds have come there, they wanted to play bagpipes; they were not in tune, not coordinated at all: everyone played their own note, stamping their foot to it.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I, a little one, come caroling: what I can sing about Christ, that He was born in Bethlehem in a stable, I want to proclaim. He is poor, He is naked, He suffers the cold: if I had what I wanted, I would give Him, I, a little pupil, a diaper and a swaddling cloth and a quilt. Give a little, a penny or a gold coin: after death you will surely be taken to heaven for it, I say here, among the saints. If you give nothing, don’t grumble and don’t blow on my singing: when I’m older, I will sing better, just wait!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Up, shepherds, Kuba, Matouš, you Klíma! "Why do you call to us, you won't let us sleep, Damína?" Are you sleeping so soundly that you don't hear the singing? a multitude of angels above us in this desert. Bárta, our brother, runs among us with diligence; hurries with a jump, nods his hat with joy. Oh Bárta, brother! tell us briefly, what are you doing? Are you running away from wolves or bears? "I run with great diligence and joy among you: that a king, our lord, was born and visited us." Bárta, you lie through your throat! How can you do that? "Enough, enough: the precious guest lies in Bethlehem in a stable on hay."

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Behold, what is this in Bethlehem? It seems there is something there: a beautiful maiden nods to me, I take to my heels, I say: Lukši! hop, hop, hop! let Prokop come here right away. I would burst with anger: where is Ferda, the little boy, to herd this big flock? maybe he's just dawdling somewhere! Let the flock stand still, it can sleep well: Bárta, take your flute, Filo, reach for the cimbalom, and you, Janku, fix the violin, Vaňku, greet your bass; where are the bagpipes, Lukáš? and where are the drums, Tomáš? We will go to Bethlehem, we will sing nicely: The child beautiful as a flower, came into the world in winter; play for him, trantará! so the little child can rest.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Hop, boys! and little girls too! Get up, hurry to the manger; the annual celebration is upon us. We will go to Bethlehem, and what we hear there, we will talk about: that a child has been born. Come eagerly, all the boys and little girls! You go first, Honzíček, you carry the cradle; when you reach the manger, bow to Jesus. Little Apolenka carried a sausage; the poor girl stumbled and went home crying; and Rozárka also carried two wafers; Zuzanka the girl carries a little dove; Maří brought diapers, Markýta brought blankets; Dora carries roses to the child in the cradle.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Baby Jesus was poor, devout; Saint Anne, his grandmother, the Virgin Mary, his mother. – I would cook porridge, but I have nothing to make it from; I would send for it, but I have no one; Joseph must go for it, nothing must be ashamed of for the child.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The little river flows, cold water. The maiden Maria washed herself in it. When she had washed, she walked along the shore; knelt on a stone, gave birth to a son. "Oh my dear mother! Do you want to eat fish?" – "Oh my dear child! How could you catch them? It has only been a quarter of an hour since you were born!"

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: We will go down there to the white courtyard: we will find the gentleman there, he is sitting at the table. Oh, dear sir! send us your lady, so that she may kindly give us the generous carols. She has given us a little rose from the heart, which blossomed for her in the green garden: but may the Most Holy Trinity repay her!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: God bless you for the caroling, Mr. Farmer! And you too, Mrs. Farmer! May everything prosper that you have in the field, in the garden, on the land: plums, apples, wheat, rye, spring crops. May every hen have chicks three times a year, may every duck have ducklings four times a year; the hen says: cluck, cluck! and the rooster: cock-a-doodle-doo! May your yard be full, and all of them hens!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Shepherds. What does it mean new in Bethlehem? We have not seen the sky so bright: the moon shines beautifully, it must mean something; the angels sing with a sweet voice, the shepherds blow their horns cheerfully; they have never done so at night: Glory to God in the highest! they always sing. Recitative. 1st shepherd: Gloria in excelsis Deo, that the child was born. 2nd shepherd: Where? 1st shepherd: In the town of Bethlehem. 2nd shepherd: How shall we go there, when we do not know the way?

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Angel. Here, here, here, shepherds! Come quickly to Bethlehem, come, come, don’t delay, lest the journey make you regret it! Shepherds. Why do you call to us so that you won’t even let us sleep? What is it, angel, sent from heaven, what are you telling us? Angel. He is born, laid in a manger, our savior, redeemer, right at midnight today. Recitative. 1st shepherd: But brother! What is this then! 2nd shepherd: Well brother! It is a wedding. 3rd shepherd: No, brother! It is Bethlehem, or the city of Jerusalem. 1st shepherd: My brother! 2nd and 3rd shepherd: Well! 1st shepherd: Remember. 2nd and 3rd shepherd: Well! 1st shepherd: What we used to say years ago – 2nd and 3rd shepherd: Well! Well! 1st shepherd: When we were tending those sheep – 2nd and 3rd shepherd: Well, well! 1st shepherd: That a Savior would be born to us – 2nd and 3rd shepherd: Well, well! 1st shepherd: Redeemer of the whole world. 2nd and 3rd shepherd: Well, well! 1st shepherd: But brothers! You keep saying “well, well!” but none of you says what you will give him: I will give him my crust from the pouch. 2nd shepherd: I will give him all my hills. 3rd shepherd: I will give him a fat ram: help me put it on these old shoulders!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Shepherds. So let's drive them, and quickly let's go to Bethlehem: you take the violin, I have the bagpipes; let's hurry to the manger, we'll play there. Song IV. (Tune 571.) Let's go together to Bethlehem, dujdaj, dujdaj, dujdaj dá! Little Jesus, little lord! I will rock you, little Jesus, little lord! I will rock you. Start, Kuba, on the bagpipes: dujdaj, dujdaj, dujdaj dá! Little Jesus, little lord, etc. And you, Janek, on the flute: dudli, tudli, dudli dá! Little Jesus, little lord, etc. And you, Mikša, on the violin: hudli, tydli, hudli dá! Little Jesus, little lord, etc. And you, Vávra, on the bass: rum, rum, ruma dá! Little Jesus, little lord, etc. Hút! hút! hút! etc. (One of the shepherds blows the horn 12 times.)

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: We have already had our caroling, no girls were with us; now you also go, dress nicely; now you also go to rock Baby Jesus. Ančička runs, trembling with cold, she was with her mother gathering firewood in the forest; she carries gifts for Baby Jesus, small apples in a basket; carrying gifts for Baby Jesus, she is trembling all over from the cold.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The glorious day has come to us, on which everyone should be joyful; let us rejoice, let us be merry in this new year. When I entered the room, I found the lady with the gentleman there – summer will pass and fade – I found the lady with the gentleman. The gentleman and the lady are sitting at the table, looking at each other – summer will pass and fade – alas, they are playing cards. Little gentleman! I feel sleepy, I will go make my bed – summer will pass and fade – I will go make my bed. When she made her bed, she went to lie down in it – summer will pass and fade – she immediately went. A pretty lady and a handsome gentleman, if the lady does not give a carol, the gentleman will give it himself – summer will pass and fade – the gentleman will give the carol himself. We wish you a new year, we give a son or daughter as a gift – summer will pass and fade – we wish you well. That we may come to you next year, and find you in good health once again – summer will pass and fade – find you in good health.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: A most glorious day has come to us, on which everyone is to be joyful, whoever has anywhere to begin, even if only for a whole week. We have nowhere to begin, we must go caroling to you, dear, esteemed gentlemen, may God grant you success! To your wife, your children, and your household: may the Lord bless you in your work. Give us a thaler or a ducat, we will remember you, for all time our group will praise you. If you lessen it for us, and give some gold: give it only willingly; we will gladly accept it from you as thanks, and we will buy wine for our coats, we will season it with spirits, and line it with bitters. We have long asked for this, that we might meet with you; united together, let us hold a celebration! We will be merry together, we will toast each other: wine, beer, and spirits for each other's health. Then we will part from you, and all go home again to our work, hardworking brewers. And you, boy, sweep, gather the grain into a pile! You know we like to drink, and do not waste. It was the last day of the year 1840. In the New Town of Prague, in a freight house, we sat down to dinner: the host, the brewer, an honest Old Czech, at the head, and the rest of us in two rows along the whole length of the hall up to the doors. Then a song sounded outside: “A most glorious day has come to us,” the doors opened and the brewing youth entered the room singing, each carrying something: the deputy elder a rod, the herd a sack, two water carriers a sprinkler and incense borrowed from the church, sprinkling and incensing, each brewer and maltster a shovel, and the boys brooms. Standing half-circle by the door they sang further. And when the words of the last stanza came: “And you, boy, sweep,” the host threw a hard thaler to the ground, the herd set the sack and the boy drove the thaler into it with his broom. Then the deputy elder wished the host and his whole family a “happy new year,” thanked them for the gift, and then, all bowing, they left again.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: January 25. If the wind blows on the day of the Conversion of St. Paul, men will be conscripted for military service that year. Whichever direction the wind blows on that day, merchants will come from that side to buy grain.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Today is Saint Matthias' Day: I carry a naked one on my body, (?) so that he grants us plenty of fruit to fullness. Or: Today is Saint Matthias' Day: wherever my voice spreads, let there be abundant fruit everywhere! May the Lord God grant us that we have something to pick: apples, pears, medlars, may the garden be full of them!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Today is Saint Matthias' Day: wherever that voice reaches, may there be plenty of grain! The first of these sayings is from the Jičín region, the second from the Chrudim region, the third from the Beroun region.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: March 1st. At midnight, when the first day of the month of March begins, girls, rising from their beds, say: March, dear March! Show me in a dream whom I will marry, so that I may watch over him.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The first day of March: please, let me see in my dreams only the one whom I am to have as my husband. Then she lies down again, hoping that her destined one will appear to her in a dream.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: We celebrate Carnival, we do not quarrel, together: why would we argue when we have met again? We have met in good spirits, we are glad to be together: before we part, we will toast to each other again.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Why then does the Lord God punish old maids for not having anyone to love them?

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Why then does the Lord God not love old maids? Why then does the Lord God not like old maids? To the young He gives husbands, to the old He cannot: why then does the Lord God not like old maids?

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Loggers, loggers! Let’s go raft. – "I haven’t finished rafting yet, I won’t go. I still have to raft the little path I made for Hančička."

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I was sorry for rafting, and I was sorry for digging: when I like nothing, rather than wandering the world. I was sorry for rafting, and I was sorry for digging: when I like nothing, rather than being a wanderer in the world.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Ugly Wednesday. On Ugly Wednesday, I do not like to spin, I like to sleep, and whatever is good, all with it.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: March 12. On Saint Gregory's Day, children chant: Today is Saint Gregory's day, a swallow flew across the sea: a foolish peasant who does not plow!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Today we celebrate the feast of the great teacher, Saint Gregory; let us rejoice in it. Whoever loves their children, guide them to good, and send your sons to school, let them learn this: First, know God, their Creator, and teach them faith in Christ, His Son. If you do not have a pupil, give us a note: for one groschen, for two, for three—you will surely not go without. You will have your reward in the heavenly paradise, where Saint Gregory dwells, praising the Lord God. If you give us nothing, beware: we will break all the pots you have on the shelf and smash the pans you have on the stove. From an older manuscript. Students used to sing this song, going from house to house with their teacher collecting gifts, until the establishment of German schools under Emperor Joseph II, after which this practice ceased. Meanwhile, they also often played “master” and “soldiers,” reciting in a recitative manner the following and other similar verses:

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Master. I. Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: One? – “One Lord our God, who dwells in heaven.” Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Two? – “Two tablets of Moses, one Lord our God, who dwells above us.” Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Three? – “Three holy patriarchs, two tablets of Moses,” etc. Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Four? – “Four evangelists, three patriarchs,” etc. Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Five? – “Five wounds of Jesus, four evangelists,” etc. Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Six? – “Six stone jars filled with wine, five wounds of Jesus,” etc. Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Seven? – “Seven gifts of the Holy Spirit, six stone jars,” etc. Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Eight? – “The eight beatitudes, seven gifts of the Holy Spirit,” etc. Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Nine? – “Nine choirs of angels, the eight beatitudes,” etc. Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Ten? – “The Ten Commandments, nine choirs of angels,” etc. Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Eleven? – “Eleven martyred virgins, buried on the Rhine in Cologne, the Ten Commandments,” etc. Master, learned master, trained in all schools! Tell me, what is this: Twelve? – “Twelve apostles at God’s table, eleven martyred virgins buried on the Rhine in Cologne, the Ten Commandments, nine choirs of angels, the eight beatitudes, the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit, six stone jars filled with wine, five wounds of Jesus, four evangelists, three holy patriarchs, two tablets of Moses, one Lord our God, 1) who dwells in heaven.” From Prague. 1) Also: one Lord God in heaven, who reigns over us all.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: II. Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: One? – "Eagle bird, that is: a machine, my dear, with a white bed, we will go lie down on it, and our little innkeeper will pour drinks." Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Two? – "Two calves and a cow, Andulička was grazing them there. Eagle bird, that is: a machine, my dear, with a white bed, we will go lie down on it, and that handsome little innkeeper will pour drinks." Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Three? – "Three horses went, two calves and a cow, Andulička was grazing them there," etc. Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Four? – "Four does went, and three horses, two calves and a cow," etc. Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Five? – "Five stallions, four does went and three horses," etc. Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Six? – "Six shoemakers, five stallions, four does went," etc. Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Seven? – "Seven roasted larks, six shoemakers, five stallions," etc. Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Eight? – "Eight unfaithful maidens, seven roasted larks, six shoemakers," etc. Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Nine? – "Nine straw monasteries, wonderfully built; eight unfaithful maidens," etc. Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Ten? – "Ten geese, they must go into the oven; nine straw monasteries," etc. Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Eleven? – "Eleven swans, they will be to our liking; ten geese," etc. Master of masters, trained in all schools! Tell us, what is this: Twelve? – "Twelve apostles sit around the table. Eleven swans, they will be to our liking. Ten geese, they must go into the oven. Nine straw monasteries, wonderfully built. Eight unfaithful maidens. Seven roasted larks. Six shoemakers. Five stallions. Four does went, and three horses. Two calves and a cow, Andulička was grazing them there. Eagle bird, that is: a machine, my dear, with a white bed, we will go lie down on it, and the golden little innkeeper will pour drinks."

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Gregory's army. A troublemaker. I am the troublemaker of that regiment; if anyone here causes any trouble, I have the will to punish them and send them away from the army.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Standard-bearer. An infallible truth, know this, brought to me by my brother: the banner of Christ Jesus, that is the gate of the heavenly kingdom; whoever wants to enter it must follow Jesus Christ.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Little soldiers. I am a little pupil, I sing like a little bird, I like to go to church, I pray to God.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I have a little bird in the oak tree, I'll send Kuba after it: Kuba has a sharp beak, he will peck it out of the oak for me.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: When I was crossing the Prague bridge, I found a silver coin: I ate, I drank, I had enough of everything.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I am a guy, a lawyer, I have a coat worth three hundred: I'll hang it on a peg, it’s not worth a throne.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I, a cadet, am standing on guard: if a bullet comes my way, I will fall like a rotten leaf.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I have a little bird in the forest, it brings me eggs; if God grants health, I will choose a young one. Advice, advice, our advice – if I had a pot of porridge, I would sit with her under the bench, I wouldn’t get in the way in the room. And why is your girl so foolish that she doesn't blow the porridge for me here: when I blow it, she will look at me; when I eat it, she will want me.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Bandur. I am Bandur from Swabian land, accept me among you, I will teach you to conjugate, to take everything in order: first we will collect chickens, then various eggs; when we come to the pub, we will have a good feast.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Major. I am a major from the army, I have to listen to friends, turning fools into wise men, deciding who should get what: but when I remember, I will take a stick in my hand; when I start shouting: retreat! everyone will shout: ouch!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Lush flower, full hive.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Liverleaf (hepatica), which blooms blue early in the spring, already in the month of March, under forest trees and bushes. Whoever first sees it in the spring picks it with a bare hand, preferably wrapped in a white handkerchief, and rubs their eyes three times with its flower, while saying three times after each rub: Liverleaf, liverleaf, liverleaf, your eyes do not pain, but my eyes do hurt; however, they will heal by morning. Sr. Suš. 735. In Moravia, this herb is popularly called sisí, sisíčko and is used for the same purpose. Treating cracked flutes.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: A log lies, a log lies, on that log a crow: as soon as that crow takes flight, the whistle will come off.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Knock yourself out, little whistle! If you don’t knock yourself out, I will complain about you to the emperor, he will give you a blow for stealing my cow: the cow was worth a thaler, and you gave me nothing for it. Bang! bang! bang! bang! And you, Janko, take off the plant!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Knock yourself out, goat’s lip, I’ll give you a bowl full of peas; if you don’t knock yourself out, I’ll complain about you to the emperor, the leaky jug. Or rather: to the emperor, he’ll strike you, and then you’ll fly off to the iron jug. Or also: to the emperor, he’ll strike you until you spin all the way around, pouring a stein of beer: You drink, you drink, gentlemen! and I will be with you. Enough, old man, enough! it’s bare bone already.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The gentleman is going from the hills, carrying cucumbers; one fell, the lady grabbed it; he chased after her until he caught up; he caught her by the gate and tore her coat: Don’t tear it, don’t tear it, you didn’t sew it for me; a seamstress sewed it for me, she sewed it with a needle, and hammered it with a mallet, tap, tap, tap, tap, tap!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The gentleman rides from the hilltop, tore his lip; the lady sewed it for him, the goat mocked them. The gentleman took the goat by the chin and threw it into the ford; the goat is not in the ford, it went around the mill, lost a wreath there, who found it? – A German. I don’t want a German, I want a young man; the young man is handsome, worth five Czech coins. Enough, enough, enough! it’s bare bones now. Or: The goat walked around the mill, stole a piece of cheese there, lost a wreath, a German found it. I don’t want a German etc. Cf. children’s counting rhymes, p. 29, no. 19 and 20.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Forge, forge, little smith! Shoe my little foot, I'll give you a grosh for it, another grosh. When I go to Police, I'll buy four pots there: in one will be cream, in the second will be milk, in the third will be buttermilk, in the fourth whey. Patka, patka until Friday.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: A pear tree stands in the wide field, those who are beasts are afraid; I am a beast, I am not afraid, I will take a saber and stand up. Play for me, blow for me 1) march up, so I have courage for the war, 1) Or: Play for me, blow for me, on the fiddle tydlidum! on the drum bum, bum, bum! March, march, march! as long as I have courage.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: When I was a little boy, I used to play the violin; my strings broke, my bow snapped. I came to a peasant woman, she was baking buns; I asked her for a bun, she said they were hot. She sent me to another peasant woman, she was cooking groats; I asked her for a little, she gave me all of it.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The shoemaker is sitting on the road, sewing shoes for the bride, the bride is happy that she will wear the shoes. The shoes fell from the clogs, killed the lad; they did not let him ring the bell – chasing the old woman around the handrail, until the woman fell. Enough, old man, enough! it's just skin and bones now.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Houdy, houdy! I go into the kennel, the kennel is empty; I go into the second, I go into the third, she is a beautiful maiden.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The old woman jumped over the pond, the chimney sweep ran after her: Wait, cursed woman, that you ate the kittens! Oh joy! Oh joy! now there will be plenty of whistling.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: A hare was running around the fence, tearing his new shoe there; the fox was sewing it for him, the squirrel was mocking him. Be quiet, squirrel, be quiet! I'll stab you with a knife! I'll stab you with a stiletto, you'll whistle like a little bird; I'll stab you with a dagger, I'll break your little soul.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: What’s wrong, little boy, what were you doing that you dirtied your shirt? "Two Turks with red flags were chasing me; they drove me up a little hill and bought me a little drum: drum, storm, out from the stick to the bone!" 1) Or: Two Turks with red flags were chasing me; they chased me to the hill and gave me a little drum. On the drum: boom, boom, boom! on the violin: tidy, tidy! on the bass: rarara! Let our lady not worry.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Dead Sunday. On that day, the girls carry out death and bring in the new summer. They dress a straw figure in women's clothing and decorate it with ribbons and other adornments, in the shape of a female figure, which they then either take under their arm and lead, or place on a pole and carry through the village, where they undress it and throw the bare straw figure either into the water or into a ditch. Having thus drowned death, they then go through the village from house to house with a maypole or with summer, that is, with a fir tree decorated with colorful ribbons and painted or gilded ornaments, singing and receiving gifts in return. 1. Morana! Morana! Where did you put the keys? I made them, I made them for Saint George, so he would open the doors to paradise for us. I made them, I made them for Saint John, so he would open the gates to heaven for us.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Saint George rises, unlocking the earth so that the grass grows, the green grass, the red rose, the blue violet.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Oh Mary! Oh Mary! Where have you been so long? At the well, at the small spring I was washing my hands, with a little lock, with a little lock I was locking myself, with a little leaf, with a little leaf I was unlocking myself. From an older manuscript.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Now they also begin like this: Death Mother! Easter Mother! Where have you been so long? By the spring, by the spring, washing my hands and feet, etc.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Linden tree, new linden tree! What have you brought us? Many good things, beautiful flowers: blue, white, and red.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: You know, girls, the wreath that has a young man; yet there are enough flowers until the meadow sparkles; yet there is enough foliage until the mountain turns green.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Saint George is coming to us, carrying a wreath for us, for us, for us, for us, for the girls of Rovinsko. Also: Saint John is coming to us, carrying a wreath for us, for us, for us, for us, for the girls of Semily.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: We carry death from the village, new sorrow to the village: be joyful, gentlemen, that we bring you sorrow with red eggs, with yellow Easter breads. What kind of Easter bread is it without spices and without eggs? It also begins like this: We have brought you death, brought new sorrow; be joyful, gentlemen, etc. Or: We bring sorrow, be joyful, gentlemen, etc. Or: Death flows on the water, new sorrow comes with red eggs, etc. Also: Death floats on the water, new summer comes to us with red eggs, etc. The rhymes "nesemy a přichodie" instead of the current "neseme a přichází" are unmistakable evidence that this song originates from ancient, pre-Hussite times, when such forms were still customary in our language.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Be joyful, gentlemen, on this Mortuary Sunday: we have taken death away from you, brought new sorrow. Welcome, gentle sorrow, green grain!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Feasts pass, flowers pass, sorrow follows behind; may the Lord grant grass up to the knees!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: God grant you a good day, fine lady and gentleman! Also with the groomsmen, flower bearers, with red eggs, with yellow mazanci. What kind of mazance is it without spices, without eggs?

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: And you, Saint Markyta! watch over our rye, and all the grain that God grants us.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Saint Margaret! Give us good grain for wheat and rye, so that we have something to live on and to bring into the barn. Also: let the ears be large, so that we may come again after a year.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Saint Peter thunders, will give us wine, so that we may drink and praise the Lord God; may dear God grant it! From an older manuscript. Now also like this: Saint Peter of Rome! give us a bottle of wine, so that we may drink and praise the Lord God.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Saint Peter is resting on the green hill. Markyta is pleasuring him, she ran away from the horses; he caught her by the gate, tore her coat. Why are you tearing it for me? You didn’t sew it for me: Barborka, who looks out from the window, sewed it for me.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: A nice lady stands, walks in a white skirt, she reaches into it, takes out a white coin, and gives it to us. From an older manuscript.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Spin around, basket! Give us a penny; break apart, pouch! Give us a coin for the green bowl!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Shake the wardrobe so the coins fall out; get moving, Míška! Give us a little money, a small one, a small one, a little red egg.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: When we go out of the house, may God give you a good day, a sweet cake for the lady, a folded cake for the gentleman.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I wish you a good day when we can go outside from home, today and for the whole week!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If you give us nothing, you will receive a bad reward: pots will jump off the shelf at you, like the miller's hens when they have gorged on the lord's wheat.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Palm Sunday. From blessed willow, bladdernut, or yew twigs, farmers make three crosses and after lunch, each carries them to their own field where they stick them in the ground, saying: God the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, bless my field from thistles!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Ugly Wednesday. As soon as the Passion ends, the boys run out of the church and, clapping and neighing, chase Judas through the village, after which they receive gifts from the more well-to-do residents. While chasing, they loudly call out like this: Judas! What have you done that you betrayed Lord Christ to the Jews? For that, you must be in hell, dwelling with the devil and the demon. From an older manuscript.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Green Thursday. On that day, fleas are driven out of the house; by whipping the bed and the floor up to the door with a hazel rod blessed by the priest, they say: Out, boneless guests!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Good Friday. Before sunrise, kneeling in the gardens under the fruit trees, they say: I pray to you, green tree! May the Lord God reward you well!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: They also go out before sunrise under the open sky, into the garden, to the field, etc., and kneeling on their bare knees on the grass, they say this: Morning, morning, early morning, before the sun rose, the Jews seized Lord Jesus. He was trembling, they asked him: Sir, do you have chills? "I do not, nor will I have, for whoever will remember my death."

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: On Good Friday, Lord Christ had a marriage. Lord Christ carries His cross until He trembles all over under it. Pilate asks Him: "Lord! Are You cold?" Lord Jesus answers: "I am not cold, nor will I be." Whoever says these words will not have chills.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Holy Saturday. At midnight from Good Friday to Saturday, farmers run through the gardens and tie strips of cloth around the trees, calling out: Put on your shoes, trees, there will be frost tomorrow; if you do not put on your shoes, we will cut you down.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: It also begins like this: The first throws into Provoda! red eggs; if you don't give red, at least give white, etc.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Red eggs! Red eggs! If you don’t give red, give white, the hen will lay others for you by the stove in the corner on a willow twig. The twig is a switch, sitting on it is a golden rooster.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The twig bends, the hen crows: catch that hen for me, we will buy a cap with it: that cap for the master, he will give a cow for it; that cow for the countess, she will give wreaths for it.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The woman goes from the meadows, carrying a bowl of flour; if it is a little for her, she will add an egg for us.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: O only heart! The time has come: give me an egg, or I will come again; red or white, but the hen will lay another for you, by the stove in the corner on a golden twig, a nice white one.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The hen runs around the yard, the egg rolls in the chamber; give her a drink, so she doesn't fly away; give her seeds, she will lay three eggs for you in a day.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The hen runs around the yard, the egg rolls in the pantry: the hen clucks! the egg cracks! you have to give it to me, madam.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The hen is brooding, the egg is rolling: cuckoo! cuckoo! She laid fifty of them on a little heap in the nettles, the godfather pupil took them from me! pupil! pupil!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The miller's hen ate some wheat, flew among the pots, and knocked them off the shelves.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I am a little caroler, I am coming to you, if you give me a few eggs, I will gladly take them: if you don’t have red ones, give me white ones, but the hen will lay others for them: on the stove in the corner, on a green twig; before the twig turns, let the mug turn. You drink, gentlemen, drink, just don’t get drunk and toast with us too: when I become a young man, I will toast you even with a wreath.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: God grant happiness to this house, we are coming to you for a blessing. Our golden, beautiful maiden! The hens have long been bringing you nice white eggs; give an Easter egg, the hen will lay another. If you give a piece of braided cake, you will get a young man; if you give a piece of roast, we will go home happily; if you give us nothing, then this year you will not marry. Sr. Krolm. sv. 11, p. 582.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I am a little caroler, auntie! I came for a red egg, for a red egg, for a white cake: how dear is this caroler to you? Don’t be angry with me, uncle! Take a coin out of your pocket, give it to the caroler, don’t laugh at him, he will thank you warmly for it. God bless you in the garden, in the field, and at home; you will have something to drive out of the yard: foals from the stable and calves from the barn, lambs from the smallest pen.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Binovačka is fidgeting so that Liduška won't believe me: we'll rush to the door, and from the door straight into the hall, and from the hall into the kitchen, and there we'll talk about something.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: My dear madam! The egg is staring at me by itself, I ask that it be given to me: whether it is white or speckled, it may even be pigeon-colored, I only ask that it be round.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Oh my dear lady! An egg on my forehead by itself, I believe it will be given to me; I would ask my uncle to give me an egg: he does not consider the hens.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Moo, moo! The cows are coming, carrying milk underwater. Where is our barren cow? By the church of God. The church is collapsing, the barn is burning.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Moo moo! The cows are coming, carrying milk with cream, and our heifer by the little church; the little church is burning, the barn is collapsing.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Jump, maiden, into the water, give the lord some strawberries. Why should I jump there, having aproned my skirt? Where would I dry it? By the stove in the corner on a green twig. The twig will turn, the beer tankard will be filled: You drink, diligence, gentlemen! and I will be with you.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Jump, maiden, into the water for the red strawberries. Why would I jump in there, to wet my skirt? Where would I dry it? At the old lady’s, by the green frog. Or rather: At Mr. Vít’s. Vít is not home, he went to Beroun for four sheep, a fifth ram, a white ram with golden horns. Also: for a sixth ram, we ate him by morning.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Moo, moo! The cows are coming, carrying milk with cream: bull, bull! Give some tobacco to the old woman for her pipe.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: He has just gotten up at this moment – the butcher killed a calf, and the butcher's wife a goose, she gave us a piece; and grandmother a rooster, she put it in the corner; and the lad a goat, he threw it into the cart.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation:
Héla. Two-part singing. Both this first line and every following one expands.  
1st choir: Héla, Héla! Our lady, žumber, žumber gives; Héla, Héla! Our lady, žumber, žumber, šveděr, šveděr, tarun, tandará.  
2nd choir: What did our lady want from us?  
1st choir: She sent us for an axe.  
2nd choir: What will we do with that axe?  
1st choir: We will chop wood with it.  
2nd choir: What will we do with that wood?  
1st choir: We will make violins from it.  
2nd choir: What will we do with the violins?  
1st choir: We will play them for the maidens.  
2nd choir: What will the maidens give us for that?  
Both choirs: With the first, we will dance; the second will make the bed; the third, we will love.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: A pear tree stands in the valley – hey! it is joyful! 1) On it are red pears – hey! hey! crimson! 1) They are green on one side and red on the other. Climb up, Jeníček! Pick a few. Jeníček went, picked some, and sent the gift to his maiden. "Here, girl, a little pear, give me your hand for it."

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Hops. There is a pole standing in the middle of the yard – praise to you, great God, God in heaven! 1) On that pole, hops grow; once the hops are picked, we will brew beer from them. Who will drink that beer? Vašíček will drink it when he has a wedding. Where will we go for his bride? Beyond the forests, beyond the mountains, to the Vitoch family’s room. In that room, a white bed, on that bed lies a maiden, holding a golden ring in her hand. Who will wear that ring? Vašíček will wear it after he has the wedding.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The royal maiden. Héla! Héla! helilalou! In that royal court, in that golden chamber, a bed stands; on that golden bed lies a maiden, on that maiden a white hand, on that hand a golden ring, through that ring grass is growing: whoever will eat that grass will have that maiden. Héla! Héla! helilalou!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Helička was not proud, not proud, yet she came to our feast, came: Heličko! Heličko! Smooth your golden cheek, hop on your bare little foot, choose a bridesmaid from the circle, from the rose a blossom, from the rose a blossom, the one that would suit you best. Now the circle will remain standing, Helička will take one bridesmaid from it and dance three times with her in the circle, during which the other bridesmaids, standing, sing as follows: Over mountains, over valleys, over waters, over forests, hey little ball, you golden quince! Let go of that bridesmaid, stay in the circle. Then Helička leads the bridesmaid back to her previous place in the circle, stands again in the middle, and the bridesmaids, beginning to turn around her again, sing as at the beginning: Helička was not proud, etc. Now Helička takes the second girl in the circle to dance, and then the third, and so on one after another, until she has replaced them all, which ends the game. In practice, the children deviate from this order of the game by having Helička, after dancing with a bridesmaid, leave her in her place in the circle and herself, becoming a bridesmaid, turn with the others around the new Helička; in this way, everyone takes turns. However, this is against the rule of the song itself, which explicitly commands Helička to let go of the bridesmaid and remain in the circle herself.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: (Tune 425.) She smoothed her little head to shine in the sun: Helička, Helička! Red and white little rose! She washed her little cheeks with dew from the wheat furrow: Helička, Helička! Red and white little rose! She straightened her little skirt, tied her little sash: Helička, Helička! Red and white little rose! She put on her little shoes on those white little feet: Helička, Helička! Red and white little rose! She supported her hips, and chose a blue-eyed girl: Helička, Helička! Red and white little rose! Then the circle will stop, Helička will take one girl from it and dance with her in the circle, while the others sing: Helička dances in the circle, loves handsome young men: Helička, Helička! Red and white little rose! Then, in the next game, the chosen companion will be Helička, and she will be a companion in her place.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: At Hřímbab, also called "at the Virgin Mary," or "at Lady Rose or Rosy." I. This game is most often played by girls alone. One is Hřímbaba (Mary, Rose) and one is the chodačka (the seeker). Hřímbaba sits on the ground; in front of her, between her knees, sits the second girl, in front of the second the third, and so on, all the others in a straight line. The chodačka walks around them saying: "I am looking, I am looking for a leaf on a sore little finger"; then, coming to the first girl in line, asks: "Where is Hřímbaba?" The answer: "Next to me." The chodačka goes to the second girl and again asks: "Where is Hřímbaba?" She again says: "Next to me." And so on, all send her "next to me," until she reaches the last girl (Hřímbaba).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Where is Hřímbaba? "I am."

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Please, Hřímbabo, let me pick some herbs for a great pain: I went through the black forest, a big dog met me and bit off a piece of my finger! Then she points with her right hand at her left hand, showing which piece. Hřímbaba asks: "Which herb do you want?" The girl names, for example, plantain. "Pick it from the edge." Where should I take it from? Hřímbaba says, for example, "from the hand" or "from the skirt," "from the ear," etc. Then the girl takes the first girl in line by the hand or as Hřímbaba said and leads her aside. Then she goes again as at the beginning: I'm looking, I'm looking for a little leaf, etc.; but when she returns to Hřímbaba and she asks her: "Which herb do you want?" now the girl names another one, for example, yarrow; and in the next round different ones again, for example, balm, sage, marigold, fat man, mint, basil, etc., as she likes, until all the girls take those herbs and finally even Hřímbaba herself is led away, being called for as if for an herb; after which they start hopping on one leg, singing like this:

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: My leg no longer hurts from that Hřímba's spice; if my leg did hurt, I wouldn't even want to dance. Then Hřímbaba sits on some elevated place, for example on a stone, on a block, on a boundary, etc., the dancer ties a scarf over the eyes of one of the girls, who were the first herbs, and leading her around Hřímbaba, says:

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I lead, I lead the angel around the church: little angel, kneel, fold your legs and hands, do not show your teeth.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: II. In many places, this game is played somewhat differently. The seeker coming in asks the first girl in line: Where is Mrs. Růžová? The answer: "Next door," and so on, until she reaches Mrs. Růžová, who responds: What do you want? I am here. Seeker: I want the golden key from heaven. Růžová: I dropped my keys into the well, pick one out. Seeker: Which one should I pick? Růžová: The one on the edge. Seeker: How should I take it? Růžová says, for example, "By the hand," or otherwise as she chooses. Then the seeker takes the first girl in line the way Růžová instructed, and leading her into the circle, says:

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I am leading, leading the little angel to the Lord's little church: little angel, kneel, fold your hands and feet, but do not bare your teeth.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The little angel kneels before Mrs. Růžová, and she begins to question him: Where did you go? Girl: Through the alley (the grove). Růžová: Whom did you meet? Girl: An old grandmother (the Lord God). Růžová: What did she eat? (What did he eat?) Girl: A roll. Růžová: Did she give you a piece? Girl: She did. Růžová: What did you say? Girl: God bless you. Růžová: What did she say to you? Girl: May God give it to you. Meanwhile, the choirmaster suggests all sorts of funny answers to the girl to make her laugh; if the girl cannot be confused by this, Mrs. Růžová finally asks: Whom will you choose: the Sun or the Moon? Thus, two sides are formed: the "sunflower" side, led by Mrs. Růžová, and the "moon" side, which has the choirmaster; these sides then fight against each other again.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: III. Another variant: Walker: Where is Maria? Answer: Under the arcade. The last to answer: I am Maria. Walker: Maria, Maria! Give me a piece of the root. Maria: Break it off yourself. Walker: Where should I take it from? Maria: From the hair (from the nose, etc.).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Then the procession leader guiding the little girl to the circle says: I lead, I lead the little angel, around the white little church, etc.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: When the little girl kneels, Maria asks: What did you eat? Little girl: Peas. Maria: Who gave them to you? Little girl: The Lord God. Maria: Where did He get them? Little girl: Where He wanted. Maria: What did you drink? Little girl: Wine. Maria: Who gave it to you? Little girl: The Lord God. Maria: Where did He get it? Little girl: Where He wanted. Maria: Laugh at me. The little girl must say: "I don't feel like it." If she laughs, she is a devil; otherwise, an angel. Finally, Maria says: Jump! Then the little girl jumps and goes either to heaven or to hell. Finally, a battle follows between angels and devils.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation:
Žalman. The same number of boys and girls. The boys stand in a row next to each other, holding hands, and about six steps away opposite them, the girls also stand in a row next to each other, holding hands. Each verse of the following song consists of eight measures, each line being two measures. During the first two lines, the row of boys or girls who are singing the verse steps forward one step closer to the opposite row with each measure; during the third and fourth lines, they step back about four steps. 

Boys: Jan is riding, Jan is riding, Žalman is riding, our entire knighthood is riding.  
Girls: What does Jan ask for, what does Žalman ask for, what does your entire knighthood ask for?  
Boys: For a beautiful maiden, like a bright star, our entire knighthood is riding for her.  
Girls: We will not give her to you, we will keep her, let your entire knighthood ride away.  

This repeats three times: the second time without any changes, but the third time they change the last verse as follows:  
Girls: Now we will give her to you, we have golden wreaths, take her, your entire knighthood.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation:
One girl, from the end of her row, steps into the line of boys between the first and second boy at the end, and then the boys, together with that girl, play the game again with the second girl in the same way as they did with the one who was among them. Then the second girl at the end of the girls' row moves into the boys' row, standing now between the second and third boy, and in the next game the third girl at the end stands between the third and fourth boy, and so on, until even the last girl joins the boys' row at the end. When, in this manner, from both rows that were at the beginning of the game, only one row remains, it is formed into a circle by joining its two ends, and all hold hands around this circle, beginning to spin and sing:  
"Sil Petr proso i pšenici, vyorali jsme tam křepelici; křepelici máme, křepela nemáme: mnoho nás, mnoho nás, jděte holky/chlapci pryč od nás."  
Now the circle breaks apart again into two sides, boys on one side and girls on the other, each side lines up, and they can start the game again. A similar game is played in Moravia during spinning gatherings.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: To heaven and to hell. This game is usually played only by boys. On the village green or on paths, they make twelve holes, each about one or two steps apart; the last one is the largest and called heaven. These twelve holes are established with the following rhyme of twelve syllables: Give, mother, bread to the children, or we will release the bull from the barn, and it will gore you. In some regions, especially in Klatovy, they have a different rhyme with only nine syllables, and therefore they make only nine holes, namely:

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Heaven, hell, hey! Just give me a piece of bread, old woman. Then the players determine how, in the game, one followed the other in this way: each player throws their closing dart from the first hole to the last (towards heaven); the player whose closing dart lands closest to heaven or in heaven is the first thrower, and the others follow in the same order as their knives lie from heaven onwards; the knife that lies farthest away is the last thrower.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Then the first thrower takes his throwing knife and tosses it into the air, calling out: "Saint Peter! Lend me the keys to heaven." Now they watch how the throwing knife falls: if it lands face up, with the expression on the blade visible, the thrower advances from the first hole to the second and places his knife there; but if his knife falls face down, with the expression on the blade underneath, the player’s knife remains stuck in the first hole until it is his turn to throw again. Meanwhile, the second boy throws, then the third, and so on, until everyone has taken their turn, each calling out like the first: "Saint Peter! Lend me the keys to heaven;" after which they either advance or remain stuck. Then everyone throws again for the second time, the third, and so forth, always in the same order and manner as the first time, until on the last throw some players, or at least one of them, reach heaven with their knives. Those who fail to reach heaven and remain in hell must compensate the ones in heaven either with something or by undertaking an agreed penalty.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Every old grandmother is happier in spring: she takes her cane, sits in the sunshine; she thinks: "If only I were younger, oh, how much I would prefer that!"

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: In May in spring, every old grandmother pushes herself toward the sun, singing a little song: "If I were younger, I would be happier."

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: May. When May comes, we will have paradise. Also: we will go to the grove; or: we will drive the goats to the grove, old women behind the stove, the young ones right behind them.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Cold May, paradise in the barn.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If it does not rain in May, then June will make up for it.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Dry March, wet May, the barn will be like paradise.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: When thunder sounds in May, grab a stone or a tree (and shake it to become strong).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The swarm that swarms in May is worth a full wagon of hay; June swarming is not worth ringing the bell.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: May 1st. On the first of May, we go to the grove. On that day, after midnight, young men go to the grove to get May trees, specifically birches, and where these are not available, they are content with firs, rowans, and other small trees. After cutting down a large May tree, they neatly trim it from base to crown and then plant it in the ground in the middle of the village square. Additionally, every young unmarried girl receives her own May pole, which her beloved places either in front of her window or in the yard, and if she does not yet have a boyfriend, another young man does it. All the May poles in the village must be erected before dawn.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Maypole under the windows. As a farewell, my pleasure! I will put up a Maypole under the windows: so that false people know that I used to come to you.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: That day the children chant: Welcome, welcome Maja! We are fond of you. Welcome, lovely summer, green grain. What summer will bring us, winter will take away again.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation:
Then, on the first Sunday of May, after noon, the young men gather in the pub, and taking music and beer with them, they go around the village from house to house where maypoles have been raised for the girls. One of them, carrying a maypole decorated with ribbons, leads them. When they arrive at a house, the young man who built the maypole for the daughter of that house takes his girl to dance, and after dancing, offers her a token on a plate, namely a glass of beer, saying:  
"O little maypole, green maypole!  
Carried over hills and valleys:  
There will be no maiden so fair,  
Who wouldn’t give a crown or two.  
Gentlemen musicians! Cheers!"

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Sometimes such toasts are more or less improvised, like this one for example: Little, little, just a tiny bit, become a short pause, so that I can say a word with my virtuous maiden. My virtuous and noble maiden, I dreamed of you today, that before your windows stood a couple of green May trees, each one more beautiful than the other, entwined with red ribbons. And you, gentlemen musicians! Pay close attention to me: when I toast my virtuous and noble maiden, watch her closely and play up high, hush!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The budgerigar is kind, it gave us eggs from May; it gave us two Czech ones – we have quite a few from it. Then, taking a girl with them, they go from here to the next house, and so on, until they have visited the entire village, after which they either have fun with the girls in the village square or in the pub, dancing, singing, and feasting, until the money collected from the girls is sufficient.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The Sedlec maypole is green, and on it a red scarf: my beloved tied it there to please the young men.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: May 25. On Urban's day, hurry to weave flax.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Havel's rye, Urban's oats: tell me later what will come of it.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: When the elderberry blooms, may the wine also bloom.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: On the Ascension of God, the Lord God enters heaven and the devil enters the baba. Pentecost holidays.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Do not take off your fur coat before the Holy Spirit, and after the Holy Spirit, stay in your fur coat again.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: In Prachensko, they also use these verses: Dear housewife! Come out to the gate, do a good deed: give us some groats, give us some flour, after all, you have fields and meadows; give us a piece of lard so you can rule over your husband; take plenty from deep down, so your hand does not hurt from year to year, and your head until death. What they receive this way, they then sell and share the money.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: When they have already visited all the farmers, they head towards the village square, and one of them, the "crier," climbs onto some elevated spot—onto a stone, a stool, or even a tree, etc.—and recites about each house and its inhabitants some complimentary, but preferably mocking rhyme for the general amusement, for example: Hey, hey! Listen, locals and outsiders, to what I will tell you on Whit Monday: At the miller’s by the stream, there are little stones, and the girls there are like pictures. Hojala! Kotala! Is it true? The others reply: "It is! it is!" and the speaker adds: I don’t make this up myself, but it’s from the entire servant council: if the servant council is a beast, then I am from it! Then the music plays a fanfare, and the speaker meanwhile looks around another house, as if thinking what to proclaim next.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Hey! hey! Listen, old and young, to what people have that is good and what bothers them: At the Tomášeks' stands a willow, because Tomášek scratches his head. Hojala! kotala! if that is so! "Yes! yes!" But I don’t have it from my own head, but from the whole council of retainers: if the retainer council is a beast, then I am from it! Tuš. – The crier often also refers to the gifts that were received during the rounds with the king; for example.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Dvořáček gave a bundle of flax because he has a beautiful wife. "So it is! Is that so?" "Yes! Yes!" – Great laughter; for everyone knows that Dvořáček’s wife is the ugliest in the entire settlement. For these inquiries, traders sometimes prepare in advance, for a long time, with great secrecy; if anyone reveals anything prematurely, they incur a fine from the "guild council."

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Our queen walks barefoot, wading her white little feet in the dew: we beg, we ask for our poor queen! Our orphaned queen has no pies, butter, or eggs: we beg, we ask for our poor queen! If the queen had a father, she would be ashamed to go to walkers: we beg, we ask for our poor queen! If the queen had a mother, she would lack nothing: we beg, we ask for our poor queen!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Our barefoot queen walks, striking her feet against the stones, wading her white feet in the dew: please, help our poor queen! The queen and the barefoot maid, the poor queen, the poor king, have no oxen or cows: please, help our poor queen! The queen is usually the smallest girl, whom the others hide among themselves so that she cannot be seen. Only after the song is finished does the maid ask: "And where is your queen?" The girls then part, the queen comes out and turns around several times. Years ago, girls in the Budějovice region reportedly still observed the custom, when walking with the queen, that after they had gone around the whole village, in the middle of the village green they would join hands and perform a dance in the manner of a circle, during which they sang a song with the famous words: "Duli duli, hoj hoj!" They no longer know this song today, however. The words: Duli duli, hoj hoj! resemble the Slovak song: "Hoja! ďunďa Hoja!" (Songbook I, 3.) Moreover, similar dances are also performed during Easter holidays.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation:
In this game, the girls are called "bunnies," and with the queen also comes the king, that is, a girl dressed as a king, wearing a crown made of gilded paper on her head and holding a wooden sword. When the bunnies enter the house, they sing:  
"In the bailiff's courtyard,  
we build little horses,  
asking for gifts.  
Come out, mother,  
take a generous hand,  
give gifts to the bunnies.  
Whatever you give us,  
we will give to the king and queen,  
we will make castles.  
We will build a king from precious stones,  
from pure gold.  
We will build a queen from peacock feathers,  
from May flowers."  

The housewife came out, greeted the king, welcomed the queen, and ordered them to be given seven baskets of eggs and three dozen groschen.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Calling of the Herdswomen. Girls in the Jičín region, herding cattle apart from each other in the field, call each other with these and similar songs, which they sing or “call out” in a certain manner of recitative. 1. Hey, hey! Mářa, Mařenka Zikmundová! Drive that little one to me: the calf has a broken leg, tied with three ribbons; if you drove it to me, I would give you one: either the green one, or the red one, or the violet one; whichever you would like to choose, I would like to give it to you. Héla helilalou!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Hey, hey! Bára, Baruška Kulichova! Drive them here to me, I’m grazing under the forest; I peel the red apples and we’ll leave the green ones until they ripen, we’ll wait. If you have a little knife, take it for yourself; if you don’t, buy one for yourself. I bought a little knife to peel the red apples; I would lend it to you, but you would lose it, wasting it between young men and maidens. Héla helilalou!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Hey, hey! Anka, Anka Tomešova! Drive them to me to the grove, the wolf is sneaking here to me, he wants a calf from me: I don’t have a calf, I won’t give him a cow; both cows are the same, one equal to the other. Héla helilalou!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Hey, hey! That Káča Kubíkova lies down for a long time, she often calls her man a lazybones; if she didn’t lie down so long, she wouldn’t call him a lazybones. Héla helilalou!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Weeding. When cattle cause damage to someone's field, the children see this and call out like this to summon the farmer: I am weeding, I am weeding down there, at the Antošovice 1) barn: if only that Antoš kept watch, he would chase those cows away 2).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: For rain. In spring, especially in May, when rain is needed and it begins to drizzle, children run outside under the open sky and call out: Lord God, more rain! Lord God, more rain! Lord God, more rain!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: May rain. When it begins to rain in May, the children in the village square take each other by the hand and, spinning around, sing: May little rain, give it to us, God! So that the bread in the fields may prosper. God, give little rain to our fields, so that the rye and wheat may thrive!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Drizzling. When it starts to drizzle, children say: It’s drizzling: the old man is burying the old woman.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: For the ladybug. When there is a lot of rain in the summer, and meanwhile the sun comes out, children run outside and sing: 1. Come out, come out, little sun! above our little field; if you don’t come up, I will take you to Tábor, from Tábor to Tejn, I will lock you in a cage. Janeček went to the hill, driving ahead of him five little sheep and a sixth ram with golden horns.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Shine, shine, little sun! I will give you a golden apple; if that is not enough, I will give you a lordly herd. The lordly herd is lying down, maiden Lída is running. Lord Jan, Didi-Jan, is herding horses on the ridge with golden reins; with a golden whip, he will drive them.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Warm up, little sun, warm up, mountains and valleys: jump up higher, make a circle on our roof.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Geese. Geese herders driving them to pasture or from pasture sing like this: Hey, hey, little geese! All in a row, all in a row: may the soldiers not steal you, not steal you!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Haj, haj, little geese! nicely in rows, nicely in rows: so you don’t all starve to death, all starve to death!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Crow. When children see it flying, they call out: The crow is flying, it has no children; we have children, we won't sell them: we will bury them in a little grave 1) and give them to God for free. The crow is flying, it has no children; we have children, we won't sell them: we will bury them 1) in a little hollow near the little church. 1) Or: we will bury them under the green little lawn! Or also: we will bury them under the green little cushion! The crow is flying, it has no children. Where do they have them? "In the black forest." What do they cook for them? "Strawberry porridge." What do they grease it with? "Lard." Instead of crow, they also say: The kite is flying, etc. When they hear a crow croaking, they say: The crow croaks: someone is walking.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Cuckoo. When the villagers hear the cuckoo calling in spring, they say: "Cuckoo, cuckoo! Cuckoo, how many years will I live, man or woman?" Or: "Cuckoo, cuckoo! Cuckoo, what will be grown this year, rye or wheat, barley, etc.?" The number of times the cuckoo calls corresponds to the number of years of life for themselves, and they count as many gold coins per measure of the mentioned grain.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Snail. When children find a snail, they say like this: Snails, snails! Stick out your horns: otherwise, I will throw you into boiling water.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Little snail, little snail! Stick out your horns, I’ll give you a kreuzer for cheese, and a penny for tobacco: you’ll become a little rascal!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Mayka. Whoever has a sore spot catches a mayka (May fly), places it on the wound, and says: Mayka, Mayka! Give me ointment for my pain; if you don’t give ointment, I will throw you into the collyrium. When squeezed, the mayka releases a black-yellow juice that heals.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The ladybug, also known as the ladybird, majdalenka (Serbian mara, German Marienkäfer), or pinka and pelesta (variegated, spotted, Latin coccinella punctata), as it is called. When children find it, they take it in their hand and let it crawl upward along their finger. When the ladybug reaches the fingertip, it stops as if hesitating; then suddenly it spreads its wings and flies away to the side. When it is about to fly away, they say: Little sun, little sun! Where will you fly? To heaven or to hell?

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Ladybug, ladybug! Fly to the sky: tell me, will the sun come out today?

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: God’s little sunshine! Your house is on fire: be careful not to let it burn down.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The young men then say at the same time: Pele– pele– pelesta! Where will my bride be?

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Dance, ladybird, dance, mountains, valleys, dance: show me the place where I can reach, where my beloved is.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Pinko, linko! Fly to the golden window: show me the side where I can get.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Chicory, also known as St. Peter's herb (solsequium, Wegewart). Girls pick its unopened flower for luck in love, wrapping a scarf or apron around their fingers. While picking it, they say: Chicory by the roadside! I pick you for luck: to wish me the beloved I have waited for in vain! Then they place the picked flower behind their bosom, saying: May you bloom! If after a short time it blooms from their body heat, the girl hopes for her happiness.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Crosswort spice (in Polish: křižovnice, polygala, Milchkraut). Maidens take this spice, crush it between two flints, and strain the juice through the corner of their apron to find out if their beloved is sincere, according to the rule: if the juice is green – love is cold; the redder it is – the more sincere.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Podražej. To make butter yellow and cream thick, they dig up a spice called Podražej or Podražec (aristolochia, German Osterluzei) in the forest in the month of May and give it in small amounts to the cattle in their drink. While digging it, they say as follows – Little root! In the name of God the Father, I seek you, in the name of God the Son, I find you, in the name of God the Holy Spirit, I dig you up and give it to my cows to use! May God the Father help me with this, etc.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: June. A cold June, the farmer shrugs his shoulders.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The swarming of June bees is not worth ringing about. Sr. May, p. 70.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If it does not rain in May, then June will make up for it.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: June 8. Medard's dew – forty days of rain.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: June 15. On Saint Vitus's Day, it dawns all night.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Saint Vitus root chips.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Saint Vitus preaches to sow, Saint John reaps himself.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: June 21. On St. John's Day, there is no night.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Long day, short thread.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Vít – cultivate, Jan – head (they say about cabbage).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Do John the Baptist, do not praise barley.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: St. John the Baptist. On the night before St. John, girls pick the down from spring rye, i.e., rye leaves, and tie them from memory in their lap, saying: Great God! Saint John! I humbly ask you to reveal to me yourself whom I will marry.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Grass, green grass, woven from memory! Tell me the simple truth, whom will I marry? What is weaving comparable to, such is the fate they expect of themselves.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Before Saint John the Baptist, no mother whose child has died should eat strawberries or cherries; for on that day the Virgin Mary distributes to the children in heaven a little bouquet of strawberries and cherries, and if she came to such a child whose mother had eaten this fruit earlier, she would say: Your mother was greedy, she ate your bouquet – I have nothing to give you! and the child would cry.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If it rains on St. John the Baptist's Day, it will rain for three whole days; if the weather is nice on St. John the Baptist's Day, you will have three nice whole days.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If we pray for rain before St. John’s Day, after St. John’s Day it will come without asking.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If the cuckoo calls after St. John the Baptist, they say: Be quiet and don't cuckoo about the high prices.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: How many days after Saint John the cuckoo calls, that many groshes (now perhaps gold coins) will be the measure of rye.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Many boletes, little bread.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Many fish, little grain.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Please, let us through that golden gate. Gate: What will you give us for that? Army: A slice of bread with butter, and you keep the last one. Gate: Go!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: II. Two boys first secretly agree between themselves who will be the Angel and who will be the Devil, and with a half-step they make a gate, but each only with one hand. The other boys, holding hands, form a circle in the gate, so that one pillar of the gate is inside the circle and the other pillar is outside the circle. The circle begins to turn, and the Devil and the Angel sing as follows: Under the pit, a mouse, under the pit: come, you tomcat, come with us! The keys ring, the violet scents. The gate is open, supported by a sword: whoever touches the gate shall be beheaded. Slash! Slash! Slash! At the word “slash!” the arch is lowered for the first and second time always between two boys in the circle, and immediately lifted again; but at the third time the circle stops, and the gate asks the one in front of whom the arch was lowered: “Whom do you join?” He then answers: “You,” pointing to one or the other, not knowing whether he joined the Angel or the Devil, and stands behind him. Then the circle closes again, starts to turn, and the gate sings as in the beginning: Under the pit, a mouse, etc. This goes on until even the last one in the circle has joined either one or the other side of the gate.
