Then the Angel and the Devil reveal themselves, and the “Heavenly ones” and the “Hellish ones” begin to fight, pulling each other across the Line as in the previous game.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: For the goslings. The same game as the boys’: “At the Golden Gate.” Two girls imitate a gate, standing opposite each other and holding hands raised so that there is a passage underneath. One of them calls herself the Sun, the other the Moon. From afar comes a line of girls, the “goslings,” one after another, holding each other by the hips over their skirts; the first called the “gosling boy.” Gate: Goslings, goslings, fly! Goslings: We must not. Gate: For whom? Goslings: For the lord. Gate: The lord is not home, he went to Beroun, for a white ram with golden horns. The goslings fly toward the gate, shouting: “Honk! honk! honk!” And when the last in line tries to fly through, the gate closes, and the gosling gets caught between the Sun and the Moon. Gate: Gosling, where have you been? Gosling: In the forest. Gate: What were you doing there? Gosling: Cutting wood. Gate: What for the wood? Gosling: For the garden. Gate: What for the garden? Gosling: To plant lettuce. Gate: What for the lettuce? Gosling: To have food. Gate: For that you must, gosling, be ours. Whom will you choose: the Sun or the Moon? The gosling answers and stands behind the one she chooses. Then the gate calls again: Goslings, goslings, fly! Goslings: “We must not,” and so on, as at first. When all the goslings, including the gosling boy, have joined either the Sun or the Moon, the “Suns” and “Moons” begin to fight, each side straining to pull the other over the line, in every way, just like in the boys’ game: “At the Golden Gate.” See also Krol. I, 295. – Suš. 729. – 1) Also: for precious roots, for heart aches.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: On the Water Sprite. I. This game is preferably played either by boys alone or by girls alone. A place is designated that is supposed to be water or a pond, with a shore or an embankment. The pond is usually a hollow or also some bushes. The Water Sprite or Water Goblin has authority only in the water; his power does not extend beyond the shore. The Water Sprite, who is first chosen by counting out, sits in the middle of the pond on a certain spot called the "saddle" (i.e., residence, dwelling), pretending to sew something and muttering: Shine, little moon, shine, so I can thread my needle.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: So far, the boys run across the dam, pretending to swim, and provoke the Water Sprite with a song: "Water Sprite, Tatrman! Put your skin on the drum; we will drum for you when you come out of the water." Whoever the Water Sprite touches in the water is already in his power and he can take them to the middle of the pond, where the captured person must remain until the Water Sprite catches all the others or until a boy frees the captives. If one boy manages to outrun the Water Sprite, jump on the saddle before him, free all the captives, and then the rescuer and the freed ones run out of the water to the shore; the Water Sprite must sit back in his place and the game starts again. If the Water Sprite manages to catch all the boys, then the last one caught becomes the Water Sprite and the game starts over again. However, they often play this game simply without freeing anyone, until the Water Sprite catches all the boys, one by one; then the last caught becomes the Water Sprite in the next game.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: II. When girls play the Water Sprite, who of course is also a girl, their game differs from the previous boys' game only in their different activities in the water and different verses. The girls, upon coming to the shore, ask the Water Sprite: Water Sprite, Tatrmánek! reveal yourself: can we wash diapers in this pond? The Water Sprite replies the first and second time, "I do not allow it!" and only when asked for the third time does he answer in a hoarse voice: You may, wash and rinse; just leave my little fish to me.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The girls step into the water and pretend to be washing, singing: I wash, I wash diapers for the little white wines; I wash, I wash shirts for God's Sundays; I wash, I wash scarves for the green holidays. Suddenly, as if they see little fish, they jump after them and catch them, calling out: I'm catching little fish! I'm catching little fish! At that moment, the Water Sprite jumps out and runs after them, growling: For this, you will give me your little souls! Whoever he catches in the water, he takes to his place, and the rest of the game continues as the boys play.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The Magpie Game. When green pods appear, the boys play for them, gathering together in a group, as follows: One is the guesser (tazač), the others, usually three, are the guessers (hadači). The guesser takes a pod in his hand so that only the tip is visible, and says: "The magpie jumps over the mountains." The first guesser replies: "And I gather peas after it." The second guesser: "Me too after it." The third guesser: "Me too after it." The guesser asks: "For how many horses?" Then the first guesser guesses, for example, "For four." The second guesser says, for example, "For five." The third, for example, "For seven." 1) If one of them has guessed correctly how many horses, i.e., seeds, the pod contains, all the guessers receive one pod each from the guesser; if no one guessed correctly, the guessers must give one pod each to the guesser, and the game starts again.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Strawberry plants. When children go to pick strawberries, upon entering the forest they say three times: Snakes, scorpions, lizards from the forest: the Virgin Mary with Lord Jesus into the forest! and on the third time they add: And we with them!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Closing the forest. When children come out of the forest with strawberries or mushrooms, upon reaching the edge, they close the forest with these verses which they loudly chant: I am closing, I am closing the forest, so that no one enters here, neither cat nor dog: if an old woman enters here, let her become a frog; if an old man enters here, let him become a hoopoe; if a maiden enters here, let her become a doe; if a young man enters here, let him become a sparrowhawk!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Strawberry pickers. The strawberry pickers go, go, carrying strawberries: full cups like blackthorn they carry strawberries to themselves. When I come home, I will take a slice of bread, and strawberries, and strawberries, and strawberries with it.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Saint Procopius, root to the end.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: July 13. Saint Markyta leads the reapers into the rye, or: she threw the sickle into the rye.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Reapers to the field, bees from the field.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: July 25. From the side St. James' wind blows, on that side there will be dear flour. Hot weather on St. James' Day, cold at Christmas.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: By the time St. James (the wine) flowers, it ripens by Havel.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: 26th July. Saint Anna, cool in the morning.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Saint Anne reaped the rye.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: August. What August fails to cook, September will not finish baking.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: August 1. Saint Peter in Chains sends pensions to the peasants. See February 22, on page 47.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: To the Steward. (Tune 537.) Blow, little wind, from the Danube, from the Danube, take an apple or two; let them be nice and red, red, like our lord.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: We have a young master, young; at Vosek's they have an old one; that one God himself gave us, God himself, the Virgin Mary, Saint John.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: That one of ours is not sharpening the sharpener until everyone nicely asks him, oh! until they ask him: he is afraid of that sharpener, as he already has a small piece left, oh! a small piece.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: That steward is a joke, he has rattling trousers; they rattle on him so much they catch on the stubble.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Tomeš Javůrkojc can do a lot, he has a girl like a rose, nicely red, similar to the most beautiful rose. If you want to know who she is: Mařenka šenkýřojc – she is beautiful!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Under that hill, by the juniper, my beloved was playing with a young lad; and he was not just any lad, he was a handsome young man. If you want to know which one he is: Honzíček, the bailiff’s son – he is handsome!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: At Javůrks, the rooster sings – Honza Žílojc climbs out of the pigsty, carrying clogs in his hand, he loves her so much he trembles, oh dear! he trembles.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: By the blacksmiths, a good puddle, Marjánka is like a rose, like the most beautiful rose, Pavloušek's dearest, oh! dearest. By the Šohaj family, a fine maple – Baruška is like a tulip, like the most beautiful tulip, František's dearest, oh! dearest.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: There, a man is coming here, look how handsome he is: he is handsome to his doll, to that barmaid Madlenka.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The budgie is on the goat, Jiříček will help her: but will the budgie help her when he can't get to her?

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Káča climbs onto the goat, I will climb after her; wherever Káča goes, there I go, if I catch her, she will be mine.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The sun is already behind the mountain, the reapers from Lhota are coming from the field. Ma'am, bring the dinner, the reapers are coming to your door. Our farmer is a kind gentleman, he gives us a jug of beer.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: To the health of our lord, who gives us beer! To the health of the whole house, that we have roast with it!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: These are good, golden times, those golden harvests: we have beer, we have meat, and a piece of bread to go with it.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: August 16. Holy Queen gives the first tapping (i.e., the first nuts).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: August 10 and 24. As Saint Lawrence and Saint Bartholomew, so will be the autumn.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: September 14. Holy Cross – sheep shearing.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: September 21. On St. Matthew's Day, whoever has no bread should not bite.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: After St. Matthew, a hat on the ears.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: September 25. Saint Maurice causes wheat to become blighted (for whoever plows or sows it on that day).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Autumn. In autumn, a spoonful of rain and a watering can of mud.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Roast in the autumn (i.e., roast). Cf. Spring, p. 53.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If there are many sloes in the autumn, there will be a lot of rye next year.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: In autumn, the apple blossom spoils the world for young people.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: At kokše. The boys stand in a straight line one behind the other, each holding firmly the hips of the one standing in front of him, so that the hawk cannot break through. The first boy is called the "kokeš"; another boy, standing outside the line, is called the "hawk." The hawk flies in front of the kokše, saying: "Kokeš!" Kokeš: "What do you want?" Hawk: "The one at the back." Kokeš: "Go and catch him." The hawk runs after the boy at the back; however, this boy tries to run to the front on the other side of the line, attempting to reach the first end of the line in front of the kokše. The hawk runs after him first on one side, then on the other, until he either catches him or the boys manage to position themselves in front of the kokše. If they manage to do this, that boy becomes the kokše himself and the game starts again; the hawk must remain the hawk until he catches someone. When the hawk catches someone, the caught boy must now become the hawk, and the hawk becomes the kokše. While chasing, the hawk is not allowed to run more than three steps away from the line.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: The Wolf Game. I. One boy is the wolf, hiding in the bushes, behind the fence, in the ditch, etc.; the second is the shepherd, and the third is the dog. The other children, boys and girls, are the sheep, sitting on a hillock and plucking grass as if grazing. The shepherd sings: "I graze, I graze the sheep on the wolf's meadow; the wolf is not home, he went to Bydžov for four sheep, the fifth a ram, for a white ram with golden horns." Meanwhile, the wolf sneaks up and jumps among the sheep, which scatter; whichever he catches, he takes to his place. This game repeats as many times as there are still sheep grazing. Meanwhile, the shepherd and the dog defend their sheep, so the boy playing the wolf must be stronger than they are. When all the sheep have been taken, the wolf also catches the dog and finally the shepherd himself.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: In some places, they play this game without a shepherd and without a dog. The grazing sheep sing: I’m tearing, tearing little grass in that green little grove; if a wolf comes to me, he will seize my neck: I am not afraid of the wolf, I will milk my little cows: come, wolf, come! Then the wolf jumps up and the sheep start to flee, dropping the grass they had torn and saying: "Here you have, wolf, your little grass!" The wolf catches whichever sheep he can, takes it away, and then goes to lie in wait again, until he catches them all. Sr. Krolm. II, 456.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: In the forest, a wolf, sheep in the pasture, and a farmer. Farmer: Sheep, sheep! Come home. Sheep: We can't. Farmer: Why not? Sheep: Because of the wolf. Farmer: Where is the wolf? Sheep: In the forest, carrying a black sheep. Then the wolf runs out and catches one sheep for himself.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: II. The shepherdess, the little dog, the flock of sheep, and the wolf as in the previous play. The shepherdess with the little dog walks around the flock, twirling a twig in her hand and singing: Our lady used to say that I should shepherd well; but I will not shepherd, I would rather leave here: she herself eats the malt’s porridge, and gives me whey; she herself drinks from a glass, and gives me from a shoe; she herself rides in a carriage, and I must ride on a cart. Now she calls into the forest: “Wolf! Wolf!” – The wolf runs out: “What do you want? I am the wolf.” Shepherdess: “If you are the wolf, catch the prettiest little sheep.” Wolf: “I don’t want the sheep; I want a pretty little girl!” The shepherdess runs, the wolf after her, and when he catches her, they take each other’s hands and, turning around, sing: Our lady used to say that I should shepherd well; but I will not shepherd, I would rather go away with the wolf: she herself eats etc. Meanwhile, the dog constantly barks and the flock bleats, accompanying the song. Štěpnice, 1857, p. 50.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Godmother Lidka. This game is usually played by five girls either in a tree garden or in the village square, where at least four trees stand together. Each girl holds on to one of these four trees, and the fifth odd one is the "beggar." The beggar approaches one girl and says: "Godmother Lidka, lend me a sieve." She replies: "I don’t have it, the one next to me has it," or: "I lent it to the neighbor," while pointing to the girl next to her. The beggar then goes to this neighbor: "Godmother Lidka, lend me a sieve." This girl again points further to the girl by the third tree, and this one points to the fourth. However, the last girl must not make an excuse that she does not have the sieve and send the beggar back to the first, but instead shouts: "Move!" Then all the girls must change their places, that is, each leaving her tree and trying to catch another; at this opportunity, the beggar quickly takes the place by the nearest tree, and the girl left without a tree becomes the new beggar. And yet, even during the game, when the beggar is talking to a godmother, the other girls continuously switch places behind their backs; if the beggar manages to take the place of any girl, she stops being the beggar, and that girl becomes the new beggar.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Warm October, cold February.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If siskins are not seen, there will be a harsh winter.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: 7th October. On St. Mark's day, whoever has no bread should bite apples.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: October 16. Saint Gall went into the cabbage.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Havel's rye, Urban's oats, what will come of it, then tell me.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: 18th October. On Saint Luke's day, abundance of bread and porridge.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: 21 October. Saint Ursula, virgin and martyr.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Otherwise, there is no celebration: the peasant women shine like gold, the young men shout in the pub: let's go there, let's find out what it means, we'll see. In the past, the farmer was poor: when he sold grain at the market, he asked where they were distilling spirits; with rice poison for the hen, he put a cap aside for himself. Now the farmer, a proud lad, calls out: Pour me some good must, for today I earned enough rags: pour a little, serve me, townsmen and farmers!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Come children, go home, dad is calling you: we will have a feast, we have killed an ox. Four sheep and two rams, a pig as well: we will have a feast, come home children!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: At the feast, the farmer is the master: the farmer’s wife goes for beer with a large jug, her breasts smell only of balm. At the feast, the farmer makes noise, he refuses to do any work: hey girl, dance around! hop, pretty girl!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: On Kunětická hůra, a peasant killed a chicken, and a peasant woman killed a goose; they each had a piece.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: This is a golden feast, this is a golden Sunday: we have meat, and more meat, with a piece of roast. This is a golden feast, this is a golden Monday: we have meat, and more meat, just like yesterday on Sunday.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Don't expect, don't expect a feast: we have no cakes, nor beer!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Turn around me, my maiden, like a robin around a stump; make a circle, my golden girl! God willing, during Carnival you will be mine.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: October 28. On Saint Simonis, you can already walk around in a fur coat.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Šimon Juda – winter is everywhere.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: On the night of St. Simon and Jude at midnight, maidens go into the green rye to listen for where their destined one will come from, saying: I don’t know, I don’t know, in which direction I will be sadly taken: bark, bark, dog, where is my beloved today! Instead of walking, they shake a fence or a tree that stands at a three-way crossroads, speaking as follows:

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: I shake, I shake, I won't stop until I get a sign: bark, bark dog, where is my dear today!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Indian summer. The week after St. Wenceslas begins the Indian summer. But since there is usually little warmth left, they say: There will probably be as much warmth as an old woman has.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Wild geese on the migration, the end of Indian summer.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: November 11. Saint Martin arrives on a white horse (snow is falling).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: On St. Martin's Day, smoke usually comes out of the chimney.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: On St. Martin's Day, the goose sings the most beautifully (i.e., geese are eaten).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If the goose walks on the ice on St. Martin's Day, it will walk in the mud at Christmas.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: 25 November. On St. Catherine's Day, hide under the duvet.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If it snows on St. Catherine's Day, add an extra quilt.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: 25th and 30th November. From St. Catherine's Day to St. Andrew's Day, one can hardly warm up under the quilt.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: At midnight before St. Andrew's Day, the girls get up and go to knock on the chicken coop, saying at the corner: Rooster, rooster! Crow, if I may get a husband, let me know. Hens, stop crowing, do not spoil, do not spoil my marrying. If the rooster crows first, the girl has hope that she will marry that year; if the hen crows, it foretells that the girl will have to come again next year.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: December 6. Saint Nicholas washes the shores (it’s raining).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: If it rains on St. Nicholas Day or if snow falls, there will be an abundant pea harvest next year.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: December 13. Saint Lucy takes a sip from the night.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Lucie, Lucie, she will steal our nights – and add no days (they jokingly claim).

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Spinning. Short day, long thread.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: After the feast day, spinning begins and lasts until Ash Wednesday. In the evenings, girls gather in one house to spin and work on tasks; during this time, they sing, tell each other what they know and can do, joke, and engage in playful teasing. Boys also come to this place, which is not unpleasant for the girls; indeed, they themselves attract the boys in this way: they sweep dirt from all corners into the middle of the room, saying: "We are sweeping away the dirt: bachelors, widowers, whoever wants to come: from the east, from the west, from the front, from the back across the garden." Half of this dirt is then thrown out in front of the house onto the road.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Little cone. God willing, on Monday my little cone gives hope: Little cone, horsetail, girl like a little rose, 1) my little cone gives hope. God willing, on Tuesday my little cone will be ready. God willing, on Wednesday my little cone I will spin. God willing, on Thursday my little cone like a mole. God willing, on Friday my little cone has a holiday. God willing, on Saturday the spindles will be on the fence. God willing, on Sunday, so that everyone knows.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Winter Games. 1. Spinning Game. Two girls sit opposite each other, pretending to spin yarn on a spindle. Using their left hand, they take fiber from the cone, and with their right hand, they spin the spindle and pull the thread, then wind it onto the spindle. However, they do not spin at the same time, but alternately: when one pulls the thread, the other winds it. While winding, they speak alternately as follows: 1. Behind Vítoch’s yard – 2. a little hare was running with a drum there. – 1. Why didn’t you catch him? – 2. I wasn’t fast enough. – 1. I would have helped you. – 2. Behind the blacksmith’s yard – 1. a magpie was flying with a drum. – 2. Why didn’t you catch her? etc. They continue playing this way, always naming a different farmer’s yard and also a different animal with a drum, for example: a carp was swimming with a drum, a frog was jumping with a drum, etc. The one who can no longer name any farmer or any animal loses.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: For the gold ring. This game is usually played by girls. One is the dealer, another stands aside as the guesser; the rest sit in a circle, with their hands folded in their laps, palms together. The dealer, similarly folding her hands palm to palm, places a ring between them, then goes to each girl in the circle, placing her hands between the girl's hands, saying: "I hide, I hide the gold ring!" or: "I smear, I clap so no one can see!" and at the same time lets the ring slip into one of their palms, but in such a way that the guesser does not know who has the ring. Then she says to the guesser: Guess, guess, guesser, who has the gold ring? The guesser walks around the girls, saying: I would like to guess, jumping over golden trees, crossing the sea, weaving nets: whoever has it, let her give it, and not make a fool of me! She has it – she has it – it’s her! Then she reaches the hands of the one she guessed for the ring. If she guessed correctly, she becomes the dealer in the next game, and the one who had the ring becomes the guesser; the previous dealer then takes her place in the circle. If the guesser does not guess and reaches empty hands, she must remain the guesser in the next game.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: In some places, the game is called: "Na makovec" ("On the Poppy Seed Cake"). The dealer, while distributing, says: "I'm hiding, I'm hiding the poppy seed cake!" or: "On the poppy seed cake! Hide it well!" and then says to the guesser: "Guess, guess, guesser! Who has the poppy seed cake, let them give it, so they don’t make fools of us." The guesser then walks around from one person to another, saying: "Bread – bread – bread… cake!" At the word "cake," she reaches into the hands of a girl to take it.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: On the slipper. A game similar to the one with the ring, except there are no giveaways here, only guessing. The girls, sitting on the floor in a circle one behind the other, push the slipper to each other under their knees. The guesser, standing in the circle, asks: "Who has my golden slipper?" Each girl to whom the question is addressed answers: "I don't have it." If the guesser suspects that the slipper is under a certain girl, she says: "Stand up!" If she guessed correctly, she sits in that girl's place, and that girl must become the guesser.

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation: Kuca-Baba. A game played in winter in a spacious room. The one who is last during counting becomes Kuca-Baba: one person ties a scarf over their eyes, then two others take them by the hands and, leading them around, ask: Kuca-Baba, where are we taking you? "To the corner." What is in that corner? "A rooster." What is in that rooster? "A golden ball." What is in that ball? "A golden needle." What is in that needle? "A golden thread." Then catch us, Kuca-Baba! Or also: Where are you going, grandma? "To the corner." What is in that corner? "A rooster." What more? "An old shoe." What is in that shoe? "A golden apple." There you have it, grandma! 1) At the last words, one of them lightly taps Kuca-Baba on the back, and leaving her standing in the middle of the room, they all run off to the corners. Kuca-Baba gropes around to catch someone, while the others give signals: if she is far from those hiding, they call out: "All water! all water!" If she approaches someone, the others shout: "Fire! fire!" In the same way, they signal if Kuca-Baba could do some harm or injure herself: "Fire" for example "window" – "shelf" etc. Whoever she catches must become Kuca-Baba instead of her. 1) This rhyme ends with: Do you like porridge? Go get a spoon!

label: Písně a říkadla výroční
translation:
The kitten sits among the barrels, would drink beer, doesn’t know where to go. Hey, kitten in the corner! Girls, jump! The lads are coming, coming, the lads are coming! The kitten lies on top of the stones, counting how many maidens there are. Hey, kitten in the corner! Girls, jump! The lads are coming, coming, the lads are coming! The kitten runs around the barn, if it falls, it will be down. Hey, kitten in the corner! Girls, jump! The lads are coming, coming, the lads are coming!

This song was connected with a certain dance or national game, which was especially performed after a wedding on a Friday. Ziegler, in his translation of the Telemachus stories (I, p. 271), describes it only as a children's game as follows: children of both sexes gathered, formed a large circle, held hands, jumped, and sang. At the last word (of each verse) every child broke free, one after another chased, and one ran away from the other. Whoever caught and overpowered whom led the overcome one to a designated place called "pykačka." There, the captured child had to stay until after the chase ended and was redeemed with a token or flag. Thus, this game was said to be played in the town of Dobruška. From this description, of course, it is not possible to form a clear image of the exact nature of this game.

— Instead of the last words: "The lads are coming!" it is also sung: "The lads up there!" In some places, this song ends like in the children’s game: "Sil Petr proso" etc., with these words: "There are few of us, few of us, come boys (girls) among us." Cf. Jičín Songbook, no. 449; Krolmus II, 123; Sug. 618.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: She stood on the doorstep, looked pretty, I gave her a kiss, she smiled. When she stepped down from the doorstep, she was very scared, telling me sadly that I had to go to the army. I will go to the army, I will fight: if the Lord God wills, I will still be yours.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Many have a beloved at home. Whoever has her across the field, a sorrowful lad! He must go to her, even if his neck were to be broken.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Love me, Jeníček, love me, we will buy ourselves a vineyard: You will plow and I will live, we will be good together.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: She was grazing geese on the clover, she asked him: "Will you come?" – "I will come, I will come, I'm sure, just prepare dinner for me!"

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: You, little church in Podhajská, you stand on a nice little hill. Maidens come out of you like little angels from heaven. One came out fair and white, and she will be my dear. The second came out even prettier, she will be my dearest. The third came out like a rose, no one can have her. I must have her, even if I have to stay dead there.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The ladybug came out over the mountains, my dear looks out from the chamber; she looked, watched, and glanced back at her sweetheart. She looks around in all directions, her little eyes are tearful: eyes, eyes, why are you crying? Go and rather get some sleep. If I knew, my dear, that you love me from your heart, I would give you a gift for your pleasure, as proof of my sincerity. I would give you a golden ring as a sign of our love, engraved with the words that you are my delight.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Oh path, narrow path! If Ančička walks along it, I will pave it with little stones for her to walk on with her little feet. The sun is standing above the mill: so where will we meet? We will meet on the little path, there we will give each other a little kiss.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: When I used to come to you, the little dog would bark at me; I threw him crusts from my pocket: let me in, little dog, into the little room. I tapped on the window, I called your daughter: your daughter was very proud, she did not even come outside to me.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Don't think, Mom, that I will marry whoever you want: I will marry whoever I want, I have to live with that until I die. They slandered me, saying I had nine girls: but I have only one, she is pretty, and I will marry her.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Go, my dear, go into the forest, see if there is dew: the dew is nicely white, and rosemary grows on it.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: There is no herb in the world that is not for something: look, my dear, for the little flower that will heal my little heart.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Wait for me, dear girl, we will go together for ducklings: I will carry them in a scarf, you will beg them from me.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: It's just a little while until daybreak: come, my dear, escort me, escort me through the grove, I will give you a few kisses there.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Why are you coming here to us, boys, when you bring nothing with you? – We are coming for the red scarf, to make love to your girls.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Which one will be mine, the little one: she chatters like a swallow. She chatters, she twirls when a handsome boy leans close to her.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The Krumlov Castle is already being demolished: that my dear one is wandering after another. He wanders, wanders, people told me – until he wanders too far, but he will come back again! My dear is traveling home from Prague: I will go to meet him tomorrow. Oh, he rides, rides with four little horses: black ones at the back, dapple greys in front.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: You haven’t come to us for a long time, girl: I thought you wouldn’t come anymore! When you came to us, come with us tonight: you’re not a stranger, you’re a good acquaintance.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: If my mother gave me grass, I would mow a load of grass for her. Grass does not only grow behind the water along the path where the young men from Chejnice ride. But not all of them, only some: those who wear peacock feathers. Peacock feathers, silver clasps: these are young men like from a peony.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A wide meadow, green grass; my dear was grazing, she was grazing a stag. It was not a stag, it was a doe: wait, my dear, for me three years. "To wait three years, yet not marry: I could raise sorrow for myself. To raise sorrow, to nurture joy, then regret it until death."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A little bird flew in from a foreign land; it brought me a letter with greetings. I wanted to see the land of Bohemia, to know that there are beautiful maidens in Bohemia. Every Czech girl has honey on her tongue; and when you get to know her, she is like poison. I prefer to stay in our homeland, I will find a charming girl in Moravia. Every Moravian girl is like a straight candle, each has a sincere heart for a young man.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: One time when I was going to my sweetheart, the moon and bright stars were shining for me. When I arrived there, she was not asleep, she was waiting for me outside under the tree. "Don't go, my girl, into my chamber, because your horseshoes clink very loudly here. Let us rather stay outside under the tree and rejoice together there."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: It’s clouding over, clouding over, that it will rain: behind our garden a green hill. And on that hill a wide stone: a boy with a doll sits on it.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Stone. Under our windows, a wide stone: sit down, girl, sit on it; God will give you happiness, blessings for your sincere love.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: When I was leaving, music was playing; my dearest was crying for me. Oh, don't cry for me, rather come with me: and I will buy you a green skirt. A green skirt, not too long, so that you can wander with me. We will wander over mountains, valleys, and the forests will be our chambers. Green grass our quilt: there we will sleep and make love, dear girl.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A crow on the oak is pecking acorns: and only the Lord God Himself knows whose girl she will be! "Whose else would I be, since I promised: under the green oak, that we would be together, I told you."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A little girl walks through the field, looking for a little herb, her head aches. Little herb! If I knew you: I would surely conjure myself a young man. But not just any, only one: I would choose for myself the most faithful of a hundred.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: If Daddy hadn't argued, I would say who was with me yesterday: argue, Daddy, or not, my dear was with me in the pub. If that Daddy hadn't hummed, I would say what my dear gave me: hum, my dear Daddy! I got a little kiss from him. If Daddy hadn't been surprised, I would say what the boy promised me: be surprised, Daddy, as much as you want, you will still gladly marry me off. My dear promised me, he promised when he kissed me goodbye: when the wheat is threshed, he won’t leave me at your place anymore.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Just you, my horse, carry me nicely, you’ll get oats today. Once you bring me to my dear one, you will shake your little head nicely.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My golden doll! How are you? When I come to you tomorrow, what will you give me? "I will bring cakes on a platter, pour you some beer, you will drink."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I would like to give you a ride, girls, if the stroller didn't collapse: but the stroller won't collapse, after all it is forged in Záboří.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Eight pins, the ninth king: I threw once, I immediately won. I won a doll, 1) which is pretty: she walks in slippers to the town. From Budějovická. 1) I won a chubby doll: she has a spotted skirt.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: And how are you then, my dear, how are you? "Oh, I am fine — when you don't care about me. You come to the pub, drink beer: and you don't remember poor me!" How am I supposed to remember you: when your people are arming to come to you? "Just never believe that, boy: they always ask, 'why don't you come to us?'"

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Only you come to us, Matoušek! Just you come to us: leave your shoes in the garden under the windows. Madam says: "Whose shoes are these?" – They are Matoušek’s work shoes.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Whose boy is that? He walks at night: he wakes people up all night long. But he is the candle maker's boy: he goes after the steward's daughter. I knock, I knock, girl, open up for me: after all, it’s not summer, it’s winter. "I would gladly open to you if my mother didn’t hear. If my mother heard, she might beat us both. You better go home, boy: leave me here to God."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Only after you leave from here, my girl, will I go after you as a soldier. "What would you do there, my maiden? You wouldn't even recognize me in the army." I would become a little bird, I would hover over your hat. I would become a swallow, I would hover over your head.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: When I used to go to Nevěsice, I marked the path for myself: I threw red rose petals along the way I accompanied the doll.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: That gentleman's courtyard is shining white, my dear one serves there, I like him; he serves, serves, serves in the gentleman's courtyard, he blushes there like a rose.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: That Řečice castle, nice and white, my beloved serves there, I like it; it is nicely red, like a flower: if only I could possibly kiss it!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Water flows around the maple: drink, my dear, it is good; drink, my dear, drink it, your little heart will be healed.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Around the Monastery, water flows: drink, my dear, it is good! "I drank to my heart's content: thank you, boy, for your sincerity!"

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Parsley bed, poppy bed: Don’t come to our house, boys. Parsley bed, millet bed: don’t come to us, boys, when there is dew. Parsley bed, carrot bed: don’t come to us, boys, when it gets dark. Parsley bed, cabbage bed: don’t come to us, boys, on Sunday.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The gentleman's little bridge is bending, there is grass on it, it is not being cut. And under that little bridge, there are small fish, there is my doll, I like it. Sew me a little shirt, mother, and I will go for Ančička. Sew me an embroidered one, mother, because I have a doll that is talked about badly. "Who talked badly about her?" My friend: he talked badly because he likes her. "Who talked badly about her?" Everyone: but I do not disgrace her, I like her. "And if you like her, then keep her, don't pay attention to what people say."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Washed small vest not folded: that my doll is insulted. Who insulted her? Everyone: what does it matter to anyone? I like it. "Washed kerchief not folded: that my little boy is insulted. Who insulted him? Everyone: what does it matter to anyone? I like it."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: What the Hosiňáci have shown us, what the Hosiňáci have done for us: they took our girls away, they took our girls away from us!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: There is a light in the mountains, I will run there; if it is a little flower, I will pick it. It is not a flower, it is my beloved: that is why it shines so much, because she is sincere.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Tell me, my dear! Why are you so thin? "Why wouldn't I be, since I am a maiden? An honest maiden, and you, young man: we both wear a green wreath."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: There is an apple tree over there, I will climb it, shake it just once, then come down again immediately. There are apples on it, nice red ones; under it my pleasure usually sits.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Under our windows grows rowan: that our girls are like a picture. Under our windows grows wild rose: that our girls are like these flowers.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I dreamed, I dreamed that the pines were frowning: and my maiden's eyes darken. I dreamed, I dreamed of the eastern glow: and my maiden's cheeks blushed. I dreamed, I dreamed that dawn had broken: and my maiden's forehead was shining.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: People said that I am not a young man: I have a hat on the side and a green wreath. "People said that I am not a maiden: honesty blooms in me like rosemary."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: What do you have, my dear, what do you have on your forehead: when I kissed you, it blushed all over. What do you have, my dear, what do you have in your eyes: when I look into them, the world spins with me! What do you have, my dear, what do you have on your cheeks: when I do not see them, my heart aches.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My dear, my dear! You have black eyes: tell me, tell me, what do you wash them with? "I don’t wash them often, only once a day, in the stream with cold water." She stood by the brook, watering a peacock: tell me, my dear, who usually visits you? "I don’t know, I myself don’t know, but today I will find out: come to us in the evening, I will surely tell you."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: If I were not afraid, my dear, of your betrayal, I would go for apples in your garden. But I am afraid, afraid that you would betray me; that my service for a year would not be enough for me. "Do not be afraid, my dear, I will not betray you: with a red handkerchief, I will cover the garden window."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Horses, a black horse stands under the maple tree, and it is being groomed for the field by his dearest. "If I knew whose little horses these were, I would bring them some cold water. If I knew they belonged to my boy, I would have fed them some grainy oats."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Kalousek's horses drink clean water: be careful, Jeníček, so they don't kill you! If they killed you, it would be a pity: the miller’s daughter would cry for you.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: In our little garden, flowers grow nicely, the sun shines nicely into our windows. In the evening, a bright star appeared in the sky: my beloved came to the garden to see me. She came to the garden, sat down beside me; she didn’t stay long, quickly she ran away again. I don’t plow, I don’t sow, yet things grow for me: I have joy, she comes to me by herself. She doesn’t come, she doesn’t come, she came only once, like a little lamb to the green grass.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: If I could be where my thoughts are: there where my pleasure wanders! If the mountains were not an obstacle to me, I would see my beloved walking in the garden. Walking in the garden picking blue flowers: oh, only the Lord God knows if I will have you! Mountains, Black Mountains, step aside: let me have my beloved as my wife.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Red little rose, why don’t you bloom? Why, my Jeníček, why don’t you come to us anymore? "If I came to you, you would cry, wiping your eyes with a red handkerchief." Why should I cry when nothing hurts me? We loved each other like two doves. Like two little doves, like two turtledoves: I gave you sincere little kisses. Sincere little kisses – your love is false: I will not believe you until the announcement. Until the announcement at our rectory, then I will call your brother a scoundrel. Your brother a scoundrel, your mother a madam; I will kiss her hands every morning. Hands every morning, feet every evening: and all because you are still mine.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I had a sweetheart; he died on my lap: I had a little chest made for him in Kolín; the chest in Kolín, and the lid in Kostelec: so that you would know, false young men!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I was glad, Jeníček, I was glad to have you, until you started seeing other girls. Once you go to Aninka, another time to Marjánka: when you are a handsome boy, every girl wants you.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A log cabin well among the vineyards: tell me, my dear, whose advice do you follow? "Whose advice would I follow but my beloved's? For him, I must not love another."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The Roztocká pub is very tall, a wild goose flew over it. It flew over it only once a year, drank the water from the Roztocký creek. It drank the clear water, left the muddy one: this is what I endured for you, my dear! What I have already endured, what I will still endure: for you, my darling, I gladly endure everything!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The wild goose was flying high, drank water from our creek. It drank the clear water, leaving us the muddy: which is like a virgin's, if it is impure!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: If I did not perish when the war was on, I will not perish now that it is over. If I did not perish among the Austrians, I will not perish among my own masters. If I did not perish on the French fields, my lady will not perish in my hands either!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: If you had come before the moon rose, I would have given you a cherry for your little hat. A cherry for your little hat, a handkerchief for your pocket: so that you wouldn’t deceive honest girls.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: God grant, give moisture to our little village: I planted a bed of rosemary. I planted green, the yellow is coming out: that my Jeníček is walking with others. He walks, he walks – let him lead others: whatever the Lord God judges for me, it will not pass me by.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Those Zvíkov gardens are nicely turning green; my golden delight used to walk there. Walked, walks, will not walk there anymore: but only God knows whose little girl she will be! Whether she will be mine or yours, to whom God will give her, who is meant to pick that beautiful flower!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: For those, for those black eyes, truly I would jump over the Danube for you. Not the Danube, not the Danube, but I would sail across the whole sea for you, my delight!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation:
What’s wrong with you? What’s wrong with you? Are you angry with me, that you don’t even give me a few kisses goodnight?  
“I’m not angry, I’m not angry, but I’m sorry that you’re seeing someone else – that’s what they told me.”

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Whose horses are those on our eel? They have been grazing there since noon. If I knew they belonged to my beloved, I would drive them into the white clover. If I knew they belonged to my Jeníček, I would drive them into the nice cabbage.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The girl went to get water, she had a new vessel; a gentleman met her there and broke her jug. The girl went, crying, lamenting her jug: "Even if you are a gentleman, pay me for the jug!" "Be quiet, girl! Don’t cry, your loss will be compensated: for the green jug, I will give you a little dress." The girl did not want the dress, she only longed for her jug: "Even if you are a gentleman, pay me for the jug!" "Be quiet, girl! Don’t cry, your loss will be compensated: for the green jug, I will give you a ring." The girl did not want the ring, she only longed for her jug: "Even if you are a gentleman, pay me for the jug!" "Be quiet, girl! Don’t cry, your loss will be compensated: for the green jug, I will take you myself." The girl no longer longed, but she was happy: "For the green jug, I got the gentleman!"

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A guy from Moravia. I am a guy from Moravia, my dear, don’t believe me: I am like a bird, I love and then I leave it be.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: When I was herding geese, I was shivering from the cold: now that I don't herd geese, I no longer shiver from the cold. When I was little, my mother took care of me: now that I am grown up, I need a fiancé.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I am young, pretty, chubby, and very pale: whoever wants to know my name, they call me Lidunka. Sometimes I am good, sometimes I am bad, a single word can make me angry; whoever does not know me, let them come to us, let them take a look at me. I was supposed to get married last year, but my mother wouldn't allow it, saying I am still too young, so I had to leave it at that. Any nuisance better not trudge to our place, not break our doors, nor babble to me about love.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Who feeds my ponies when I am with my little girl? She didn’t want to let me go home until the stars appeared. The stars disappeared, I was still with my dear one: my ponies, unfed, whinnied unbearably. Be quiet, be quiet, ponies! I will cut you some grass so you won’t whinny at me when I am with my little girl.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The journey at night. No one knows like I do how long the journey at night is; no one knows like I do how cruel the journey at night is. Who feeds my little horses when I am not home at night? Who feeds my little horses when I am with my girl? Who clears my dishes when I am not home at night? Who clears my dishes when I am with my beloved?

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Tall juniper, as tall as I am: may you be mine, my maiden, may God grant you to me!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: That is why I bought the kanafaska, so that you, my Jeníček, would like me: in red, in green, in white, so that you wouldn’t go to another woman.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My dear went to the little grove, to the green one, where she met a blue-eyed young hunter. "Young hunter, please come to our place tomorrow, I have a white scarf at home, and I will give it to you." The young hunter opens the door, and she wipes her beloved's eyes. "Don't cry, don't cry, my maiden! I know my way to you: when the rye turns green, I will give you my hand."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: In front of the neighbors, geese are fighting by the creek: "Go, Jeníček, take the little gun, kill one of them." "I won’t kill the geese, I know them well: they are the lady’s geese, where I usually go. If I killed one, she would be angry; and if I came there afterwards, she would be upset."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A girl sits at the table, like a rose blossom, telling herself that she had five. One fell in love with her, the second took her away from him; and the third's heart aches because he did not write to her. The fourth stands under the window, wiping his eyes; and the fifth, during the music, twirls a handkerchief.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A red apple was rolling, rolling: who will get you, my golden girl? Two apples were rolling, rolling facing each other: who would I get to, if not to you, Jeníček? Who would I get to, if not to you, my dear: you are handsome, I am too, we will suit each other.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: On the little hill, a juniper, under the little hill flowers: how lovely it is to love when the moon is shining! On the little hill, a juniper, under the little hill grass: how lovely it is to love when there is a beautiful maiden!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Fly, my little messenger, like a bird to my delight, greet him a hundred times and let him know: That I love him faithfully, carry him in my heart: that he should come to us in the evening, that I kindly ask him.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I lost a slipper in the wheat, I lost a slipper in the rye: only you, my Honzíček, look for it for me, I lost the left slipper.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: In Strakonice, on the corner next to the gingerbread maker, I lost a slipper: go for the reeve! And you, Mr. Reeve! Act according to the law: I lost a slipper, I would like to find it.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The Austrian girl is better than the Moravian girl: the Austrian girl will give a kiss, but the Moravian girl does not want to.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Behind our little grove, fine rye is growing: my dear one is far away, I do feel sorry for him. I will go to meet him, take nice clothes, give him a sweet kiss and also my heart.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Sorrow, sorrow is coming, my heart is afraid: everyone is accusing me here, everyone is barking at me. Sister, mother forbid it, father even more: I certainly won’t leave you, my golden girl!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My dear cute one, what a pretty girl: her skirt is torn, and her little shirt is shining. My dear one does not have a skirt, but she will have one: green, red, whatever she wants.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The little doll went to the grove, caught a little bird there, put it in her handkerchief so she wouldn't crush it. "Let me go, let me go, golden child! I will sing to you: when you walk here with your beloved."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: In that little grove by the moonlight, a nightingale was singing: that Jeníček no longer loves Andulka. They loved each other for so long that they got angry: but shortly after, they met again.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Seeing off. It’s just a little while until day, come, my dear, see me off; come, my dear, come, come, see me off. Dawn is breaking, breaking into dawn, prepare, my dear, breakfast; come, my dear, come, come, see me off. It’s dawning, dawning, the day is white, I must leave my beloved now; come, my dear, come, come, see me off. See me off at the gate, my plump little maiden! come, my dear, come, come, see me off. See me off at the crossroads, wish me luck on the way; come, my dear, come, come, see me off. See me off on the path, give me a kiss for the road; come, my dear, come, come, see me off.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: It's raining, raining, just pouring down! Where shall we put the horses? Put them wherever you want, I'm sticking to the girl. Put them in the garden, I'm sticking to Marijánka; put them under the window – as long as the two of us don't get wet.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My doll serves in Prague, my heart longs for her: but I will not leave her there, I will come to get her.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation:
The doll went to confession, she had golden lacing, and the priest asked her who had laced her like that. "Father, why do you ask? You are only questioning me: I lace myself, and I love Honzík."  
"Stop, girl, with the love talk, better turn to repentance; though you won’t escape punishment, you will go to hell."  
"I'm not afraid of hell, only of losing what I cherish: having my Honzík, my faithful lover."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Behind our little barn, behind our barn, my doll was crying there, crying over me. "Don't cry, don't cry, don't lament, 1) I will come soon: early in the morning at dawn, before the sun rises." If you are to come tomorrow morning, better stay with me right now. You will cheer my little heart, you will also cheer yourself.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Behind that little barn of ours, behind our barn, my sweetheart was crying, saying I was seeing another woman. Don't believe it, my dear, don't believe anything anyone says: I only go to you, and I won't take another. I wouldn't go to you if you weren't beautiful: when I don't see you for a week, I long for you.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: In front of our little window stands a green linden tree: often, my dear girl, my little heart longs for you. People keep saying that the war will come again: I don't know, I don't know, my dear girl, what will happen with you.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I was in the military for fourteen years, and you still haven't married: who have you waited for, my dear, who have you been waiting for? "I waited, my boy! I waited for you: until you come home from the army, then we will be ours."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: When I see you, my maiden! kneeling in that church, I cannot serve God at all, I must look at you: if I loved God as much as I love you, I would long ago be a saint, like the angels from heaven.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The geese have not honked yet, it is not morning yet: the boy is still visiting, he will not leave from here yet.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Our little Honzíček, a small bud, was shuffling up and down the pole to the Zápotocký courtyard. Zápotocká saw it and told the old man: "I'll go there! If he's there, I'll give him a good thrashing!" Honza heard the scolding, left the girls' lovemaking, ran away, hurried so the old man wouldn't catch him.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Don't stand, girl, in the sun, protect yourself from the spring breeze: your face will change, freckles will catch you. She just laughed at it, after all, she stood in the sun: when she was expecting Toníček, she wasn't afraid of the sun. And when she met him, she preferred to go back to the shade; where the sun shone, she didn't like it so much.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Yesterday the sun was shining on me, today my hands are freezing: if you were honest, Honzíček, you would buy gloves. "You buy them for me, my dear, in the autumn, I'll buy them for you in the spring: when you start walking on the grass, so you don't catch a cold."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: She was sitting under the pine tree, crying because she had a poor little rosemary plant in the garden. She prayed for divine rain so that her little rosemary plant would grow in the garden.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A lively girl. Youth, joy! They won't let me grow up either: yesterday I passed fifteen years, I have already lied about nine. I won't give, I won't give, young men, I tell you, apples were not picked from the tree: you must wait for the right time.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: If it weren't daylight, the girl would have given me three or four kisses, she's a charming girl!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The girls are already coming from Lomeček, the girls are already coming from Lomec: they have red caps, they have green wreaths.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Tell me, oh my dearest! How will I come to you? Around the barn is a path, it will lead you to us. Around the barn is a path, it is a nice grassy one: when you come to us, my Honzík, sit in front of our gate. In front of our gate is a nice white stone: when you come to us, my Honzík, just sit on it. In front of our gate is a nice little stone: so that people will know that you are my lover.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Our little Adam! What are you doing? They are grazing you in the meadow, you are among the lads, you do not care about anything.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My little Adam! Keep to your word, what you promised me when you loved me, that you would be mine. "My little Andulka! It's not possible: just wait until spring, when the lilac blooms, then you will be mine. My lovely Andulka! My longest love until death – no one else."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My Matoušek, he carries manure: when he takes it out, the little horses will come out, then he will be mine. On the willow a bird, in the stream a crayfish, in the pond a fish: my golden girl, I love you!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My beloved is from Rájov: is she as healthy when she gets up in the morning as I am?

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Under the Prague bridge flows the water: give my little horse a drink, my dear, my lovely little mouth. "I won't give him a drink, I'm afraid of him: he would trample me, he would trample me, who would heal me?" The Prague doctors are physicians: they would heal you, my pleasure, in two hours.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Karlíček, Karel! Don’t say to me: pears are ripening, apples too, when they ripen, I will give you some too, come, Karlíček, to them.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: When you come to us, don't come alone: take a friend with you, the lady will be glad, through the green grove. In the green grove they are waiting for you: they will kill you for Ančička, bury you under the fir tree, there they will leave you.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Under our windows, blackberries are growing: tell me, Ančička, my golden little heart! Who was there today? "Josífek was there, standing under the window." – Tell me, Ančička, my golden little heart: what did he ask you? "He called, he knocked on the window: Open up, don’t slumber, open up, don’t slumber, my kiss! And I would gladly open for you: if my little head didn’t ache because of my little boy. But it doesn’t hurt you as much as you act: that you have someone else there, my kiss, my golden little heart! Who would I have here? I am here alone: only the lady lies here with me in the little bed by the door."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Where, my love, where have you been, that you were not here? Without you, I live in longing in my youth – so where did you go? "Through mountains, through forests, through towns and villages, I went everywhere, I did not let myself sleep, I troubled my heart."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Třeboň, Třeboň! What is that flickering there? I will go there too, I will try to convince myself, there is a pretty girl there.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I have a nice picture, I know what I will do with it: it has two hearts on it – I will give one to the girl, and keep the other for myself. There is joy in Hrazánky, there are plenty of girls there: may God grant me to soon be able to help one of them from here!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: When you leave, stop by, I'll give you a piece of lungwort, a little bouquet from the brook: maybe I'll catch you, charming girl!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: In our little garden, a pretty bird is hopping; lapwings are ringing, people are praying: go home already, boy! 1) "I won't go home, I'll stay here a little longer: just a little moment, until you give me a kiss, then I will go home." Around, girl, around, beneath that noble manor, I walked all around, yet I did not find anyone equal to my maiden.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Which is the wicked love, when a girl has two: one loves her, the other promises her – they leave her in uncertainty.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My dear, my sweet, do this for me: knock with your knuckle and tip your hat, make a bow. When you visit a maiden, greet her parents, then sit quietly, the maiden’s heart sighs that you do not speak to her. My dear, my sweet! don’t do that to me, to go after another when you already have your beloved, be sincere with me.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: In the little grove in the valley on a nice plain, there is my beloved, dear Andulka, serving wine. Jeníček came there, sat down at a little table; he sighed deeply and lamented to himself, my dear boy! Don’t sit, don’t sit, come, help, pour! So people wouldn’t say, so they wouldn’t notice, that you used to be my beloved. "I didn’t come to you to pour wine: I came for love, my dear picture, to love you. When I was in custody in that town of Klatovy, nothing troubled me there when my beloved girl came to visit me. When I was in prison for seven years underground, nothing was wrong with me when my delight came to visit me. My delight! help me out of prison." I will help you out of prison, but I cannot be yours – it’s not possible, no!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The lapwing flew out of Vysoké Mýto, and she passes on to me that a black-eyed girl sends her regards. She was black-eyed, sewing white dresses, a red kerchief, a green wreath, my dear little doll. Janeček came there, all tired, sighed deeply, lamented sadly, and sat down at the little table. "Don’t sit, don’t sit, come, help, pour, so people won’t talk and blame us, that you are my dear." "I’m not going to pour, but I’m going to court you: if it is God’s will, we will be together, so it can happen." When I was walking through the meadow past the juniper, I met my dear, my beloved girl; her heart was aching.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My darling little parrot! Where have you been? "I was at the pilgrimage at Saint Dorothea's, there I prayed. For none other than my beloved: that I might have him as my husband in the white bed."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: The poured swallow says that dawn is breaking: go home, girl, I cannot help you, I would be beaten. I would be beaten by my mother: for not shearing, for not giving green fodder to the cows. "You will not be beaten, you will not go to the grass," the dew, the cold water, said, "it’s no lie."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: She was sitting by our gate, fair white as a dress: look, maiden, look, you are losing your beauty by not going to sleep alone. "That is not true at all, I always sleep alone in our chamber, no one can come there but our lady mistress."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: At our little stable, water flows from the rocks: whoever tastes this water must go to the maiden, even if she is under locks.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Longing. Blow, breeze, in the direction where my beloved serves: convey greetings to my joy, my heart longs for there.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Oh you lovely black eyes! How I long for you: if I do not get you, I will not serve here anymore.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Do not go, my dear, into the forest, cold dew is falling; your shoes are old, completely torn, you would have to go barefoot.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: What is that nightingale singing from midnight? How beautifully the sound resonates! It perches on a little branch, singing a song to be heard by its dear female.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I have a girl, she wants me, she has black eyes: when she sees me, she already feels shy and immediately covers them.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Ančička, get up, I am already coming to you: it struck half past three in the Monastery; Ančička, get up, I am already coming to you. Someone is coming to us, I do not know him: he has blue little eyes, rosy little cheeks, when he comes to us, then I will recognize him.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I stand under the windows, soaking wet like a mouse: dear lady! Open the gate for me, if you may. "I would gladly let you into our little room; but we don't have any lights in the whole building." "Put a penny on the candle, my dear, I will add two: then we will enjoy ourselves together until dawn."

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A little bird flew onto a juniper, plucking moss: that my darling has sweet lips like honey. I have traveled around four countries, a fifth village: yet I have not found anyone equal to my darling until now.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Sing, nightingale! Wake up my little mouth, it sleeps beside the door: so that it does not sleep, it rises early, for it is already getting dusky.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Above Strakonice, by the road, there grow three roses: one is blooming, the second is finishing blooming, the third cannot bloom. If I could perhaps pick one flower from those three roses: I would leave all my friends – for the boy, the whole world.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Where shall we go for a walk, my dear? "To a pretty garden, you and I." Don’t argue with me, my dear! What shall we do there, my dear? "Pick nice flowers, you and I." Don’t argue with me, my dear! Where shall we put them, my dear? "In a white handkerchief, you and I." Don’t argue with me, my dear! What shall we make from them, my dear? "To weave a pretty wreath, you and I." Don’t argue with me, my dear! Where shall we go to the party, my dear? "To our pub, you and I." Don’t argue with me, my dear! What shall we do there, my dear? "Drink a mug of beer, you and I." Don’t argue with me, my dear! Where shall we go to sleep then, my dear? "Each to our own bed, you and I." Don’t argue with me, my dear! And when we wake up in the morning, my dear? "We will kiss here, you and I." Don’t argue with me, my dear!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Those monastery bells, they are worthy bells, they ring beautifully: their hearts made of silver, of gold, they attract boys to us.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My little Andulka, you gave me no peace, you wouldn't let me sleep at night; you wouldn't let me sleep, I had to get up, I had to love you. I came home when the moon rose, the farmer had fed the animals. My golden farmer! I was captured by a handsome girl.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Ančička the shepherdess, don’t tell anyone anything: not even the lady, that we know each other; don’t tell anyone anything, Ančička the shepherdess!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My dear! The beloved is coming to us: what kind of dinner shall I prepare for him? Dinner is ready, roast mutton, beer brought for my pleasure. My dear! The beloved is coming to us, what kind of bed shall I prepare for him? An oak bed, a feather quilt, snow-white bedding for my pleasure.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A little oak tree is growing, oh oak tree! on a slender hill: wait for me, my golden maiden! I am still very young. "I would wait for you, oh I would wait! but you will not take me: you will mount a black horse and go off to the army!"

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I stood by the window, I stood, calling to my beloved: arise, my dear, gently, come closer to the little window, arise, maiden! And she rises, rises, wraps her skirt around; she approaches the window, looks at her beloved, golden girl. She speaks in a quiet voice, speaks to him sweet words: "Where have you been, my golden Venclíček! Why didn’t you come for a moment to our home? The gate is already closed, closed, daddy has the keys; I must not go to him for them, I cannot open for you, golden child!" If only I had the wings of a little bird, a nightingale! I would soar upwards: I would fly to the window, fly into the little chamber to my heart.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: For a beloved, nothing is difficult; for a beloved, I long to the same extent. My dear has a white coat, but I am not allowed to take it because he is a soldier: I would gladly take it if my mother did not forbid me. When he comes to us, he says nothing, and when he leaves, I am immediately beaten: I don’t mind the beating, only when I have my beloved with me!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: It was one o’clock past midnight when my beloved saw me off: “Go through that green grove already, my dear, go alone, go with God!” There are not as many little stars in the sky as my beloved gave me kisses, there are not and will never be, as long as the world exists, so many little stars. “If my mother had sent me to a convent while I was still little, I would not know Honzík now.”

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Meissen apples – let's get to them: we will cook them in cream; we will invite Mařenka, my dear sweetheart, to join us as well. My dear Mařenka is sleeping in the pantry; Jeníček came to her and woke her up: "Drink, my Mařenka, my dear sweetheart! To my health." "Why should I drink to your health? I'm not your sweetheart." "You are, my Mařenka, my dear sweetheart, no one else." "But people have told me that you used to see someone else?" "Don't believe it, my Mařenka, my dear sweetheart! People lied!"

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: By our little door, by our gate, a nightingale sings there many times during the night; it sings beautifully, as its song spreads out many times during the night.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Lenorka. I have a girl, a pretty girl, a nice girl named Lenorka: she is a girl like a flower, a joy to look at; she is a girl like made of wax, I would marry her immediately!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: A young budgerigar cannot be loved, it runs away from me: I run after it, I fell, I lie down; I bruised both my knees, they hurt.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: Gladly, gladly, my dear Honzíček! Gladly, gladly I love you! "Is it true that you love me so much?" Gladly, gladly, I love you!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: I would go to my beloved, but I must not, the path has overgrown with reeds; once I cut down the reeds, I will not leave you there, my dear. I would go to my beloved, I do not know where, the path has overgrown here and there; once I untangle the here and there, I will not leave you there, my dear. I would go to my beloved through the forest, reeds have grown on my path; once I break the reeds, I will take both the daughter and the mother.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: When you come this way, stop by: I will give you a little bouquet of mallow; a little green bouquet: if you are so sincere, come to me.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My mother always told me to avoid young men: yet I have them in my heart: after all, if the young men are good boys. When I went to Sunday morning mass, a young man met me for love: a handsome face, blue eyes – it’s a wonder my little heart didn’t jump out!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: My dear fell ill because I didn’t buy her the trimming: I will go there, I will heal her, I am not afraid of this illness at all!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: When I was walking a week ago from the feast, I met a maiden I did not know: she was pretty, nicely pale; she had little red apples in her hands. I was a foolish boy, I took the apples, they were good; as soon as I ate them, I immediately went pale: return to me, my dear, my beauty now. “I would return your beauty to you, if the dove had not flown away with it; it flew away into the grove: may the Lord protect you, my lad!” I told you these words: that you do not want just one, that you want both; you cannot want more than one: either leave me, maiden, or him!

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
translation: In our little orchard, there is grass, no one cuts it, it is very fine, it is very fine, it just creeps: my beloved walks me along it. Lend me, my maiden, a little lantern, so I can light the path, to see if it is dry or muddy: the path shines like gold.

label: Písně věku mládeneckého a panenského
