"Don't join the army, don't sulk your parents." "I will join the army, I will sulk my parents. When I am standing on guard, come to see me. I will jump on the horse, wave a white scarf. I bid you farewell nicely, my dear, I bid you farewell nicely."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: The soldiers are heading to the mountains, where will I hide, mother? "Hide wherever you want, my son, if you do not want to listen to me."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: They searched me thoroughly, caught me for that army draft: now they have me, now they are leading me, the contribution awaits with its measure.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I will not wander at night, I will rather imagine a lovely dark-eyed girl, who has supported hips. I will not beg virgins, I will rather carry a saber, a new steel saber, gifted by the queen.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I went through Louny, they were recruiting for the dragoons: those green dragoons, they are my delight. I didn't know what to do, whether I should also saddle a horse: horseshoes clattered, black eyes were crying. Black eyes, why do you cry that you won’t have him? Don’t cry for him, the Lord God will give you another.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I was leaving Miletín, I was caught near Jičín, soldiers caught me and asked me: Where are you from? I was born in Miletín, I have my pleasures there; and I must go there again to visit my beloved.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: If you had listened to your father, spoken kind words to your mother, you wouldn’t be in the army, you wouldn’t be grooming horses. "I listened to my father, spoke kind words to my mother: yet I groom horses, I enjoy being a soldier."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I went through the gate, I met a maiden there: she was, she was my dear little maiden. I began to ask where her mother was. "Just ask, watch out for the beautiful green house." They were drumming on two drums when they were recruiting Honzík; they drummed, blew horns, until they lured him away from me.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I wander off, whom will I complain to? Play for me, trumpet for me, play me a march! I will complain to my sweetheart, she says she doesn’t want me! Play for me, trumpet for me, play me a march!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Go, soldiers, go lie down, for you have nothing to eat for dinner; go, soldiers, go to sleep, for you have nothing to do.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Mountains and forests, my dear, where are you? They have already tied my hair with a ribbon. A white ribbon, for my dear, because I loved a different one every day. I never thought I would be a soldier, that I would have to march away from you. They took me by force, my dear, at night, I had no help with me. The drums were beating, the music was playing; I was leaving, my dear was sleeping. People ask, who is the music for, who are they saddling their horses so sadly for? Don’t ask anything, just play for me, just saddle the horses for me all the more.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Oh son, son! Are you home? Daddy is asking if you plowed? I plowed, I plowed, but little: my plowshare broke. I plowed, I plowed the white path, the one I used to walk to my beloved. Is that the trodden path? Is my beloved crying? Is that the hard path to plow? We can no longer be united!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: The love is already ignited: they put a rifle on my shoulder. The love is already in flames: they put a saber on my belt.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Let's have a drink, boys, let's have a drink: after all, they're already tailoring a coat for us in Prague. And in Kutná Hora, a waistcoat: because each of us had a pretty girl!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: If I have hurt any of you, any of you, for God's sake forgive me, I am leaving, I am leaving. If I have hurt anyone, anyone, for God's sake forgive me, I am leaving the house, I am leaving the house.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: The young son was leaving before daybreak, his beloved was looking out the window. "Where are you going, my dear boy, where are you going? You won't even sit here with us." "When my time has come, my dear: friends are waiting for me to go." "What will you give me, my dear, for the journey?" "That little ring you have on your finger." "That ring is hard for me to give: but even harder to leave you behind!"

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: If there were a war, I would be glad: the emperor would have to give me a horse; a black horse, a saddle on it: 1) goodbye maidens, God be with you!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I was walking from Krumlov along that little path, oh dear! along that little path, I met a very pretty girl, oh dear! very pretty. I started courting her: her mother began to scold her. "Don’t want him, my daughter, he’s a soldier: he has white pants, a white coat!" Are you reproaching me for that, mother? You have sons for that too!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: New times have come for us: they brought the uniform on two carts; on one, coats and cartridge belts, on the other, caps and sabers. The officers are heading to the assembly: that fathers must go with their sons; each son says he will not go: "You go there, daddy! What will it be?" You must go, son, without delay; orders have arrived for us even from Vienna: you will receive a special hat, a gift from the emperor. "My dear daddy! pay me: I will obey you first; pay me, dearest daddy! I will obey you even better."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: My mother raised me with difficulty, passing me from hand to hand. They were rocking me in the cradle, already talking to me about the army. I haven't even grown up yet, and I am to become a soldier: since they have already tied me up, what can I do? They tied me up, tied me up with ropes so I wouldn’t go after girls. But I will untie myself, I will go after all, for my girl is a fine maiden. My golden girl sleeps in the little room, oh, I wish I could speak to her! I will go, knock on the little window: Get up, come open for me, my dear! Open, open, while I wait, while I still do not wear a white uniform. "Would I gladly open for you if my head didn’t hurt." Surely your head doesn't hurt as you claim, but that in your heart you have another! The groves are turning nice and green, others are courting my girl. Let them court her if they want: just let them not kiss her in front of me. If they embraced her in front of me, they would make my heart heavy. Turn around, little sun, over those forests: my girl no longer comforts me. Although she comforted me, she will no longer: she will comfort me somewhere else!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: What is that sparkling behind the gate? The boys from Horažďovice are going to the army. May God give them luck so they arrive safely, and that none of them fall from their horse into the water. None of the others fall, only the miller’s son: Anna the blacksmith’s daughter will cry for him.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Unhappy deceiver, that’s what they called me! They took me to the army, I had to go; they took me to the army because of my beloved, that I loved a different one every day.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Boleslav, Boleslav, what a beautiful town: do not cry, do not wail, do not lament, my bride! "How then should I not, sadly, weep, lament, when I see you dressed in military uniform. Those military clothes torment my heart!" Do not cry for me, do not lament, we will meet again. "How can we meet again when you are leaving? What will you send me then, my lad, what will you send me?" I will send you more than one letter in a year: so that you always trust my faithful love.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Jeníček is brushing the black horses in the stable, looking aside to see who will bring him what. They are bringing you, Jeníček, two handcuffs and a lock: the emperor is giving you a betrothal gift, Jeníček. Be careful, Jeníček, when you leave the maiden, the lords of Novodvorský are waiting for you there. The lords of Novodvorský like you: they will give you a black horse and a saber. – And I escaped from them all the way to the white court, there I climbed a high mountain for myself. There I slept until daybreak, until my lady called me to breakfast.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: The sidewalks, Lučice fenced around: they took me to the army for my pleasure. They took me, gave me a black horse: and I will jump up, sit on it.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Hussars, hussars! painted children: when he mounts a horse, he flies like a bird. As his trousers are, so are his shoes: he comes into the pub, dancing without a note.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Hussars, hussars! Painted children: where is the maiden flying after you. Hustopeče, Hustopeče is a beautiful little town: when we arrive there, we drink a little wine. The Hustopeče gate is nicely painted: hussars walk there with maidens.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Hussars, hussars, you have fine horses: I will ride with you, which one will you give me? Which one other than the black one? I will saddle him and mount him. I will saddle him with white covers so you will recognize me when I come with the soldiers. I will saddle him with a red saddlecloth so you will recognize me when it is before the battle. I will saddle him with green brocade so you will recognize me when we march. When we march three hundred miles beyond Prague: there, my dear, I will see the Turkish army. When we march three hundred miles beyond Vienna: there, my dear, I will see the whole Turkish land. The whole Turkish land, that foreign country: there I, my dear, there I will perish.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: What were you looking for, my dear! How did you recognize me? "By the black horse I was riding. I rode it with red brocade: so I would recognize you when you became a soldier. I rode it with green cloth: so I would recognize you before the battle."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: That castle in Lužany is very tall: climb it, my dear, look at me. Look at me, see what kind of coat I am wearing: so you know that I am not begging you. I am not begging, I am not begging, I will not beg, so you know that I will not be yours.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I used to be cheerful when going out, as it blossomed beneath our flowers. Now I face a sorrowful farewell, as I must be deprived of my pleasure. Who is taking it from me? The military authorities: they wrote a note that I must go to the field. To the wide field, before the Emperor: my beloved cried until the white dawn. Do not sit, my dear! do not sit beside me, so that your tears do not fall on me. "Leave me, my darling! let me sit here so that my little heart can soften."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Many thanks to my dear mother for raising me in a soft, cozy blanket. She raised me, raised me, not knowing for whom: when she had raised me, they took me to the army. They took me to the army without even asking, although my elders had a hard time raising me!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: If you had listened to your mother, Honzíček, you wouldn’t be carrying a little saber at your side now. You wouldn’t have a saber and a black horse: you wouldn’t have listened to a stranger.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When you did not want to retrieve me from the military service: now look at the troubles I have. When you came to me, I was standing guard first: now look at what I have already endured. What I have already endured, what I still will endure: all of that, my dear, I am gladly enduring for you.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: It's a shame a hundred times that the blood red will be spilled on the green grass! On the green grass, on the cold dew: until my little horse carries me across the field. I will not die on the ground, I will die on the horse: as soon as I fall off it, the saber will ring for me!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Kumbur's little castle on the round hill: I got a nice black horse. The trumpeters sounded, horseshoes clattered: The girls of Jičín were crying for us. Don’t cry, girls, and you my acquaintances: we won’t go alone, we will all go together. There will be counts and lords: many a mother will have to cry. Many a mother, many a sister, the one who has her brother in the army. “Thank you, boy, for your service: may God grant you a safe return!” Thank you, girl, for loving me: may God grant you a good husband!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Green dragoons! Where have you disappeared to? As if you had never been in Brandeis! Green dragoons! Where have they taken you from here? You marched against the Frenchman. Green dragoons! You will long for it when the French sabers flash at you!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Young men, young men! What have you come to, that your hair was cut in Prague? They cut your hair, put on a white coat, and drove you straight to the Hungarians. Hungarians, Hungarians! What do you think you are doing, killing Czech boys so cruelly?

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I, a little Hussar, with torn shoes: I will not go to the army until I have new ones. Father heard this, came out of the cell; he had the shoes made, he had to go to the army.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I am a little hussar among the hussars: the hussars are a company, and I am alone. If there were still two hundred of those hussars, I would stand up to them like a brave girl.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I’m dancing with you for the last time, my dear: tomorrow morning I’ll march away from here. When I become a soldier, a green dragoon: I’ll be riding on a black horse!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: That Strahov Gate, nicely painted, beneath it my girl is all in tears. Why are you crying, lamenting, when you still have me here? You can cry for me later, when you fail to watch over me. Instead of my home, I have a black horse, a saber, and a carbine, and a saddle for it. Instead of beautiful girls, a few pistols on it: Adie, my dearest, stay here with the Lord God! I’ll jump on my little horse, wave my scarf: Adie, my dearest! I take my leave of you. I’m sitting on my little horse, gazing sadly: don’t cry for me, my girl! That I won’t see you. Don’t look back at me or your eyes will fall out: a pity for your blue eyes, to whom will they go!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: My parents want me to be a priest: but I have decided to be a tailor. It's no use, my dear father! It's no use complaining: and I will not be a priest, you'll see. If I had to preach sermons: pretty girls would come, and I would have to laugh. If I had to confess a girl: I might make a mistake, I would want to embrace her. If I had to visit a sick person at night: I might lose my way to a pretty girl.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Beautiful little stars, how small you are! How you comforted me those whole nights! One of you the most, the bright morning star: she always used to lead me to my dear little darling. Moon in the clouds, how high you are! How far my dear little darling is from me! My dear daddy always used to tell me that I would enjoy a commission sandwich. My dear little mother always used to tell me that one day I would like the water from the pond. My dear little brother always used to tell me that the black horse would suit me well. My dear little sister always used to tell me that the little saber by my side would look good on me. My dear friends always used to tell me that one day the enemy in the field would kill me too. I will go to the field, and in bloody battle once again I will remember my little darling. They dug a little grave in that green grove: once again greet my dear little darling for me. Farewell now, my joy! Don’t cry, don’t whine, don’t lament, rather pray.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: How nicely the state guards when the moon is shining: it is even better to love a girl like a flower.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Oh dear lady! Why then are you so strong that you let soldiers with clappers stand under the windows? The soldiers with clappers, the night watchmen with lanterns, and the recruiters with shackles are already standing by the gates. The recruiters say that I am pleasing to the emperor; they put shackles on my feet and took me to Prague. A powdered head, a little sword at the side: there my beloved is wiping her eyes!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Oh, it hurts me, my little heart hurts in my body: yet they have long told me that the authorities will take me away. The aldermen, the reeves are walking around me, and my dearest one is watching to see if they will soon load me up. I am already lying on the wagon, heavy shackles on my feet: hitch four black horses, we will go to Prague. In Prague, they are waiting for me, they will give me a white coat; and those curly locks of mine, they will cut them there. I will still look towards the Vyšehrad gardens to see if my dearest is there somewhere, I will call for her. But I neither see her there, nor hear her there: with whom then, my golden maiden, with whom then will I find comfort!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: On our little saddle, on our little saddle, my Jeniček turned, turned on the horse. He turned back and forth, 1) he turned back and forth: "To whom shall I leave you here, my maiden? I will leave you to my little brother, I will leave you to my brother: if you cannot be mine, you will be to a friend."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I was leaving my beloved, the sun was high: look, my maiden, how far I already am. "You are far, my dear boy! You are far from me: but if you are so sincere, you will come back to me!" I was three fields away from her when she still called after me: "Forgive me, my dearest, for whatever I have angered you!" You angered me once, you won't anger me anymore: I will ride my little horse, and you will stay here alone. "I won't stay, I won't stay, and I can go with you: my parents are in the graveyard, they won't know anything. My unkind brother and sister won't stop me: I will ride my little horse and come to you."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Departure. When I was leaving my dearest, the moon was shining high, oh God! the moon was shining high: look, my golden maiden, how far I already am, oh God! how far I already am. I was four furlongs of fields away from her, she still called after me, oh God! she still called after me: "Stay here, my golden lad! stay with us until morning, oh God! stay with us until morning." I began to make excuses to her that I had to feed the horses, oh God! that I had to feed the horses: she began to wipe her blue little eyes, oh God! her blue little eyes. Why then are you crying and lamenting yourself, when you still have me here? oh God! when you still have me here: only cry and lament yourself when you can no longer watch over me, oh God! when you can no longer watch over me.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: The road to Prague is long, the road is long there: I took you, my maiden, I took you into my heart. And into my heart very fiercely, I cannot let you go: and now I must leave you here, I will go to the army alone.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: In Prague, it was a quarter to five at the town hall when I volunteered for my golden maiden. When luck does not want to serve me, it moves away from me: what my heart loved, it will not love anymore. Where is the handkerchief I gave you, the one I used to wear, into which I embroidered my name in the corner? Take it out of your pocket, put it on the fire, let the ashes scatter in the air: so that people cannot say that I was once your beloved.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Behind our little barn, behind our barn, my girl was crying there, she was crying over me. Why are you crying and lamenting? I cannot help you: in Olomouc, they are recruiting for the army in the two squares.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Fly, little bird, over those forests, greet my parents there: so they don't cry for me, that I have a cheerful heart. Fly, little bird, over those forests, greet my beloved there: so she doesn't wait for me, that I already have another one again.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: A little dove flew over the manor barns, hooted, cried, 1) because the boy was taken away from her. Don't cry, don't cry, don't lament, I will still marry you: in a year, in two, in three years, when I come back from the army. That is why I joined the army, I liked the army: to see how the French fight with ours. Come on, musicians! With courage, we will go into battle: Queen Theresa will be watching us.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: That's why I joined the army, I liked the army: look, girl, how the green jacket suits me. That green jacket and red epaulettes, a yellow cap, golden tassels and silver buttons.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: With you, girl, I rejoiced, with you, girl, I was happy, as long as I carried you in my heart, as long as I carried you in my heart. Look, my darling, I am already a soldier, 1) look, my darling, I am already a soldier: since you did not want to love me, 2) I must march. If I had known that I would get you, if I had known that you would be mine, I would have bought you a golden ring, my lovely darling!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Our people always cared about what kind of wife they would give me: they gave me a saber at my side, and even pinned it on me.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Embroidered scarf. When I passed by the little house where my beloved dwells, oh God! where my beloved dwells: she is sitting at her little table, embroidering a scarf with gold, oh God! embroidering a scarf with gold. It is good for you, my golden maiden, to embroider a scarf with gold, oh God! to embroider a scarf with gold: but we poor soldiers must march now, oh God! we must march.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: The soldiers are riding, riding, over there beyond the mountain; they met a girl with a green bouquet and took her with them.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I always thought that military service was a joke: that I would be there for a week or two weeks, then come home again. A week, two weeks, or an hour: ah, only the Lord God knows, my dear parents, where I will perish! If I perish among the Turks or the French: how heavy-hearted my mother will be for her darling! Not so much my mother, but my beloved, the one who always carried me in her heart all this time. When he came to the gate, he twisted his handkerchief: Return to me, my beloved, dear Andulka, what I lent you. “I cannot return it, I am not obliged: let another one at the military return it to you, my golden Jenik.”

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: If you want to know, girl, what kind of house is mine: under that wide sky, those distant fields, that is my house. If you want to know, girl, what kind of food I have: roasted horse meat and Danube water, that is my food. If you want to know, girl, what kind of bed I have: green grass, white clover, that is my bed. If you want to know, girl, what kind of blanket: I put a bag under my head, cover myself with a cloak, that is my blanket!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I always look back at the Pušperské gardens; I always look back to see if I can spot you there, so I can call you over. Come here, come here, my girl! I have something to tell you: that they are recruiting in Prague, that they are recruiting in Prague, they want to take me there too.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I am an imperial boy, who are you girls nicely: I will recruit you, whoever wants to give herself to me, I will teach you German.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I ride through that forest, only you, my little horse, carry me nicely: step and step, just don’t stomp, carry me to my dearest at the very end.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Nothing comforts me here except the manor garden, where I used to walk, lead my maiden, who wept sadly. Why do you cry, lament, when you still have me here? You will only cry and mourn sadly when you no longer watch over me.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: What have I done to you, local peasants! That you took me, sent me to the army for my dearest? The wide, distant Klášter field: on it one walks when the sun rises, my pleasure.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: We are already riding off to war, riding off: take good care of yourselves here! But you will soon remember us, oh my dear mother, a hundred times! Oh, little brother, my brother, my brother! Saddle the horse, for I must leave, away from my sweetheart. When you have killed my brother, killed him, you will kill me too: take care of yourselves, and your children. When you have killed my brother, killed him, you will kill us both: the water will carry us around Jarošov. It will carry us, carry us, carry us on the fir-wood raft: the dark-eyed girl will cry and wail.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: That little church in Sebranice, surrounded by a dark forest: I will look once more to see if I can spot whether my beloved is coming by. Ah, my beloved is coming, riding that black horse, fastening a little saber to his left side. After fastening the saber, he stood in front of his house; he wept and grieved until the horse cried out beneath him. Ah, my dear mother! Do you not recognize me, that you let me and my horse stand so long in front of our house? When mother heard this, she ran out into the street: "Welcome, my son! Who sent you to the army, who caused this?" Our lord of Sebranice made me join the army: they took me to the army, tied me with ropes, and set a guard over me. And that guard stood by me from evening till morning; and as God gave the day, as God gave the day, they took me to Brno. When they brought me to Brno, they stopped at the gate: "Open the gates, we bring you a recruit, a subject of Sebranice." Ah, my dear mother! I beg you sincerely, do not harm and do not hurt my young wife. If God grants her a son or a fair daughter this year, I will send her from the army an embroidered pillow, embroidered pillow and a pink cradle cover: here you have it as a memento, my precious dear, that I loved you.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: My doll lies in the grave, my little heart longs for her; play for me, trumpet for me, play me a march: I am leaving you now. When I wander away from here, whom shall I grieve for? play for me, etc. I will grieve for my mother, I will give a kiss to my little sister: play for me, etc.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Grove – grove – green little grove! I have already been caught for the army: they took me for the army, tied me with ropes, sent me far and wide into the world. Grove – grove – green little grove! I already have a saber by my side: a steel saber, she is my wife, she will strike me down when I need it.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: They took me into the army as a virgin and gave me a white coat: a bright white little coat, red lining, that they gave me. I received a black horse, nicely saddled: I will be glad of it when I go out into the wide fields from here.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: That servant of ours blows the horn long and nicely cheerfully: he will be trumpeting for us when we march before the enemy.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: What have I done to you, gentlemen neighbors! What have I done to you that you took me, accused me, and the lord captain handed me over? Play for me, gentlemen musicians, play me up! My lady says not to play because I must go to the army. Me to the army, my dear lady! I will not go to the army: for these expensive clothes, embroidered with gold, I will not become a soldier!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I will not be here, truly I will not, I will not serve here: my dear, lovely girl will long for me. I will not be here, truly I will not walk this path: my dear, lovely girl will cry for me. Goodbye, my dear, lovely girl, I am mounting my horse: perhaps I will never watch over you again, my beloved heart. I will ride somewhere to the mountains and forests, to a foreign land: but I want you, the only one my heart has ever loved.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: My little darling! The horses are already leading me, we will ride through the forests: you used to be mine before they gave me the horses, but now you are no longer mine. So what am I supposed to do, live on and long for you, I know I won't get you: after all, I have as many girls as there are weeds on the Varvažov side.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I do not regret serving the emperor: after all, I am better off than a shoemaker or tailor; I do not regret serving the emperor. The stable boys have horses, they saddle them nicely; cannons and muskets stand near the village: pikemen, get ready! The heart trembles in the body.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I walked along the path distressed, I sat down by the green stream: there I wept bitterly and lamented to myself for my sorrow. When I walked along the path to Jičín, I knew the cause of it: such a cause that you would no longer be mine, that I was already going to the military draft.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: She peered out of the little window at the very top, saw her beloved on a black horse; she immediately opened the gate for him, greeted him respectfully, and took his hand. "I welcome you, my dearest, I welcome you, my beloved: my parents are glad to see you; the sword at your side gleams, with it you are bound until death – until they kill you!" Yet not everyone has to be killed in that army: God Almighty in heaven will protect me; when I am with the army, my friends will be there, they will defend me.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: They still rocked me in the cradle, oh dear! rocked me, already telling me about that war, oh dear! telling me: my girl, don’t cry for me, I enlisted voluntarily.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Unfortunate lantern, it went out on me; before I turned around, the guard arrived: they took me and tied me up, put me in jail for my beloved. A day passed, a second day, the third as well, my beloved began to inquire about me: where I was and what I was doing, why I was not visiting my maiden.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: What happened to you then, my dear? That you have changed so completely; that you are sad, all pale: has your head started to hurt? How could my head not hurt? There is a great war around Tábor: if they take you, my dear girl, I will have sorrow in my heart.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: My dear Daddy! I beg you, buy me out of the army, after all, I am your son. After all, I am your son, your child: if you would buy me out of the army? If my dear mother were alive, she would buy me out of the army. After all, I am your son, etc. My dear mother! You sleep soundly, you know nothing of my suffering. After all, I am your son, etc. If you, mother, only knew, the coffin would open for you! After all, I am your son, etc.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: The soldiers are already behind Čimelice: they tied my hair with a ribbon, a white ribbon, that’s for my beloved, because I loved a different one every day.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When we mounted our horses, back then the girls cried for us. Girls, don’t cry for us: we’ll drive away the Turk and come back again.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I did not say today or a week ago that I would be a soldier, that I would carry a bayonet on my rifle; for the pleasure of a white strap: don't cry, my darling! it is of no use. When I go away from here, you will see how my beloved will carry a scarf for me; a little red scarf and love is in it: if you did not want me, what do I care about it!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I pass through Budějice here, I will send my greetings to my maiden: if she is not too proud to come outside, she came to say goodbye to me for the last time.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: You will no longer, my golden maiden, you will no longer deceive me: I will mount my horse, ride into battle; you will look after me sadly!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: You don't care when you have a small sword at your side, you don't care when you have a sword at your side: a small sword at your side, a rifle on your shoulder; you don't care when you have a sword at your side.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: She always just asked where the army was stationed, saying that she would like to be a soldier: that she would take a little bag, some schnapps in it, and sell it for pennies.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: The hussars are already gone, they marched away, the hussars are already gone, they left: the hussars left, the Stukknachts arrived, the girls were crying!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: They keep giving me what I don't like, they keep giving me what I don't want: they give me an old, ugly, pocked one; I don't want what I don't like! It's better, father, to ride a black horse, it's better, father, to go to the army than to marry and have an ugly wife; it's better, father, to be a soldier.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I will not go to the army, nor will I travel, until His Majesty the Emperor gives me a horse: a black little horse, I will sit on it, and no one will guard me until death.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: The Klášterská land is nicely green, green, green, green it is: look at me, my beloved, how my black horse carries me so well! I mount my little horse, I bid farewell to my beloved: Give me your right hand, my dear! She gave me her hand, she cried sadly: "How could I possibly ride with you!"

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I go here, I won’t look around, I have no more pleasure here: my pleasure is my saber for battle – to you, my dear, I forget! I mount my horse, I look around once more, whom I have angered, for that I ask: I ask you, those who are here, now forgive me – I probably won’t guard you ever again.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I heard, I saw ponies neighing, I heard, I saw horses neighing: ponies neighing, my darling crying, that we will march to the field.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I was walking past the green grove, black eyes peeked out behind me: eyes, don't look, better hide, because you will not be mine. When I was walking away from my love, they honked at me nicely; a cuckoo on the oak cuckooed: the cuckoo cuckooed, my dear cried because they had already taken her away.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Look again, black-eyed girl, look again out the little window: how nicely they played for me, how they led me out, how they led me out of the barracks.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Look at me, my pleasure, look at me, my dear: how they have made, saddled horses, how they have made me a lord!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: My dear little mouth, if you feel lonely, just look at the sun: wherever the sun goes, here marches, here marches our army.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Hey, Mr. Governor! How do we make my beloved want me again? "Take some water, wash yourself, wipe yourself with a handkerchief: another girl will love you."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: In Jičín, they recruit, whistle, drum: my golden maiden, should I join? "Join or not, what do I care about you; I won’t stand guard for you."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: It's a pity for you, Jeníček, that you have that saber, it's a pity for you, Jeníček, that you are a soldier! On weekdays and holidays you wear a white coat; it's a pity that you have to march!

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Don't cry, don't complain that I am in the army, don't cry, don't complain that I am a soldier: after all, I asked you, wore a little hat; you didn't want to shake my hand.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I went from Prague to Kolín with writing, I sent my dear to greet there: that she should get married, not wait for me, because I cannot get out of the army.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I want no other than the Prague tapster, I want no other than the tapster: she has ducats, she will pay me out of the army; I want no other than the tapster.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: It was worst for me 1) when they put me on the wagon, put a little horse on me, everything was shiny: everything was sparkling, yet I was longing to go to the army from my dearest.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: You Vorličtí lords, 1) write me a letter, send it to my dear one on the Prague bridge: that she should get married, not wait for me; because I cannot 2) get help from the military service.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Give me the pistol, saddle the horse; call my beloved out to me once more. We shook hands, we bade farewell to God, that we were fond of each other, my lady and I.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I am a guy from Polská, the locksmith work suits me well; I am a guy from Polská, it suits me well: the Kůt lords wrote about me, that they will support me, that I have to go to the army, that I have to go for them.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: My dear girl! Where are you grazing the horses? On the green meadow, the steward saw you: he has already been led to you right at the burgrave’s place, saying you will carry a sword at your side. "My golden maiden! I am not afraid of anything: I will mount the horse and ride away; tie up your dress, you are coming with me too; we will go together, we won’t get lost." My dear girl! Where will we go then? "My golden maiden! Straight to Hungary: there we will live well, drink wine instead of water; we don’t have to work, we won’t work."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Hunters, hunters! You are recruiting, I would join you too, but you won’t take me: I don’t know how to shoot, I don’t understand the tricks: yet I will join you, maybe I will learn. Don’t worry about anything, brother, come, give me your hand; take a hat on your head, a saber at your side: we will show you on that Prague island: if your trial succeeds, honor will be yours. Hey, brothers, don’t stand still! Make sure to pour drinks, come welcome the new comrade: give him your hand, toast to his health: musicians, play us a cheerful march! Brothers, comrades! Let us be merry, and now let’s mock those cavalrymen: we outsmarted them, we went where we wanted; when they take us away, we did not expect it.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I leave Vorlík, from Vorlík, I will sound the march; my beloved laments, laments that I will not come again. Be silent, my beloved, be silent, do not cry, for I will come back soon: if I do not come in a quarter of a year, half a year, I will come again within a year.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: I still want to ask you something, my dear girl: when you go off to the army, should I get married? "Why shouldn't you get married? Who would you be waiting for? Choose someone as nice as you are."

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: On the little hill, on the knoll, there are hussars, they wear short shirts like fishermen: they are cutting there, clinking their sabers; my beloved is afraid for me, that they will kill me. They won't kill me, they won't kill me, I have a small sword: I will cut down twenty-four of those hussars myself. I am a man like a bird, I love my beloved, I will leave it be, I will mount my black horse, I will ride away from here. "A little dove flew over the noble yard, she wept, she sighed that you will not be mine!" Be silent, Handulka, be silent, do not cry, it will be different again, if it is God's will, we will meet again.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: When I leave here and ride through that green orchard, I will look back in all directions where I used to be happy; where I used to be happy, loved beautiful girls: now I must march on, leave off loving. My little dark bay horse jumps beneath me; my dark-eyed damsel cries mournfully: Be silent, my dear, be silent, don’t cry, it cannot be otherwise; if God wills, when I return, we will meet again.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Hello, hello! Play me something cheerful so much that my dearest answered me: I do not care about that, I will tip my hat aside; because I am a handsome lad, I will sing to myself! Green is a handsome color, the color of love; a black-eyed girl whom I like already loves me: she loves me sincerely, promises much happiness, that when I return home from the army, she will be mine. I am already sitting on my black, black horse; bring me, friends, my little dove: so that she can make peace with me, give me her white hand, my little dove will see me off across the fields.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: A soldier. Ah, I, unhappy, what am I to reckon for myself in this world? I loved a beautiful girl, the neighbor’s Lenorka: stop! my father forbids her to me, says he will not give her to me, he will not allow it. I am already an imperial soldier, I have a saber at my side, I must keep a strict watch, I must not be afraid of anything: all this is for you, unhappy father, because you would not willingly give me your daughter.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: It's a pity this time that I must stop making love: I have begun to love, but I must march without delay. I joined the army under conscription when they were recruiting on all the streets of České Budějovice.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Spring is opening, flowers are blooming, trada tradadada tradada! The little cuckoo calls in the dense forest, trada tradadada tradada! The nightingale in the grove sings its song: that I will carry a little saber. You thought, my dear, that this was fun, trada etc. and now because of the fun you have sorrow, trada etc. now you will cry and remember me, and I will march away from you.

label: Písně o stavích, živnostech a jiných stránkách života občanského
translation: Spring is opening, everything is smiling, it is melancholy to sit still; the trees are greening, the birds are singing, it is a joy to go out: and I will not go on foot, I will ride a horse, and I will contemplate the green color and the beautiful red. The captain said to me: "Tell me honestly, what compels you to join the army in the camp near Prague and want to be a hussar?" And kind sir! Whatever I ask for, let it happen: my little heart has long suffered, it longs for that green color. Then they dressed me, wished me luck, gave me a green coat, red cuffs – oh, how lovely that is! – they gave me these as a gift; a cap with a feather: My lady! Hurry here, give me your hand and give me a little kiss, we are parting now. My lady runs, wringing her hands, she cries and laments: "Oh, what have you done and why have you grieved me so?" She wipes her black eyes. Don't cry and don't lament, and think of this joy; go back home, commend yourself to God – you have plenty of young men there!

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: The neighbors of Miletín are worthy – traladritum worthy, in that they do not carry under the sabre – traladritum do not carry; what they drink away, they pay for, they do not beg from anyone ouvě – traladritum by ouvěra. They gave themselves privileges written in gold – traladritum written in gold, so that they could love their maidens dearly – traladritum dearly; these were confirmed to them by Count Waldstein for a du – traladritum for a ducat.

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: Around Lysý, Lysý has beautiful forests: Count Špork used to be there, he used to sail on the Elbe – those were the days! When he sailed here and there, bagpipes played for him; on every shore, crowds of people stood in large gatherings. On both sides, his subjects were on the banks: he threw them seven-groschen coins, thus he honored the peasants loyal to him.

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: Woe to us, woe! Breda has bought us: he will turn rich peasants into beggars, woe to us, woe!

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: Put on your slippers, my son: we are going to Bavor, it lies just beyond the hills.

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: When the Prussians marched into Bohemia, Prague was closed to them; they did not stop throwing into it until it was opened to them.

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: The shirt is torn, barely holding on the body: wait, wait, Brandenburg, wait, we're coming for you, yippee! wait, we're coming for you!

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: Wait, Brandenburg, I have a bullet for you so that you don’t go to Bohemia for onions!

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: Kolín, Kolín! on a nice plain: many a young son will perish with you. Kolín, Kolín! you are not worthy to stand: many a mother will lose her son there. A mother her son, a sister her little brother; many a young woman her lover.

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: Our stucco workers delivered the pieces to White Mountain: they delivered the pieces, drove away the Prussians – it will be fine.

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: Godfather Vávra! Godfather Říha! Don’t be upset; that you lost the mare, don’t mind it at all; there are many mares in the world, you can buy thousands of them: if only Brandenburg hadn’t burned our village. We may complain as much as we want, what good will it do? Only if our skin isn’t being stretched over our heads yet: a good time will come again, everything will be replaced; we will see it soon, like a goose sees the grain.

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: Bělohrad, Bělohrad! It is a beautiful town: the emperor said he would tear everything down. The Turk replied that he is not afraid of him: that there is water all around him. Bělohrad, Bělohrad, Turkish borderland: many a mother's son lies there. Many a son, a sincere little brother, many a nice little sweetheart of a maiden!

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: Where have the young men gone? Where has the youth disappeared? The lords took them, sent them to the army – the French turned them into meat.

label: Zpomínky historické
translation: Get ready, girls, get ready for the ball until the general arrives: when the general returns, someone will pay with their life.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Wandering fiddlers, two handsome young men. One said to the other, brother to his brother: “Do you hear, dear brother! I know of beautiful wood. Maple wood, good for violins. Let’s go, let’s cut it down, let’s make violins. A violin for me and for you, so we can play for ourselves.” When they first cut, the wood sighed. When they cut a second time, blood spurted out. When they cut a third time, the wood spoke: “Don’t cut, fiddlers, you handsome young men! But I am not wood, I am blood and body. I am a lovely girl from this little town. My mother cursed me when I was fetching water. When I was fetching water, I lingered with my beloved: So that I would turn into wood and become a maple. A tall maple with broad leaves. Go, fiddlers, go, play for my mother. Play by the door about her daughter.” The fiddlers began to play, the mother started to cry. “Don’t play for me, fiddlers, you handsome young men! Don’t play for me, don’t prick my heart. I have enough sorrow, 1) when I no longer have my daughter. Unhappy mother, who curses her children!”

label: Písně rozpravné
translation:
“Who is walking around the cemetery? Treading the grass on my grave. Treading the green grass, crushing the cold dew.”  
“I am the priest who serves the divine service.”  
“If you are the priest, announce to my young lady; Let her sew me a new shirt, a new shirt, just and fair. One that is neither spun on Saturday nor rolled to mass on Sunday: So that I cannot rot in it here, nor find peace in the earth.”  

The lady immediately took the keys, opened all the chests, took the linen, and sewed the shirt.  
“Who is walking around the cemetery? Treading the grass on my grave. Treading the green grass, crushing the cold dew.”  
“I am your young lady, bringing you the new shirt, the new shirt, just and fair. One that is neither spun on Saturday nor rolled to mass on Sunday.”  
“If you are my young lady, enter here into my grave, and leave the keys as a sign!”  

As she entered the grave, the grave closed behind her. Two doves flew out, flew to the bell tower. They mournfully cooed as if ringing the bell.  

When the children heard this, they immediately ran to the cemetery:  
“Our mother, dear mother! To whom have you entrusted us?”  
“To none other, my children, than to the Lord God Himself! And you others, who are servants, listen to one another. And the youngest boy, may the Lord God Himself provide for me!”

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: When I was walking through the black forest, two maidens were reaping oats there. Tell me, maidens, doves of my heart: Is my beloved also reaping there? Is she as healthy as I am? "Oh, no, no – she died, last week she had a funeral." Show me the path by which they carried my little girl. "A good path, familiar, entwined with rosemary. A good path, all the way up to the new cemetery. Where the young men carried her, there the stones grew. Where the bridesmaids carried her, there the little roses grew." Show me the place where she lies. "Behind the little church by the door." I walked around the cemetery twice, but did not find my beloved’s grave. The third time I walk around the cemetery, I find my beloved’s grave there. "Who is treading on my grave, that will not give the dead peace? Tearing the green grass, shaking the cold dew." Rise, my beloved, rise from the grave, look out, I am your beloved. Today marks the third year since I passed, and I have come for you. "I would like to rise from the grave, but I cannot for faintness. My little heart is distressed, my hands and feet are stiffened. Search, my dear, among the graves, the gravedigger has his tools there. He has a hoe and a small spade: dig out your little girl. Once you dig me out of the grave, then take me home with you!" My beloved, dug up, entwined with rosemary, but what of her – she is cold! Where did you give, my beloved, the gifts you took from me? "If I took the gifts, I did not take them with me. My mother has those gifts, go, she will give them to you again. You have a little red scarf there, and in it a silver ring. Throw that scarf into the thorns, love will come to an end. Throw that ring into the sea, all sorrow will end." Ring the bells on all sides, my joy has died. The young rose’s bloom has withered, therefore the whole world grieves me!

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Water flows from Tábor, 1) it flows around the maple tree; it carried away all the footbridges, leaving only one. Where the young huntsman walked, he led the girl by the hand. He picked an apple from the apple tree and let it float on the water: “Flow, little apple, wherever you wish, just don’t help me; flow, little apple, flow down to the mill under the maple tree.” When the apple floated by, it stopped four wheels; it could not make them turn and drove the water onto the shore. “Why don’t you turn, wheels, are you not pleasing my sweetheart as you used to when you were turning?” The huntsman was in Tábor, walking through the game park; he was herding deer there, wearing a green hat. He used to keep company with the deer, loved beautiful girls; he sent his deer to the mill under the maple tree. “Go, little deer, along the creek, don’t regret your leap, go down there to the mill where the maple is green. There on the shore under the maple, you will find the girl standing, my dear maiden sadly grieving. Four mill wheels stand still, misfortune is born in the mill! Bow your antlers before her and jump with a footbridge into the water. Where the first little wheel is, you will come and find the apple; impale it on your antlers, and hurry to the game park.” Night approaches – the deer runs, and the earth trembles beneath it; on its antlers it carries the nice red apple. The huntsman took the apple, cut it in half: a little bird flew out, a white little dove. Don’t believe, don’t believe the huntsman that it was a little bird: it was, in fact, a transformed beautiful girl.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The established shepherd. The shepherd herds, the shepherd herds sheep in a nice green hat, herding on a little hill in a birch grove. Under the oak, suddenly two maidens stood, the shepherd greeted them good evening, and they laughed at him. One was completely white, like a dove, the other chirped to him like a swallow: "Come, shepherd! Come with us, sleep at our place until morning; and let whoever wants herd your sheep." They took him by the hand, led him into the mountains: he never found his sheep or his little cottage again.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Listen, maidens and young men, to what happened in the town of Kamenec. There was a leatherworker there who had a son, and he wooed the miller’s daughter. Her mother opposed it vehemently: “I’d rather drown you than marry you off to him!” When the leatherworker heard this, he became angry and set off into the world. On the road, they agreed to wait for each other for a year and a day. The mother wrote a letter saying she had already married off her daughter a week ago. She wrote the letter in small letters, saying her daughter had been married for a week already. When the leatherworker heard this, he fell ill and died on the third day. Unable to wait any longer, the daughter went outside and cursed him: “Even if you are locked in hell, you must be here at this moment!” Her mother urged her to go home and pray. “Praying is useless when I am tormented; I will shorten my life for pleasure!” Before the stars began to shine, horses were stomping outside the house. “Oh God, God! Who is coming to us? Open both gates!” “I am coming, my dear, coming for you: without you, I have no peace in the grave. Rise, my dear! You must leave your husband’s white bed.” “Oh, I have no husband; I am waiting for you, my beloved, with longing!” “Rise, my dear, get up and lace your dress; I am running out of time, I cannot wait. My horse is as fast as a bullet: it will take us a hundred miles by morning.” And when they rode out of the town through the gate, guests awaited their wedding. And when they arrived at the cemetery gate: “Open the grave gates! Open the grave gates: we bring the bride, the wedding is complete!” The black door immediately opened, and the beloved and his beloved disappeared into the grave. When they could not be together in life, now in death they hasten to be together.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Hurry, maiden, hurry, don’t delay any longer, the time has come, the hour, you should go with us. "Wait, you gentlemen, black yeomen! Just let me sing to you, I will call my father. Oh father, dear father! Why do you sleep so deeply, why do you sleep so deeply that you do not hear me? Wake up from your dream, I bless you, I bless you, I won’t watch over you any longer."

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Beyond the mountains, beyond the valleys, there is a bed with quilts. In that bed lies a son, holding a chopped head. Who chopped it? Two soldiers, they chopped it by the moonlight.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The linden tree was burning, burning, and under it the girl was sitting; sparks were falling on her, young men were crying for her. Why then are you crying for me? After all, I am not alone in the world, I am not the only one: the village is full of girls!

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Up there under the linden trees, three young men stood together, talking about love. One began to say what he wanted to give the maiden: "A pretty red ribbon, embossed with silver and gold." The second began to say what he wanted to give the maiden: "A little white brandy mixed into a drink." The third began to speak: "It's a shame to harm the maidens. But whichever one is dear, let us love her sincerely." Ančička was walking, fetching water, where they were plotting their plan. She went down to the bridge for water: "Come, maiden, come for the honor!" They called her to honor and let her drink from a glass. "Drink, you will drink from the glass, but you will not drink any more!" She drank from the glass, immediately turned pale, came home, and fainted at once. Her old mother asked her: "What is it, maiden, what have you done?" "I drank a bitter drink, I have been poisoned!" She sent for her beloved: "What have I done to you, that you have poisoned me, destroyed my young life!"

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The meadow was wide, 1) on it tall grass. "Let's go there, sister! We'll cut some grass in the morning." The king was watching them from the window, called to his page: "Get up, page! Prepare the horses, we are going hunting in the field. We will hunt the roe deer, twenty-year-old girls." And when they arrived at the meadow, the black horses stopped: "Who told you to cut the grass here? One of you must come with us!" "Our father and mother told us to cut the green grass." One was cutting and tying it, the other was crying sadly: "Oh, for this piece of meadow grass, I must leave my mother! Oh, for this piece of sedge, I must leave my parents! Oh, for this piece of clover, I must leave my friends! Oh, for this clover flower, I must leave the whole world!"

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: I know of a beautiful meadow, 1) on it a small town called Rakovce. Two coopers lived there, both good farmers. One had a pretty daughter, prettier than the king’s queen. 2) The grandmother was weaving the garden, 3) the king goes to the woman for advice: “Hey woman, woman! give me advice on how I could win that maiden.” “My advice will be very kind, if only it is paid for.” “Don’t worry about payment, just give me good advice.” “Buy yourself a piece of damask, have clothes sewn in the Prague style and a cap in the Moravian style. Roam, roam around the market square until you reach the coopers’ house. And when you arrive at the house, knock on the outside door.” 4) “Who is knocking like that, who is it, 5) that won’t let me rest at night?” “I am a hawker from Opava, going to Prague for goods. I sent the carts ahead, I stayed behind myself. I ask you to shelter me 6) even if only on bare ground.” “Go, Kačenka, go open the door, a beautiful lady is standing at the gate. Go, Kačenka, prepare supper, let the lady have her supper.” The lady had supper and asked to rest. “Go, Kačenka, make the bed, the lady must get up early!” “Oh, my dear father! I don’t like the lady: I have never seen a lady with a beard!” “Go, Kačenka, don’t complain, do as you are told.” When she took off her cap, she gave it to Kačenka. When she took off her ring, she gave it to Kačenka. “Extinguish the candle, Kačenka, so I can take off my skirt. I am an honest lady, more modest than others.” And when it was already midnight, the lady walked around the room. And after midnight: “Oh father, help me!” “Don’t, Kačenka, don’t scream! I myself am the King of Hungary.” And when it was morning, it was announced throughout the town: That Kačenka the cooper’s daughter was no longer a maiden. All maidens weave wreaths, sew caps with the coopers. All maidens go for wood, at the coopers they dress for baptisms. All maidens go for water, to the coopers’ daughter for the wedding. “My little brother is coming from the army, 7) what sad thing is he bringing me?” “I bring you a green wreath, pearls woven into it!” “Oh, what do I care for the wreath, since I am no longer a girl!” “Show me, sister, your husband, your husband, my brother-in-law.” “Oh, who could I show but the king himself!” “If I knew which king, I would tear him to pieces!” “That one, brother! Don’t do that, seek no blame in my honesty. My honesty runs through the world, no one can catch up with her. No one can catch up with her, even if there were three hundred horses!” When she was rocking the little son, she sang to him like this: “Sleep, little one! be quiet, my son! you have a heroic father! Sleep, little one! be quiet, my son: the King of Hungary is your father!”

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: There was a nobleman who had a beautiful daughter, oh God! Oh Lord! named Kateřina. The Turkish prince heard about her, oh God! Oh Lord! he sent her a letter. He wrote her a letter and gave a ring to be delivered to her house. When she received the letter, she gave it to her father to see what was written in it. Her father read the letter and sighed sadly to himself, oh God! Oh Lord! he sighed sadly to himself. "My dear father! why do you sigh so? What bad news do you find in that letter?" "My dear daughter! how can I not sigh when I have to give you to a Turk as a wife?" "My dear father! do not do that, do not give me into the hands of that Turk!" The Turk is riding to the gate. "Go open for him: I am going to the chamber, there I will die." "Good day, mother! where is your daughter?" "She lies in the chamber, oh, she died yesterday!" The Turk did not want to believe it; he went into the chamber, oh God! Oh Lord! he found her dead. He took a handkerchief from his pocket and wiped his cheek: "Why have you left me, my dear pearl?" He gave her a dress sewn from pink velvet: "Why have you withered, my precious flower?" He took out a precious pearl and placed it around her neck; he took a gold ring and put it on her hand. He gave her a chest made of pure silver, and on the chest a lid of pure gold. He gave a silver hoe to dig her grave, and a gold shovel to throw the earth. He ordered a bell to ring throughout the whole region so that it could be heard in the Turkish land. The Turk rides away from the house, the daughter leaves the chamber: "God accompany you to the Turkish land!" The Turk rides through the field, waving his little hat, oh God! Oh Lord! he burst with grief.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: A shepherd is herding sheep on a green meadow. A butcher was passing by with a spotted dog. "God bless you, shepherd! How much will you sell four sheep for?" "Four for two gold coins, and I accept money too." He took the money, lost it, and drove the sheep home. The shepherd is driving them home, and a man approaches him: "God bless you, shepherd! Where did you put the four sheep?" "Four wolves came, each took one sheep." "You have a stick, you should have used it to drive them; you have a dog, you should have set it on them." Not far from Lysá, the shepherd hangs there.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: There will be a war, there will be, who will go to it? She who has a sincere beloved by her side will weep. "And I have a sincere beloved by my side: if the emperor were to give me a horse, I would mount it." "What would you do there, my dear, what would you do there?" – "I would wash shirts so that you would wear them white, that’s what I would do there." "Where would you rinse them, my dear, where would you rinse them?" – "There is a stone in the Danube, I would stand on it, I would rinse them there." "Where would you dry them, my dear, where would you dry them?" – "At the royal court, on a silk cord, I would dry them there." "Where would you fold them, my dear, where would you fold them?" – "On a black horse, my golden lad, I would fold them there." "Return home, my dear, commend yourself to God: when seven years pass, the war will end, then I will marry you." "Seven years are passing, my beloved is not coming: oh God, my God! he perished there, my joy fades away." She stood by the Danube, asked the Danube: "Are you as deep as you are wide?" She asked the Danube. She jumped into the water and immediately sank: "Oh God, my God! have mercy on me for what I have done!" Her white feet tread on the sand; her white hands, her white hands grasp the shore. Her black hair floats on the water; her blue eyes, her blue eyes rise to the sky. Seven years passed, her beloved returns from war: why does his black horse, why does his black horse go so heavily under him? "Why, my horse, why do you go so heavily for me? Can you not carry me, a young, handsome lad?" "It is not your body that weighs me down, it is your beloved: who drowned herself for you yesterday." The beloved turned around, jumped off the horse, and plunged his sharp sword, and plunged his sharp sword into his heart. They were not found together in this vain world: now on their grave, now on their grave rosemary blooms.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: He stood under the window, 1) playing the violin, sadly calling to his dearest. "Are you sleeping or not sleeping, or can you not hear me? Or, my dear, are you not allowed to open for me?" "I am not sleeping, oh I am not sleeping, I hear you well: it’s just that, my dear, I am not allowed to open for you. Our mother is lying right next to the door, she would hear if I were to open." Mother got up in the morning and asked her daughter: "My dear daughter! Who were you talking to?" "Who else, mother, but my dearest: he comes to us at night and wakes me from my sleep." "If this is not true, make yourself drown, make yourself drown, the boy will be hanged." The daughter heard this and ran to the Danube; when she reached the Danube, she jumped into it. "God on high, take me to joy! My white hands are already grasping the shore. My white feet already touch the sand; my black hair is already floating on the water. My black eyes are turning over the mountains, over the mountains, over the forests, over my dearest!"

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Ah, I dreamed at night that my beloved came to me here: I wake up – but there is nothing, only my heart aches. If I had a secret messenger, I would send for my beloved: go, my little messenger, like a bird, greet my beloved a hundred times. The messenger approaches the castle, my beloved looks out from the castle: "Return, little messenger, back home, for I cannot come today. The white rose's blossom has perished, which was dearer to me than the world; the one I carried in my heart, I have sadly lost! I will come in the morning, early before the sun rises." The sun rises from the mountains, but my beloved does not come to me. My beloved rides across the bridge, beneath him a little horse worth two hundred; the little horse prances, my heart rejoices. The little horse broke a leg, my beloved knocked his head. Where should I seek help? Where should I send for a doctor? "Do not bring me doctors, rather have the church bells rung for me." Ring the bells in all directions, my joy has died.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The mother was walking along the shore; she was carrying her handsome little son in her arms. "Oh son, little son, what should I do with you? Should I drown you or raise you?" "My dear little mother, don't do that; rather raise me and send me to the army. You will receive praise from our king for raising a fine soldier. You will receive praise from the emperor for raising a fine cavalryman."

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: When the beloved was taken to the army, 1) he instructed his maiden not to marry; she waited for him for seven years: "My maiden! do not marry, in seven years I will come back; in seven years I will come home, I will wear a golden ring." And when it was the seventh year, she was cutting grass by the stream; when she had gathered it, she tied it up, looked around the field. She saw no one there, only a lone soldier; she asked him: "My dear soldier! do you know about my beloved?" "Your beloved got married there, I myself was at his wedding. What message do you send him, little dove, white, red rose?" "I send him so much this: may lightning strike him!" He turned around and laughed, the golden ring sparkled. "This is the ring from my finger, which I gave him on his journey!" — "What else do you send him, you blue-eyed girl?" "I send him as much health as there is grass on that meadow; I send him as much happiness as there are drops in a heavy rain!" The little church in the black forest: "Come, my dear, we will marry; the priest will tie our hands, no one will untie us anymore." 2)

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: When mother was home alone, a company of hussars arrived – there. "Madam, you have a lovely daughter, it's a pity you don't have her at home – there." "My daughter is out in the open field, she is weeding the wheat from the darnel – there." And when she had weeded the patch of wheat, she saw the squadron of hussars – there. "A warm welcome, gentlemen hussars! Have you seen my beloved – there?" "We have seen her, but lifeless: she fell from her horse, her head beside him – there. Come with us, and leave off ploughing, 1) you will find another joy – there." "Rather than be a hussar’s woman, I would sooner jump into the water – there. Into that water, that hollow where my beloved watered the horses – there. Into that water where the winds swirl: oh, pity his black eyes – there!"

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: She was herding geese above the yard, sitting alone under a maple tree. The villa was a wreath of olive, woven with clover and cornflower. Her beloved was passing that way: "Give me that olive wreath." "I must not give you the wreath, my mother would scold me." "If you are afraid of being scolded, come, come over these mountains with us." "I would gladly go with you, but who would I leave the geese with?" "Let the little geese go on the water, let them float to your mother. Let the little geese go down the stream, we will go gently on our own." The old goose honked, the old mother cried. "Hey, goose, honk more, you won't out-honk the herder!"

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Water flows down the valley from the forest: my beloved was sitting under a maple tree. A young lad came to her: "Give me the wreath, maiden!" "I must not give you the wreath: my mother would be angry." He looked at her over a fir tree: he jumped over the fir tree, spun around nicely, gave a kiss.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation:
In the morning she got up, had breakfast, and after breakfast she drove the geese. She drove them, drove them to the lawn, to the green clover. She sat herself on a little hill and wove a wreath from little tears. Before she finished the wreath, she fell three times and fainted.  
"My heart is locked, only the Lord God knows who has the keys.  
Ah, I know well who has the keys, to whom I gave them myself!"  
Water flows down from the mountains: "Come, my love, let's go together."  
"What kind of journey is that, if we are not married?"  
"There stands a church in the black forest, come, my love, we will marry!"  
"What kind of marrying is that, if the parents forbid it?"  
"There in the church the priest preaches, he will tie our hands with a stole.  
Tie, tie – not untie, until the Lord God himself commands from heaven."

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The maiden was herding a peacock alone among the mountains; gentlemen were passing by: "Come, maiden, with us." "If I went with you, where would I leave the peacock?" – "Let the peacock go along the stream, and just come with us." "I am not used to walking, my little stump wobbles; I would rather stay at home, I would suffer harm. Go on, dear gentlemen, I will not go with you: go with God, I will follow my peacock."

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Beyond the mountains, beyond the forests, my maiden was dancing with the girls. Two hussars were passing by: "Come with us, girl, pretty girl, come with us." "Before I would go with you, I would rather drown myself in the Berounka!"

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Shine, bright little moon, don’t set yet: my dear is on a long journey. On a long journey, across the plains: only the Lord God knows what is between us. Between us two is the most steadfast love; it will last for us until the longest death. The longest death love will last: until the larks sing at Christmas. The larks sang, my dear did not come: perhaps he is angry with me? Perhaps he forgot me? By St. John’s there is a dark little forest, where her lover rests. If his joy knew this, it would pray there for him!

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: On the little hill, there was a chapel, Ančička was whitewashing it; when she finished whitewashing it, she looked around to see if she could spot Jeníček. "I don't see him here anywhere, nor do I hear him; then with whom now, my dear God! with whom shall I find comfort!" In the valley below, a pond, in which Jeníček is watering four black horses, neatly groomed, he is all bloodied. That is not blood from the horse, it is from my Jeníček, because he was cut down, because he was cut down by that military saber.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: On our battlefield lies a soldier in the rosemary, a soldier killed, covered with rosemary. His beloved came to him, wiping blood with her little kerchief, gave him a kiss, pressed him to her heart. Who will shoe the horses for us when we march? Our blacksmith was killed, covered with rosemary. Shoe the horses until midnight, march from midnight; horseshoes clattered, blue eyes wept. "Ring the bells in all directions, my beloved is buried; the rose blossom that comforted my world has perished!" Horseshoes ring, and soldiers clash sabers; the music plays merrily, and her heart sinks.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: "My dear Jeníček! You are completely drunk. Stay here with us, I will look after you." Andulka fell asleep and did not pay attention. He went home after all and drowned in the river. A sign remained, a hat on the log cabin. Andulka took it, went home, and cried.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The journeymen are traveling from Vienna, carrying Venclíček in a basket. Oh, what have you done, Venclíček! That you have a small saber by your side? "I haven’t done anything bad, I just killed one. I killed, I killed a deer, now they’re taking me to Brno. It wasn’t a deer, it was a doe, now they’re taking me, I don’t know where."

label: Písně rozpravné
