translation: Our lady is ill; she will not live to see the morning. She wanted to drink some wine, but there was no one to get it for her. She sent for her lord, etc. - - - - -

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Twelve maidens fell asleep for the love of their beloved: only one did not sleep, she waited for her beloved. They could not wait for him, she went to tune the music: "Musicians, play, play for me joyfully! When I begin to sleep sweetly, play the piyamenty for me; musicians, play, play for me joyfully!" She went out of the castle for a walk, asked if the gentleman in embroidered clothes and silk stockings was here? The pageboy answered that he had seen no one but a black horse saddled. She went into her garden to pick a sprig of marjoram: he lay under a tree pierced by a sword. She pulled the sword out of him and drove it into her own heart: that was the end of love, a futile obstacle! Whoever rides by will regret two beautiful persons pierced by swords. Cut a green branch, cover that innocent blood, so that the crows do not caw, the maidens do not cry.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The First Sin and Punishment. (Tune 621.) The Lord God went, He went to Paradise, Adam followed Him, kneeling. When they reached the middle, the Lord God said to Adam: "You may eat from all the trees, but leave only one, which stands in the middle of Paradise, blooming with a blue flower." The devil took the form of a serpent, deceived Eve and Adam. He quickly plucked an apple and handed it to Eve. Eve took it, tasted it, and shared it with Adam. "Eat, Adam, eat this apple, which is very sweet!" Thus both sinned and were driven out of Paradise. The Lord God gave them a hoe and sent them to the vineyards. "Go, go and dig, earn your bread!" Before they managed to dig the bread, they both cried a lot.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The doll Maria stood like a red rosebud. The angel of the Lord came to her and gave greetings to the maiden. The morning star was frightened by the angelic greeting. It was not a bright star, but a beautiful maiden. Do not be afraid, pure maiden! You are to give birth to the Lord Christ. She gave birth to the Lord Christ and laid Him in the manger. Christ the Lord, a tiny child, reigns over the whole world.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Maria went, she went to paradise, she was met by the angel of the Lord. "Where are you going, Maria? You won’t make it, you carry a heavy burden under your heart." Maria did not believe him, yet she stepped into paradise. And as she stepped into paradise, she immediately asked the servants. "We will not give you servants, we have no chambers: We only have a small stable, in it is tied a little donkey." As Maria stepped there, she immediately gave birth to a little son. As she gave birth to the little son, she laid him in the manger. "Pass me, Joseph, the swaddling clothes, so we can wrap the little one." We wrapped the little one, laid him in the manger. Rejoice, angels! For we already have a savior. Rejoice, heavenly kingdom! For we already have Lord Christ.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Maria walked through the wide world, carrying her little son in her holy life. She came to a blacksmith: "Blacksmith, blacksmith! Give me lodging." "How can I give you lodging? I have many journeymen: they work day and night, they don’t put out the fire." "Blacksmith, blacksmith! What are you forging, what are you forging that you don't put out the fire?" "We are forging three iron nails, which Christ on the cross will need." When the Virgin Mary heard this, frightened, she hurried away. She came to an empty stable, stepped just inside the threshold, and gave birth to her son. The blacksmith's child came to the stable: "Lull me, my little baby!" "How can I lull you when I have no hands? No hands, no eyes!" "Touch the swaddling clothes, and you will have hands; touch the manger, and you will have eyes." The child came home, came to his father, playing with his hands, blinking his eyes. His father asked: "Where did you get those hands? Those white hands and blue eyes?" "The Virgin Mary gave them to me, she who yesterday asked for lodging in the evening." "If I had known it was Mary, I would have laid her in gold, in silver. And I myself would have lain down with my head on the stone, head on the stone, feet in thorns!"

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: Under the hill, the grass is green, and the Virgin Mary is walking on it. She was met by Saint Elizabeth: "Where are you rushing to, my dear aunt?" "I am rushing to the Body of God to perform the divine prayer." The Lord God sent an angel from heaven to see where Mary was. Mary is kneeling in the pure church, praying there at the Body of God. Whoever sings this song three times will be given happiness and health by the dear Lord God. The Virgin Mary sang it when she was rocking the Son of God.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The body of God. When the mountains turn green, blooming with blue flowers, the Mother of God walks among them, searching for her son: "Have you seen my son?" – "We have not seen him, just like on Thursday at the supper. He is there sitting with the apostles, distributing the body of God: Eat my body and drink my blood, remember my law."

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The cross and the crown of thorns. The sun rises out of the mountain: Lord Christ is coming out of heaven. He was met by Mother Mary: "Where are you going, Son of God?" – "I am carrying the wood, the holy cross, on which I am to die." He was met by Saint Peter and Saint John. "Go there, inform them that I have a crown of thorns, made of seventy-seven pieces, placed on my holy head."

label: Písně rozpravné
translation:
A star rose at the edge of summer, illuminating half the world. It was not a bright star, but a beautiful maiden. Mary walked, crying, searching for her son. The apostles came, they were sad, not joyful. "Oh, dear apostles! Why are you sad and not joyful?" "We are sad, not joyful, because we have not seen Christ. We did not see or hear how on Thursday at the supper, When He broke the bread and distributed it, He let us drink His holy blood. 'Eat my body, and drink, but this night you will lose me.'"

When the Jews heard this, they seized Christ and put Him on the cross. They pierced His hands and feet, and prepared vinegar and gall. When Mary heard this, she immediately ran to the holy cross. She stood under the cross, crying, where the holy blood dripped. Where the holy blood dripped, roses bloomed everywhere.

"What are you doing here, mother, standing? Why are you wailing so sorrowfully?" "How can I not be sorrowful and mournful, when holy blood is dripping from you, such great pain it is!"

"Be silent, mother, be silent and do not cry, for I will rise again on the third day. I do not suffer for anyone but for the sins of the wicked world. I suffer only for Christians, so that they may come to me."

Grant this, Lord Christ! Amen, may the eternal flame not consume us.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The Passion of the Lord. There was a well built, framed with a chalice, where the Virgin Mary dwelt. Why are you so sad, Virgin Mary? "How can I not be sad? I had one son, the Jews came and took him from me; they took him, put him on the cross, and pierced him with nails." Lord Christ went to the garden, leaned His holy head on the green grass, on the cold dew. Go to the young man there, speak to the old man there, how thorny wreaths were placed on Christ's head. Whoever says this prayer will have twelve days of indulgences; will free three souls from purgatorial sufferings: one of the father, one of the mother, and one of their own, opening the path to the kingdom of heaven. Lord Jesus Christ! Son of God! You carry the wood of the cross on which you are to die, to pour out your precious blood! Here came Saint Peter, Saint John, who grieved over His wounds. O Lord Jesus Christ! Have mercy on me; stand at my head, Virgin Mary at my feet, angels at my sides, Saint Benedict above me, so that the devil may have no power over me. Gracious God the Father † Son † and Holy Spirit † Amen.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: The Five Wounds of Christ. I hear bells ringing. I see, I see the Lord Christ devoutly celebrating the Holy Mass. All the souls go to Him: and I, a sinner, will also go, I will drink from the spring where the Lord Christ washed Himself, where He cursed the ugly devil. The Lord Jesus rose from His bed, put on His robe, examined His five holy wounds, which were bleeding severely. His beloved Mother came to Him and began to grieve over His wounds. "Oh, do not grieve, my dearest Mother! for these wounds are given for sinners, for Christians."

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: A Sinful Soul. There stands, there stands a little church, 1) in that little church a little bell; that little bell used to ring by itself when the Lord Christ used to visit there. A little dove flew in, took it under its wings, and flew with it to heaven. When they reached heaven, the heavens opened, the souls rejoiced: only one did not rejoice, she walked around the door. "Why, soul, do you not rejoice, why do you walk around the door?" – "How can I, sad, rejoice: I did not honor my father and mother and I opposed them!" – "Give me your right hand, soul, I will lead you through torments, through torments into purgatory, there you will watch your parents weep and lament that they did not punish their children." Saint Mary Magdalene washed the feet of the Lord Christ, wiped them with her hair. Whoever says this little prayer three times a day will free three souls: one father's, one mother's, and one their own, and will have the path to the heavenly kingdom opened.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation:
There was a green meadow, sprinkled with tearful dew. Two souls were walking on it, followed by a great sinner. When they reached paradise, they knocked on the door there.  
“Rise, Peter! Take the keys, see who is knocking?”  
“Two souls are knocking, Lord, and behind them a great sinner.”  
“Let those two souls in here, send the sinner away. Show her the wide path by which sinners go to hell.”  

The little soul went sobbing and regretting her sins. She came from heaven, lamenting until blood dripped from her eyes. She met Mary the Virgin:  
1)  
“What do you lament, sinful soul?” – “How can I not lament when I don’t know where to go? I must go the wide path by which sinners go to hell.” – “Return with me, sinful soul! I will ask my son.”  

And when they reached paradise, they knocked on the door there.  
“Rise, Peter! Take the keys, see who is knocking.” – “Your mother is knocking, Lord, and behind her that sinful soul.” – “Let my mother in here, send the sinner away. Show her the wide path by which sinners go to hell.”  
– “Not so, not so, dear son! Forgive the sinful soul her guilt.”  
– “Ask her, dear mother! What good has she done? How many holidays did she keep? How many Fridays did she fast? How many poor did she support?”  
2) – “I did not keep holidays, nor fast on Fridays. I only did one thing: I gave alms to the poor.”  
– “Give me your hand, sinful soul, I will lead you through the torments. Through the torments into the abyss and from the abyss into joy. A penny, a small alms, helped you into paradise!”

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: There was once a virtuous lady who had three daughters to marry off. She took two to church, the third dressed up at home. And when she dressed up, she hurried to the garden. She was picking red roses, making herself a wreath. A young man came to her: "Give me that wreath, maiden!" – "I will not give you the wreath, I am waiting for a more handsome one." A second young man came to her: "Give me that wreath, maiden!" – "I will not give you the wreath, I am waiting for a more handsome one." A third young man came to her: "Give me that wreath, maiden!" – "I will give you my wreath, I am no longer waiting for another." – "Jump up, maiden, on my horse, I will take you to where my house is. I will take you to the land, to paradise, to paradise." When they left the town, the roads were not good. Only through brambles, through brushwood, through that devil's goods. And when they arrived at the place, they saw blazing hell. "Hey, open up, fellows! I bring you a body and soul! Set up a chair for the maiden, pour a glass of wine." The first opened the gate for them, the second poured pitch on her. She drank the first glass, her color immediately changed. She drank the second glass, flames shot from her mouth. "Open the door, gentlemen, let the breeze blow on me. Ah! Even just a little window, so my heart can cool." – "If you wanted to cool down, you should have done well." – "Ah! If only there were someone good here to tell my mother. She still has two daughters at home, she would guide them better than me. They would serve the Lord God, reject worldly vanity: worldly vanity ruined me, destroyed my soul forever." – "There is no need to send them a message, they have someone to inform them: they have a spiritual messenger there, let them act according to his word." From Prague. – Compare Suš. 21; Kol. II, 12: Žcg. P. 76; Vójc. I, 99, 101, 291; II, 301; Olesk. 411; H. Luž. 290; Mein. 213. – Besides, there is another song in Bohemia of the same content, from which only these verses have been obtained: “Sit down, maiden, on my wings, we will fly straight to heaven.” And when they arrived at heaven, they turned to hell: “Hey! hey! fellows! I bring a body, a sinful soul,” etc.

label: Písně rozpravné
translation: At the churchyard in front of the church, the soul argued with the body: "Body, body, what have you done, that you have not cared for the soul? Whatever you saw, you wanted it all, you did not remember God. Body, body, sinful body, you were proud in the world. You walked in silver and gold: and I, the soul, suffer for you! You went to the music halls, and I languish in severe torment!" – "Do not blame me, soul, you were with me every moment." – "If I was always with you, I did not have control over myself."

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: Old woman doctor. I am an old woman, by God I will help what I can; and what I cannot, I leave to the Lord God.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: Water. A sick person who wishes to recover quickly will send for water from the well to which they have the greatest trust. Then, making the sign of the cross, they say: Cold water! You who are blessed by Saint John at the baptism of Christ the Lord: be helpful to my health. And then they drink that water.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: For the blister. Take a piece of charcoal and three times press it on the top of the blister in the shape of a cross, then say as follows: I consecrate you, blister or sore, in the name of the Lord; I consecrate you so that you grow no more: if you are of the wind, go to the wind; if you are of the water, go to the water; if you are of the earth, go to the earth and give me (name) peace! To this end, may God the Father, God the Son, God the Holy Spirit help me! The last part is said three times and then concluded with: Amen.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: For roundworms. The Virgin Mary was pure beyond all, she had three own little sisters: one spun thread, the second was a fairy, the third blessed for roundworms; she blessed with heartfelt, liver, lung, blood, and intestinal blessings this and that person (name), so that the worms would not eat their flesh, would not drink their blood, and would give that person peace. May God the Father, God the Son, God the Holy Spirit help with this, amen.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: For roundworms. Saint Lucy had three daughters: the first was a fiend, the second a fairy, the third a spinner. Give, God! that these roundworms leave this person (name), just as the crooked mayor, the crooked steward, the crooked priest’s cook have left! Then spit three times on your fingers and wipe it over that person for life.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: On the rose. Place some hot coals in a shallow vessel, sprinkle on them the blessed spices from the Corpus Christi wreath and some dried rose petals, use this smoke and say: I command you, itch mite, rose mite, bone mite! into the deep forest, into the tall oak, into the standing and lying wood: there thrash and splinter yourselves, and give peace to this person (name)! Repeat this three times, and then have the person say the Lord’s Prayer, the Creed, and Hail, Queen.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: For the hiccup. Škytka went across the sea, met Řehoř there: Řehoř returned, lost Škytka there.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: On a stretched vein. Flesh to flesh, bone to bone, blood to blood, water to water. Holy, holy, holy Joachim, Joseph, Anna! Say this three times, then the Lord’s Prayer and the Hail Mary three times each. And if the livestock is ill, dip a piece of cloth in human urine and apply it to the spot.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: On the decline. A seasoning called oubutové (grows in fields and looks yellow, similar to panpalík – sic!) is boiled, poured into a wooden container, and the sick person sits over it, like when steaming during a cough. This is done only on Wednesday and Friday, in the morning and evening: at sunrise it is wiped upwards, and at sunset it is wiped downwards, while saying: Oubutě, souchotě! Stand as still as the water in the Jordan at Lord Jesus; leave from the heart, veins, and lungs! May this be helped by [God], etc.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: On Bewitchment. The Lord God forever and ever – wondrous are His mercies! You and you (name)! I make water for your cold, for the spectacle, for the bewitchment: whether you were struck by water, or by wind, whether by fright, or by exhaustion; whether someone bewitched you with any eyes: be they blue, or green, be they black, or red; be it a man, or a woman, be it a youth, or a maiden, be it a boy, or a girl. To this, may God the Father help me, etc.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: Against the evil eye. Holy, holy, holy, Lord God of Hosts! Say these words three times; then take a glass of cold water and seven hot coals, and continue saying: If the maiden cursed you, may Saint Anne help you; if the young man cursed you, may Saint Lawrence help you; if the woman cursed you, may Saint Magdalene help you; if the man cursed you, may Saint Hainuš help you; if the old woman cursed you, may Saint Saba help you; if the old man cursed you, may Saint Melchizedek help you; if the child cursed you, may Saint Vitus help you! With each name, drop one coal into the water; the one that sinks and remains at the bottom can be taken as a sign that the named person, i.e., maiden, young man, woman, etc., has indeed cursed the sick person. Then give the sick person a little of that water to drink, and wash their face and hands and three veins with the rest.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: For a good recovery from smallpox. Saint Barbara had nine smallpox spots; Saint Barbara had eight smallpox spots; Saint Barbara had seven smallpox spots; Saint Barbara had six smallpox spots; Saint Barbara had five smallpox spots; Saint Barbara had four smallpox spots; Saint Barbara had three smallpox spots; Saint Barbara had two smallpox spots; Saint Barbara had one smallpox spot; Saint Barbara had no smallpox spots! Cross your fingers of both hands over that spot, breathe three times making the sign of the cross on the smallpox spot, and say three times: Holy, holy, holy Lord God of hosts! Then say the Lord’s Prayer three times, and the Hail Mary three times. If the inflammation is too big, apply a dry plum. It is best to do all this in the morning and in the evening, not during the day.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: On worms. Saint Peter lies in the grave, he has nine worms with him; Saint Peter lies in the grave, he has eight worms with him; Saint Peter lies in the grave, he has seven worms with him; Saint Peter lies in the grave, he has six worms with him; Saint Peter lies in the grave, he has five worms with him; Saint Peter lies in the grave, he has four worms with him; Saint Peter lies in the grave, he has three worms with him; Saint Peter lies in the grave, he has two worms with him; Saint Peter lies in the grave, he has one worm with him; Saint Peter lies in the grave: he has no worms with him! May God the Father help from this, etc.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: For a cold. Disperse all evil spirits, disgraceful beings, infections, spells, as my spirit disperses, you and you (name) throughout your life! Then breathe on the sick person three times in the sign of the cross.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: For a cold. A burning, stinging, sharp cold! I banish you: aches, pains, as the rock does not grow, stones, from the birth of the Son of God. I banish you to go away to the red sea! May God the Father, God the Son, God the Holy Spirit help me with this! Repeat this three times and after the third time conclude: Amen. However, this blessing should be performed under the open sky.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: For warts. Growths, signs! as stones do not grow from the birth of Lord Jesus. Help me with this, etc. Repeat this three times and while doing so, wipe the warts with a white cloth; it must, however, be done outdoors and when the moon is waning.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: On the visiting bone. Take a wrapped bone from a carrion pit, for example from a dog, place it on the visiting bone and say: The Lord God first! minerals, minerals, a sign, as the stones do not grow from the Son of God's birth: go away to the red sea! Assist me with this, etc.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: For ringworm, for warts, etc. A certain old woman in the Hradec region had a bluish stone with which she circled the affected area three times, saying: Ringworm and the mark of deformity, like the stone from the birth of the Son of God: as you have appeared, so disappear, so that you may not grow forevermore. Three times the Lord's Prayer and three times the Hail Mary.

label: Říkadla v nemocech
translation: If something falls or flies into someone's eye, they must spit on the ground three times and say three times: Pfí z očka do potočka!

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: From the earth I came to the earth, on the earth I found understanding: I walk on it like a master, into it I will be buried – may the Lord God Himself comfort me! Where are those golden little hands of my dear mother, who rocked me, pressed me to her heart when I was little? They are no longer here, no, no – long ago they have rotted in the black earth: how shall I repay my dearest parents for their kindness!

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: Where have those times gone, which used to be in my youth? They are no longer and will not be: this troubles my grey head. When I was very young, I loved a pretty girl: what a pity my youth, which I enjoyed with joy!

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: It used to be, it used to be, it used to be good, it used to be, it used to be, it used to be good: in our younger years, the world used to be like a flower; it used to be, it used to be, it used to be good.

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: Those times that once were will no longer be; we will no longer be as we used to be. That accursed world, entirely turned upside down: it was not always so completely reversed. Where are the old Czechs? Look a little from the cemetery; surely you would now say: I would rather die again! So one now wanders in this confused world: it was never all so reversed. The human mind takes on what it cannot bear; do not transform with a good elbow the skin on your own body. As it seems, it shoots, but does not hit, or it misses; it thinks it is right, but in fact the opposite. Faith, love, sincerity, not even a spark smolders; instead now only falsehood and secret shame prevail. Who takes advantage of whom, many rather than a few; it was not always so completely reversed. Where is the old Czech sincerity that once was? It has passed away, perished with those old people. Where once was sincerity, now there is lie and deceit; it is all so reversed in this world. The young forget the old Lord God, as if He no longer existed, many now act this way: it does not enter their mind that death will come, and it will also turn us all so completely upside down.

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: The futility of life. Everyone is a guest in the world, from day to day just a step closer to death; what remains here after the miser will be given to ungrateful people!

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: Trust in God. Whoever hopes in God does not despair: poverty and wealth are both received as a gift from God. Carved in Gothic script, so called, from the 15th century on the stone frame of the main entrance of the inn "At the Black Horse" in Hradec Králové, now however densely covered with lime plaster.

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: There is nothing better in this world than to honor God, to invoke Him, to serve Him, to praise Him.

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: A cheerful mind in good health, and on top of that a good conscience: whoever wishes this to oneself, no matter what happens, fears nothing and is afraid of nothing.

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: Nothing, nothing, nothing, there is nothing in the world: whether young or old, everyone must go to death; nothing, nothing, nothing, there is nothing in the world.

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: When I die, what will you give me? An oak little chest, a white little shirt, that is what you will give me. On that chest, the word of God: so that everyone knows who lies here. Lies, lies a little lad, the lover of all beautiful girls.

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: If I knew I would die this year, I would have an oak coffin made. An oak coffin, a linden wood lid, so the girls would feel sorry. And on that lid, a green wreath, so they would know I am a bachelor. In that wreath, a pink ribbon, so the girls would find me here. They would take me, carry me on a bier, my parents would weep. On each side, six beautiful girls, and six young men would carry me out. Carry me, carry me, lay me in the grave and cover me with black soil. On the grave, place a wide stone, and write these words on it: “Here lies, oh here lies the young body, which loved girls dearly. Fair maidens, you who pass by, pray for his soul!”

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: Take care, my mother! Wherever God assigns me a place: my place is in the Obděnský cemetery, mother! Behind the wall in the cemetery, they will make my bed; instead of feather quilts, they will give me pine chips. Those bell-ringers from Obděn, they are famous men: they will play beautifully for me, and I will sleep deeply. Even if they played for a hundred hours, I would not wake up; even if they played longer, it would be all in vain!

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: Cemetery, cemetery, green garden! What precious seeds are sown into you. They keep sowing there, yet none sprout: perhaps the gardener buries them too deep. I gathered sprigs of rue with broad leaves: if my beloved sleeps, may he wake soon. The rue has already withered, the wind has carried away the leaves: and my dearest has still not awakened!

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: Almighty God! A farmer died in Olomouc. They rang the bells for him in Prostějov, and buried him in Brno. In Kiev, they made a coffin for him, and from Jihlava they brought the bier. A shroud from the town of Kojetín, the gravedigger came from Budín. They took the priest from Hustopeče and the cantor from Proseč. Altar boys from Třebíč, boys with a cross from Meziříč. Those who carried him were glad to have met: three were even from Krakow, the fourth a night watchman from Makov.

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: Mirotice church, you stand on a nice little hill: when we rest there, the sparrows will sing about us.

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: When a rich man dies, every bell tolls for him: Fields, meadows, house! Fields, meadows, house! But if a poor man dies, they ring one bell: He had nothing! he had nothing! he had nothing! he had nothing! Or: Poor fellow! beggar! poor fellow! beggar!

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: Our bells sound beautiful when they ring on the holiday: bim bam bim bam!

label: Marnost světská, smrt a pohřeb
translation: Bombi – lombi – bom! Against Klatovy! Who is ringing? Peter the fool behind the altar – bombi – lombi – bom!

label: POHŘEBNÍ
translation: Come, chosen soul, redeemed by precious blood! Receive the joy of heaven, the angelic delights with us. Behold! Heaven is open, a place is prepared for you by Christ, the Son of God, your dear bridegroom. The angels await you, holding a crown, singing: receive eternal glory in heaven, with us unending joy. O blessed soul, who are called there! You see the King in eternal glory, forever standing in constant joy. All you my friends, parents! Remember this moment each for yourselves, for it will come to you as it has to me. You see the grave dug, prepared for my body, that it may rest there, awaiting resurrection. And who can tell the joy and delight to be revealed, which the saints in heaven have, those who dwell with Christ. Help us, dear Lord! That we may leave this world, then come to you, and dwell with you forever.

label: POHŘEBNÍ
translation: And why do you weep and wail so much for the death of your dear child? God gave it to you, God has taken it away: so it pleased His will. You have no grievance against Him in this, wretched man! Why do you wail so much? Knowing this, do not grieve anymore, but always give thanks without ceasing.

label: POHŘEBNÍ
translation: Moderate your sorrowful tears, dear parents! You who have begotten me. For a certain day was appointed by God for my entrance into the world, an early birth: So now the hour appointed by God has come for me to leave this very wretched world. Even now, dear parents, I am departing from you to my God, the eternal Father. From a valley of pain, tears, and sins to a glorious, eternal, joyful, and safe homeland. Blessed is he who can arrive early at the desirable safe shore. Moderate your sorrowful tears, dear parents! You who have begotten me. I now bless you and take my leave; may God be your abundant comfort in your grief. And when your appointed hour comes, may He be gracious to you in your coming to Heaven.

label: POHŘEBNÍ
translation: I know that my Redeemer, who Himself frees from eternal death, lives and reigns with the Father, and by His power sustains me. On the last day and the judgment, I shall be raised again, I will receive a glorified body, clothed in new skin. I will see God in my body, this I always fully believe, that these very eyes of mine will behold God, and no other. This hope is established and rooted in my heart, that the One I have trusted in is mighty to raise me again. In Him I will not fall into disgrace, but come to glory; for He will take me for Himself with soul and body into heaven. I entrust my soul to God, I give it to Him alone: receive it, Lord Jesus! into Your holy heavenly kingdom.

label: POHŘEBNÍ
translation: Man! Whoever you are, now look upon me, dead: as you are now at this moment, so was I in the same way. Young, handsome, strong enough: now I am dust, ashes, bones; frogs and worms are already tearing at my body and devouring it. Coat of arms, honor, glory, nobility, goods, property and splendor, money, pleasures, wealth—death has brought all to ruin. After death, both the poor and the rich are equal; you cannot distinguish a lord, prince, or student from a peasant. Today you are healthy according to your will, tomorrow you will be a dead body; today honors multiply for you, tomorrow you will be laid in the grave. Therefore, do not rely at all on your youth, freshness, strength, beauty, or joy, for it is all fleeting—the work is in vain. But first, take care to repent before dreadful death seizes you; while you still have time, do penance, so you may obtain mercy.

label: POHŘEBNÍ
translation: Very sorrowful is the time in which they heard the sad voice: I now bid you farewell, I entrust you to the Lord God. I am already departing to God and can no longer watch over you, neither my wife¹ nor my friends, in this mortal body of mine. For the goal appointed by God to me is fulfilled, I am free from pain, I enjoy joy with Christ. I have placed my hope firmly in Christ the Lord, that my soul rejoices with all the holy angels. All of you, look upon me and strive in your hearts always to avoid sin, to remember your own death. And so I bid you farewell for a long time, my acquaintances! I bid farewell to all of you, I entrust you to the Lord God. After me, all of you also prepare yourselves, pay close attention to this, so that you may be ready and enter the wedding feast with Christ.

label: POHŘEBNÍ
translation: Merciful God! I pray that You acknowledge Your creation; for I am nothing but dust and mud, forgive me my transgressions. When I stand before Your judgment, be merciful to me: grant me to dwell with You in heaven!

label: POHŘEBNÍ
translation: Let us bury this body, let us bury it with peace in the hope of the same resurrection, in the dwelling on Zion. What is broken is hidden, it will rise glorified for judgment; what is mortal is buried, it will become immortal through this. It is taken from the earth, it goes into the earth, the spiritual comes forth from the earth when on the last day the voice of the Son of God sounds with the trumpet. In these words let us rejoice, in good works let us persevere, ready for the same thing, let us prepare for the judgment of the Lord. Christ! For your passion, grant us forgiveness of sins, lest the eternal flame consume us, keep us, Christ! Amen.

label: POHŘEBNÍ
translation: Christ, Son of the Virgin! In the last hour, have mercy on us, forgive us all our guilt; intercede with your Father, for we call to you, in you we have hope, with you we wish to be forever and ever, amen.

label: POHŘEBNÍ
translation: And so the little soul rises to God, like a white dove rejoices. For adults. (Melody 811.) The last hour strikes, we die: by the will of God the Lord we shall meet again.

take a white scarf  
and tie it for me.  
3. And when he began  
to tie her head,  
she began to faint  
in his hands.  
4. He tied her head,  
his hands fell,  
why are you afraid, Pepíček,  
why are you frightened?  
5. I am not afraid,  
I was just worried  
that my sweetheart  
was dying in my hands.  
6. I am not dying,  
I am just fainting,  
because my Pepíček  
is wandering after another.  
5. When he wanders away,  
he will come back,  
he will take you, Mařenka,  
he will take you.

label: MILOSTNÉ
translation:
1. Behind our little barn,  
behind our barn,  
my girl was crying there,  
crying there over me.  
Why are you crying and lamenting,  
I won’t marry you,  
I’ll mount a black horse  
and go to the army.  

2. Me to the army, back from the army,  
are you still single?  
Who, my girl,  
were you waiting for?  
I was waiting, my Pepíček,  
I was waiting for you,  
until you come home from the army,  
so that we could be together.

label: MILOSTNÉ
translation:
1. She was mowing grass  
in the dry meadow  
couch grass,  
two green  
hunters came upon her.  

2. They took her sickle,  
broke it:  
Go home,  
they’re taking away  
your pleasure  
to the army.  

3. Let them take,  
let them not take,  
what’s more,  
there are still  
a thousand  
nice lads.  

4. She was washing diapers  
by the well,  
crying,  
that she has no time (ask her,  
friend,  
Fagan, what’s wrong with her).  

5. She won’t tell you that,  
friend,  
you took away  
her little green wreath.  

6. Let you have taken,  
let you not have taken,  
what’s more,  
there are still (in the world)  
a thousand  
nice lads.

label: MILOSTNÉ
translation:
1. Gray little dove,  
where have you been?  
Where have you lost  
your ash-gray feathers,  
where have you lost  
your ash-gray feathers?

2. I lost them  
in the field,  
and to you,  
my dear Honzíček,  
and to you,  
my dear Honzíček,  
defiantly.

3. Defiantly, don’t do anything to me,  
just you, gray,  
gray little dove,  
just you, gray,  
gray little dove,  
be careful.

4. I will lure you  
with a rye ear,  
I will catch you,  
gray little dove,  
I will catch you,  
gray little dove,  
soon again.

5. Play, musicians,  
cheerfully,  
as long as I have  
my green wreath,  
as long as I have  
my green wreath  
on my head.

6. When I no longer  
have my wreath,  
then to you, my  
dear musicians,  
then to you, my  
dear musicians,  
I thank you.

label: MILOSTNÉ
translation:
1. Youth, joy,  
let it be, young men,  
the maiden grows up  
As soon as the mother  
has barely awaited  
her daughter,  
you already want  
her to be given to you.  
2. I won’t give, I won’t give,  
young men from Jitkov,  
I tell you this,  
red apples  
to pick,  
you must let them  
ripen.

label: MILOSTNÉ
translation:
Dear ladies,  
open the gates for us,  
we are bringing you a bride  
made of pure gold.

label: MILOSTNÉ
translation:
1. That Sopot parish priest  
preaches nicely,  
distributes pictures  
at the altar.  
2. I will also go  
to that sermon,  
I will ask  
for one picture.  
3. And that kind chaplain,  
he gave me a picture,  
it was alive.  
4. I will put it in books,  
it won't fit,  
I will put it in the chest,  
it will suffocate.  
5. I will put it in the bed,  
lie down next to it,  
it will give me a little kiss  
and I will give one to it.

label: VOJENSKÉ
translation:
1. Whose field is that  
unplowed,  
that is my sons’ who  
have neglected it.  
2. I have neglected it,  
but only a little,  
when my little plow  
broke.  
3. If it broke,  
get it repaired,  
so I can get rid of you,  
my son.  
4. Don’t drive me out,  
I will go myself,  
I will ride a little horse,  
I will be a hussar.  
5. The hussar has a little horse  
for fun,  
the farmer must pay  
the contribution.

label: VOJENSKÉ
translation:
1. I am sorry, my mother,  
I am truly sorry for you,  
that you raised me so hard  
and now I must leave you.  

2. Thank you, my father,  
thank you a hundred times,  
that you faithfully cared for me,  
thank you a hundred times.  

3. I am sorry, my maiden,  
I am truly sorry for you,  
that we loved each other faithfully,  
and now I must leave you.

label: VOJENSKÉ
translation:
1. Moravia, Moravia,  
dear little Moravia,  
what comes from you,  
what comes from you,  
is a noble youth.  

2. A noble youth,  
obedient, inclined  
and strong to fight  
against the French  
and against the French.  

3. The Viennese townspeople,  
they did badly,  
that they secretly,  
that they secretly  
gave the keys to the French.  

4. They let those uninvited guests  
across the bridges  
and now our boy,  
as soon as he grows up,  
must wear a uniform.  

5. There will be war, there will be,  
who will go to it?  
Whoever has a dear one,  
true to themselves,  
will lament.  

6. And I have a dear one,  
true to himself,  
If the emperor  
gave me a little horse,  
I would ride it.  

7. What would you do there, my dear,  
what would you do there?  
I would wash shirts,  
so you could walk in white,  
that’s what I would do there.  

8. Where would you rinse them, my dear,  
where would you rinse them?  
There is a stone in the Danube,  
I would step on it,  
there I would rinse them.  

9. Where would you dry them, my dear,  
where would you dry them?  
In the imperial court,  
on a hempen line,  
there I would dry them.  

10. Where would you iron them, my dear,  
where would you iron them?  
In the imperial chamber,  
there are two sets of irons,  
there I would iron them.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. When I leave this world,  
I will sadly sing to myself:  
Behind our barn  
The hares are grumbling,  
soon one, soon two,  
soon there are many of them.

2. When I leave this world  
I will sadly sing to myself:  
By the brook, by the water  
I was picking strawberries there.  
If I don’t get married this year,  
I will jump into the water.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. Red beer,
Czech pastries,
and that Czech girl
(blue-eyed girl)
happily jumps around
Trada rata, trada rata,
trada rata, trada,
tradrata, tradrata,
trada rata trada
Red beer,
Czech pastries

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. Be careful, girl,  
of the Czech, of the Czech,  
for he will ruffle you up,  
he will ruffle you up.  
2. Be careful, girl,  
of the German, of the German,  
for he will shake you up,  
he will shake you up.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. Just treat me well, my dear,  
I will buy you a silk scarf.  
2. I treat you well, my dear,  
you even gave me a ring,  
now I don't have it anymore.  
3. I was washing my hands  
in the brook,  
there I dropped that ring.  
4. Red scarf,  
spin around,  
my dear is angry,  
I don't know why.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. Whoever drinks, feasts,
pays the fiddler,
he is accepted into heaven
by the holy angels.
I drank, feasted,
paid the fiddler,
when I went to heaven,
Peter sent me back.
Ah, dear Peter,
you don’t know me yet,
I am a big reveler,
I party until morning.
I am Petr Faust,
woman Marketa,
we will buy a barrel organ,
we will go out into the world,
you will play,
you will dance,
we will earn
something together.

2. I will play nicely
and you will collect,
and so we will
travel through the world.
Ah, dear Peter... etc.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
If I had,  
what I would want,  
I would like that,  
a bundle of straw,  
a girl on it,  
that would be it.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. When I see a hussar  
walking down the street,  
and his tassel swings  
behind his cap.  
2. Wait, cursed girl,  
what will the old man tell you,  
that you lay with a hussar  
on the bench by the stove.  
3. The girl danced with the hussar  
from evening till morning,  
she danced even more,  
until she tore her shoe.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. I served at a farmer's place,  
he was a real scoundrel.  
As soon as he woke up in the morning,  
he ran to the stable  
and called for the farmhand  
if there was any stew.  

2. He orders to cut the stew,  
but doesn’t care about the bread.  
Just potatoes all the time,  
damn it all,  
the soup smells like smoke  
and I ached from it for a week.  

3. The crumb soup,  
the peasant woman, snotty,  
my stomach hurts from it,  
I'd rather run away from them.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. I worked at the calico mill,  
not long ago,  
I was supposed to learn  
how to make fabric  
Tra, tra, trata, data,  
trata, data,  
tra, tra, tratadata, tra.  

2. One leg stretches out  
and the other bends,  
the warp is stretched,  
the shuttle jumps into it  
Tra, tra, trata, data...etc.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
Uncle Nimra  
bought a dapple-gray horse,  
for half a kreuzer and a half,  
came home,  
grabbed his wife,  
danced the madęra with her.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
Yesterday on a walk,  
it was quite a scene,  
I saw my dear  
with a soldier in the garden,  
and she kept  
nudging him in the back,  
telling him to go with her to the shade.  

And then suddenly,  
they fell asleep together,  
both went away  
into one garden,  
and I, very quietly,  
with my hat tipped,  
had to leave  
to the captain:  

Dear Captain,  
just take a look  
into their garden,  
you'll find some animals there,  
they just closed the gate,  
so they wouldn’t run away.  

And he had them caught,  
put them in jail,  
that’s what you get,  
my dear,  
if you were  
like you used to be,  
but you’re like  
you came from a brothel.  

She walks the street,  
day and night too,  
associating with soldiers.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. In front is a woven garden  
and in it is a made well,  
by it rosemary,  
that I know well,  
when it was planted.  
2. When I nicely entwine you there,  
yet I will tell you, my dear,  
that you came to us,  
spoke falsely,  
that I know well.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
What’s the matter, little Honzík,
about the little woman,
she has skirts
down to the ground
And when there’s dew,
she is barefoot,
she drags her skirts
all messy.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. Honzíček is coming from Pest,  
he is bringing a bride for his mother.  
2. The lady looks out the window:  
“What kind of fool are you bringing me?”  
3. “Madam, don’t be so harsh,  
I want to be your bride.”  
4. “You won’t be my bride,  
better go back.”  
5. “I won’t go back,  
I will be Honzíček’s wife.”

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
What poverty is this  
what need is this?  
Káča has money  
by the stove in the alcove  
I will court Káča,  
I will take Káča,  
I will take the money,  
I will drive Káča away.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
The deer is mowing vetch,
the fox is sneaking,
the hare is loading the cart,
the fly is raking.
The mosquito is stretching its legs,
that it will get married,
the fly is pricking up its ears,
that it will go to the wedding.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
If you were Catholic,  
I would take you to my heart,  
but since you're a harlot,  
I don't want you, monkey.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. When I was standing on the balcony,  
a ghost came to me,  
it wore shoes and pants  
and was pressing against me.  
2. And I, a girl still young,  
didn't understand it,  
I slipped into a nightgown  
and lay down beside it.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. Look, look how thin I am,  
I haven’t eaten since Monday,  
look, look how thin I am,  
I haven’t eaten today yet.  
2. Look, look how thin I am,  
like a stump after a flood,  
look, look how thin I am,  
I haven’t eaten today yet.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. Our dear late father,  
give him, Lord, the kingdom,  
he used to help old maids  
sometimes to some joy.  
2. Our dear late father,  
give him, Lord, the heavens,  
he used to carry old maids  
on a wheelbarrow into the forest.

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. In Tábor, in the yard  
at the farmer’s place  
two girls were fighting  
over a soldier.  
2. The soldier says to them:  
“Don’t fight!”  
He threw them pants:  
“Split them!”  
3. One grabbed them,  
runs away,  
the other shouts after her:  
“Half each!”

label: ŽERTOVNÉ A TANEČNÍ
translation:
1. The reel was getting married,  
taking a long pole for itself.  
2. All the birds were invited,  
only the owl was left out.  
3. When the owl heard this,  
she immediately flew to the wedding.  
4. She sat there on the bench,  
and started playing a German tune.  
5. The first dance – the sparrow took her,  
stepped on her right thumb.  
6. Sparrow, sparrow, you are foolish,  
don’t step on my claws.  
7. If there were no guests here,  
your bones would be cracking.  
8. But since there are guests here,  
I don’t want to cause trouble.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. If I had known  
that I was going to die today,  
I would have had  
an oak coffin made.  
2. And on that coffin  
a silver lid,  
so that all the girls  
would be sorry.  
3. And on that lid  
a green wreath,  
so you would know  
that I was a bachelor.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. Around the month  
wheels are made,  
those Jitkov girls,  
those Jitkov girls  
are sought by thousands.  

2. Thousands seek them,  
they do not have themselves,  
only that one skirt,  
only that one skirt  
that they wear.  

3. On Friday they will wash it,  
on Saturday iron it,  
and on Sunday in the pub,  
and on Sunday in the pub  
they show it off.  

4. The mother scolds her:  
“Daughter, stay at home!  
You know well about it,  
that we have no bread.”  

5. “Even if we had nothing  
at all to eat,  
as long as I run around  
with a young man across the halls.”

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. A crow flew in,  
sat on a thorn,  
a second flew in,  
sat next to it.  
2. A little bird flew there,  
sat on a little branch,  
and a second flew in,  
singing a little song.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. On Monday there is soup,  
on Tuesday peas and lentils,  
on Wednesday there is cabbage  
as sour as the devil.  
2. On Thursday there are peas and barley,  
that is very silly with us,  
and on Friday there are dumplings,  
eat up, girls.  
3. On Saturday there are potatoes  
or bread, sausages,  
on Sunday there is meat,  
eat up, guys.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. I would not want a widower,  
I would be afraid of a widower,  
that the poor woman  
would be standing behind the door.  
2. She stood behind the door,  
admonishing me,  
not to harm  
those little orphans.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. Zezulinko, where have you been,  
that you haven’t looked in for so long?  
Cuckoo, cuckoo,  
I was sitting on a beech.  

2. I was sitting on the small branch,  
calling out to the hunter:  
Cuckoo, cuckoo,  
I was sitting on a beech.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. When I was standing on the balcony,  
a ghost came to me,  
it had shoes and pants  
and pressed against me.  
2. And I, still a young girl,  
didn't understand it,  
I slipped into a nightgown  
and lay down next to it.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. Look, look, how thin I am,  
I haven't eaten since Monday,  
look, look, how thin I am,  
I still haven't eaten today.  
2. Look, look, how thin I am,  
like a stump from a little water,  
look, look, how thin I am,  
I still haven't eaten today.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. Our dear late father,  
grant him the Lord's kingdom,  
he used to help old maidens  
sometimes to some joy.  
2. Our dear late father,  
grant him the Lord's heaven,  
he used to carry old maidens  
in a wheelbarrow to the forest.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. In Tábor, in the yard  
at the farmer's,  
two girls were fighting  
over a soldier.  
2. The soldier said to them:  
"Don't fight!"  
He threw them pants:  
"Split them."  
3. One grabbed them,  
runs away,  
the other shouts after her:  
"Half each!"

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. A motovidlo was getting married,  
   taking a long pole for itself.  
2. All the birds were invited,  
   only the owl was left out.  
3. As soon as the owl heard this,  
   she immediately flew to the wedding.  
4. She sat there on the stove shelf,  
   and started playing German music.  
5. The first dance – a sparrow took her,  
   stepped on her right thumb.  
6. Sparrow, sparrow, you are foolish,  
   don’t step on my claws.  
7. If there were no guests here,  
   your bones would be cracking.  
8. But since there are guests here,  
   I don’t want to cause any trouble.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. If I had known  
that I was going to die,  
I would have had  
an oak coffin made.  
2. And on that coffin  
a silver lid,  
so that all the maidens  
would be sorry.  
3. And on that lid  
a green wreath,  
so that you would know  
I was a bachelor.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. Don’t look, farmer,
at how the lad dances,
but see if in the stable
he’s cleaning his little horses.
The horses all dirty,
the harness torn,
look, farmer,
what kind of lad he is.

1. Don’t look, farmer,
at how the girl dances,
look into the kitchen,
how she’s cleaning the pots.
All the pots are dirty,
under the bench a stirring spoon,
look, farmer,
what kind of girl she is.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. A crow flew in,  
sat on a thorn,  
a second flew in,  
sat next to it.  
2. A little bird flew there,  
sat on a little branch,  
and a second flew in,  
singing a little song.

label: RŮZNÉ
translation:
1. On Monday, there is soup,  
on Tuesday, peas and lentils,  
on Wednesday, cabbage  
as sour as the devil.  
2. On Thursday, there are peas and barley,  
that is very silly here,  
on Friday, we have dumplings,  
eat up, girls.  
3. On Saturday, there are potatoes  
or bread, sausages.  
On Sunday, there is meat,  
eat up, folks.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. When I went through that black forest,  
three maidens were lamenting the oats there.  
2. I began to ask them,  
if my beloved was there too.  
3. Your beloved has already died,  
her funeral was a week ago yesterday.  
4. Take that path up,  
to the new cemetery.  
5. I walked around the cemetery three times,  
but I could not find her anywhere there.  
6. Only a small window—  
are you sleeping, my dear Ančičko?  
7. Who is walking in the cemetery,  
treading the grass on my grave?  
8. No one else but my beloved,  
my dear Pepíček.  
9. Go to the gravedigger’s porch,  
he will give you two tools.  
10. He will give you a spade and a hoe,  
dig up your dear Ančička.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. They are ringing the bells for the holy mass,  
all the maidens are going to it.  
2. Only one did not go,  
she entered the garden.  
3. She wove three wreaths there,  
and that for her lovers.  
4. A gentleman came to her,  
and that was the devil himself.  
5. Give me, maiden, that wreath,  
for I am a handsome young man.  
6. Give me, maiden, both of them,  
for I am a handsome person.  
7. Give me, maiden, all three,  
for I am as handsome as you.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. The girl was mowing, mowing the grass  
not far from the marshland.  
2. A young hunter came to her,  
the forester's young lad.  
3. What are you doing here, girl, what are you doing here,  
you are to perish here now.  
4. Before I perish here,  
I would rather call out three times.  
5. Call out even four times,  
not even a bird will answer you.  
6. When she called the first time,  
a crow answered her.  
7. When she called the second time,  
a magpie answered her.  
8. When she called the third time,  
a jackdaw answered her.  
9. The older brother heard this,  
he went to tell the younger one.  
10. It’s bad, brother, it’s bad with us,  
our sister is calling in the grove.  
11. When they arrived at the grove,  
they found the young hunter.  
12. What are you doing here, young hunter,  
that you have a little saber stained with blood?  
13. I killed the little dove here  
at the innkeeper’s window.  
14. Who cooed at night  
and gave the young men no sleep.  
15. That wasn’t a little dove,  
that was our sister.  
16. All the maidens weave their wreaths,  
while our sister’s hands are rotting.

label: BALADY
translation:
1. When the Virgin Mary  
walked through the world,  
from town to little town  
she asked for lodging.  

2. She came, she came,  
to a blacksmith:  
“My dear blacksmith,  
give me lodging.”  

3. “I have no lodging,  
I have too many apprentices,  
they forge day and night,  
they do not put out the fires.  

4. They forge, they forge,  
three iron nails,  
which Christ on the cross  
will need.”  

5. When the Virgin Mary  
heard these words,  
all frightened,  
she hurried away from there.  

6. She entered, she entered  
an empty stable,  
and when she crossed the threshold,  
she gave birth to her son.  

7. Then came, then came  
the blacksmith’s child:  
“Lull me to sleep,  
my little baby!”  

8. “How can I lull you,  
when I have no hands?”  
“Touch the manger,  
and you will get hands!”  

9. He touched the manger,  
and immediately he gained hands,  
immediately he gained hands,  
and hurried home.  

10. “Oh, father, daddy,  
now he has been born,  
now he has been born,  
who made my hands.  

11. She gave them to me, she gave them,  
the Virgin Mary,  
who yesterday at your place  
asked for lodging.”  

12. “If I had known  
that it was Mary,  
I would have laid her  
in gold, in silver.  

13. And I myself would lie down  
with my head on the stone,  
with my head on the stone,  
with my feet in the thorns.”

label: MILOSTNÉ
translation:
Formánek is riding  
from Jaroměř,  
over mountains and hills  
turning the shuttle,  
he has blue eyes,  
riding to his lady.

label: MILOSTNÉ
translation:
1. If I were a little bird,  
that little singer,

The first verse is still sung in the Chodsko region.  
J. Krečman’s father was a shoemaker, his son trained as a baker but did not practice the trade. For 40 years he worked as a roofer; besides that, he repaired harmonicas and clocks. During World War I, he was taken prisoner by the Russians, where he did shoemaking work, then he joined the legions. After the war, he traveled for a year through England and France, making a living playing the harmonica in pubs. Even during the occupation, he worked on roofs, but after the fall, he was seriously injured. After recovery, he worked as a security guard on a railway construction site.

label: PIJÁCKÉ
translation:
Hen Dance  
According to the singer's account, this dance was very popular during her youth.  
The dancers, boys and girls alike, sang the song while dancing. Cf. Hol. V-148, V-191 (Caught a little hen in green oatgrass..., here the dance is called "spider"). Pohankovi, EÚ AV ČR Brno, manuscript 726/1, Poličsko 1952 (Caught a hen at night on the street...), ČL VIII-s. 130 (They killed a hen on Trinity Sunday), VIII-s. 131 (In Prague on the street, I caught a hen).  
The singer, wife of a headmaster, comes from the agricultural village of Hříště near Přibyslav. She spoke the local dialect, although her husband often corrected her to speak standard Czech. She recalled that in her native village during her youth, young people used to gather in the evenings on the village green on a bench and sing. She still knew how to perform the hen and kucmoch dances.
